# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Comparison of Major Ionization Techniques

| Parameter | **Electron Impact (EI)** | **Chemical Ionization (CI)** | **Electrospray Ionization (ESI)** | **MALDI** | **APCI** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Hard | Soft | Soft (very soft) | Soft | Soft |
| **Energy Transfer** | ~70 eV electrons | Proton transfer from reagent gas (CH₄, NH₃) | High voltage (2–5 kV) | UV laser pulse + matrix | Corona discharge |
| **Ion Formed** | M⁺• (radical cation) | [M+H]⁺ (protonated) | [M+nH]ⁿ⁺ (multiply charged) | [M+H]⁺ (mainly singly charged) | [M+H]⁺ or [M–H]⁻ |
| **Fragmentation** | Extensive | Minimal | Minimal | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Analyte Polarity** | Non-polar, volatile | Non-polar, volatile | Polar, thermally labile | Polar biomolecules (proteins, polymers) | Less polar, thermally stable |
| **Analyte MW Range** | < 1000 Da | < 1200 Da | Up to ~150,000 Da (proteins) | Up to ~500,000 Da | < 2000 Da |
| **Coupled With** | GC | GC | LC | LC (offline) or direct | LC |
| **KTU Biotec App.** | Small metabolites | Pesticides in food | Peptides, proteins, drugs | Proteomics, biopolymers | Steroids, lipids |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Multiply Charged Ions Matter (The ESI Trick)**
> Because mass analyzers measure **m/z**, not just mass, generating ions with multiple charges allows the analysis of very large biomolecules (e.g., a 50,000 Da protein with 50 protons has m/z = 1001 — easily within quadrupole range). This is why ESI revolutionized biological MS in the late 1980s (Fenn, Nobel Prize 2002).

## 2.2 Detailed Fragmentation Mechanisms (KTU Board-Favorite)

### 2.2.1 The Molecular Ion Peak and Nitrogen Rule

The **molecular ion (M⁺•)** is the unfragmented ionized analyte. Its m/z gives the **molecular mass**.

> [!NOTE] **Nitrogen Rule (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
> If a compound contains an even number of nitrogen atoms (including zero), its molecular ion will have an **even nominal m/z**. If it contains an odd number of nitrogen atoms, the molecular ion will have an **odd nominal m/z**. This rule is extremely useful for distinguishing between compounds of similar mass.

### 2.2.2 The Most Important Fragmentation Reactions

**1. α-Cleavage (Alpha Cleavage)**
- Cleavage of the bond **adjacent (α)** to a heteroatom (O, N, S) or unsaturated group.
- Driven by the stability of the resulting **oxonium, iminium, or acylium ion** + neutral radical.
- Most common fragmentation in EI spectra of alcohols, amines, ethers, ketones.

**2. McLafferty Rearrangement**
- A **six-membered cyclic transition state** transfers a **γ-hydrogen** to an unsaturated bond (C=O, C=N, C=C) **via β-cleavage**, producing an **even-electron fragment ion** and a **neutral alkene**.
- Requires: a **π-system** (e.g., carbonyl), a **γ-hydrogen**, and the ability to form a **6-membered H-transfer transition state**.
- Diagnostic of aldehydes, ketones, esters, amides, and many pharmaceuticals.

**3. Retro Diels-Alder (RDA) Fragmentation**
- Cyclic olefins (especially cyclohexenes) fragment in a concerted pericyclic reaction to give a **diene + dienophile**.
- Critical for steroids, terpenes, flavonoids, and many natural products.

**4. Loss of Small Neutral Molecules**
- Loss of **H₂O (18 Da)**, **NH₃ (17 Da)**, **CO (28 Da)**, **CO₂ (44 Da)**, **HCl (36 Da)**, **HBr (80 Da)**, **CH₃OH (32 Da)**.
- Each loss is a structural clue (e.g., loss of 18 → hydroxyl; loss of 44 → carboxylic acid → decarboxylation).

**5. Peptide Backbone Fragmentation (Biotechnology-specific)**
- **b-ions:** Contain the N-terminus; formed by cleavage of the peptide C–N bond with charge retention on the N-terminal fragment.
- **y-ions:** Contain the C-terminus; formed by cleavage of the peptide bond with charge retention on the C-terminal fragment.
- The mass difference between consecutive b- or y-ions corresponds to the **residue mass of an amino acid**, allowing de novo peptide sequencing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Utility

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Engineering / Biotech Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$m/z = \frac{M}{n}$$ | m/z = (mass of ion) / (number of charges) | Universal MS relationship; basis of all mass analysis |
| 2 | $$M^{+} \;\text{has}\; m/z = M$$ | For singly charged molecular ion | Identification of molecular mass |
| 3 | $$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{B^{2} r^{2}}{2V}$$ | Magnetic sector analyzer (B = magnetic field, r = radius, V = accelerating voltage) | High-resolution accurate mass measurement |
| 4 | $$t = L\sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ | Time-of-flight: time-of-flight to detector of length L | Used in MALDI-TOF for rapid mass analysis |
| 5 | $$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{4V}{q^{2}\omega^{2}r_{0}^{2}}$$ | Quadrupole m/z (simplified stability) | GC-MS, LC-MS, triple quadrupoles |
| 6 | $$E_{\text{ion}} = eV$$ | Kinetic energy acquired by ion of charge e accelerated through V | Energy imparted to analyte ion |
| 7 | **Nitrogen Rule** | Even N → even m/z for M⁺• | Distinguishing N-containing metabolites |
| 8 | **Even-Electron Rule** | Even-electron ions (e.g., [M+H]⁺) cannot fragment to give a radical cation + neutral radical; they must lose a **neutral molecule** | Predicting fragmentations in CI/ESI spectra |
| 9 | **Stevenson's Rule** | The fragment carrying the charge is the one with **lower ionization energy** | Predicting which fragment retains charge in EI |
| 10 | **b-/y-ion masses** | For peptide of n residues: bₖ + yₙ₋ₖ = M + H (protonated) | De novo protein sequencing |

> [!NOTE] **Stevenson's Rule — Intuitive Explanation**
> When a molecule fragments, the positive charge goes to whichever fragment is "more willing" to hold it — i.e., the one with **lower ionization energy** (the more stable cation). This is why branched alkanes show prominent fragment ions at branch points.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals:** LC-MS/MS with **MRM (Multiple Reaction Monitoring)** is the gold standard for **pharmacokinetic studies, drug metabolism, and bioequivalence testing** in clinical trials.
- **Clinical Diagnostics:** **Tandem MS-based newborn screening** detects >40 inherited metabolic disorders (e.g., phenylketonuria, MCAD deficiency) from a single dried blood spot.
- **Proteomics:** LC-ESI-MS/MS (Orbitrap, Q-TOF) is the workhorse of **bottom-up proteomics**, identifying thousands of proteins from cell lysates.
- **Food Safety:** GC-MS detects **pesticide residues, mycotoxins, and food adulterants** at ppb levels.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** LC-MS quantifies **pharmaceuticals in wastewater, PFAS, and microcystins**.
- **Metabolomics:** Untargeted LC-MS identifies **biomarkers for cancer, diabetes, and neurological disorders**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — m/z Relationship in a Magnetic Sector Analyzer

A magnetic sector analyzer separates ions by exploiting the **Lorentz force** experienced by a moving charged particle in a magnetic field.

**Starting Physical Principles:**
1. An ion of mass m and charge ze is accelerated through a potential difference V, gaining kinetic energy:
$$E_{k} = zeV$$
2. This kinetic energy is also equal to:
$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$
3. The ion enters a magnetic field B perpendicular to its velocity. The magnetic force provides centripetal force for circular motion of radius r:
$$Bzv = \frac{mv^{2}}{r}$$

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Equate the two expressions for kinetic energy.
$$\frac{1}{2}mv^{2} = zeV$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the velocity v of the ion after acceleration.
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}}$$

**Step 3:** From the magnetic force equation, rearrange to obtain the mass-to-charge ratio.
$$Bzv = \frac{mv^{2}}{r} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Bz = \frac{mv}{r} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{m}{z} = \frac{Br}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for v from Step 2.
$$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{Br}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2zeV}{m}}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by squaring both sides.
$$\left(\frac{m}{z}\right)^{2} = \frac{B^{2}r^{2}}{\dfrac{2zeV}{m}} = \frac{B^{2}r^{2}\,m}{2zeV}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel one factor of m/z from both sides.
$$\frac{m}{z} = \frac{B^{2}r^{2}}{2V}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\;\frac{m}{z} = \frac{B^{2} r^{2}}{2V}\;}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Implication**
> By scanning **B** (the magnetic field) at fixed r and V, ions of progressively increasing m/z reach the detector, generating the mass spectrum. Alternatively, by fixing B and scanning V (electric sector — used in double-focusing instruments), extremely high mass resolution is achieved.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Analyzer

In a TOF analyzer, ions of different m/z are given the **same kinetic energy** and then allowed to drift through a field-free flight tube of length L. Lighter ions travel faster and arrive at the detector first.

**Step 1:** All ions of charge ze are accelerated through the same potential V, gaining identical kinetic energy.
$$zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for velocity v.
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}}$$

**Step 3:** The time t required to traverse the field-free drift tube of length L is:
$$t = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute v.
$$t = \frac{L}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2zeV}{m}}} = L\sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\;t = L\,\sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}\;}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for m/z:
$$t^{2} = \frac{L^{2} m}{2zeV} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{m}{z} = \frac{2eV\,t^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why TOF is ideal for MALDI**
> MALDI produces ions in **discrete pulses** (each laser shot = one ion packet). TOF is a **pulsed analyzer** that can measure the arrival time of each packet. The combination offers nearly unlimited mass range — essential for analyzing intact proteins (>100 kDa) and large biopolymers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — McLafferty Rearrangement Mass Shift Rule

For a McLafferty rearrangement of a compound of general formula R–CO–CH₂–CH₂–R' (where R contains the γ-hydrogen and the carbonyl is the π-acceptor), the **mass of the enol radical cation fragment** can be calculated.

**Step 1:** Identify the γ-hydrogen and the acceptor (the C=O group).
The γ-hydrogen is the hydrogen on the carbon atom **three bonds away** from the carbonyl carbon.

**Step 2:** During the rearrangement, the γ-H transfers to the carbonyl oxygen, and the bond between the α- and β-carbons breaks.

**Step 3:** Two products form:
- A **charged enol radical cation** containing the carbonyl group (mass = mass of R–CO–CH₂ = R + 43, where 43 is the mass of CH₂=CO•⁺ part).
- A **neutral alkene** (CH₂=CH–R') released as a stable neutral.

**Step 4:** General formula for the enol cation fragment:
$$m/z(\text{fragment}) = M - (\text{mass of neutral alkene CH}_{2}\text{=CHR}')$$

**Step 5:** Example: For methyl butanoate (CH₃–CH₂–CH₂–COOCH₃, M = 102):
- The McLafferty rearrangement transfers the γ-H (on the terminal CH₃ of the propyl chain) to the carbonyl O.
- The neutral alkene lost is **CH₂=CH₂ (28 Da, ethylene)**.
- The fragment ion mass is **102 − 28 = 74 Da** (protonated methyl acrylate).

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\;m/z_{\text{McLafferty ion}} = M - M_{\text{neutral alkene}}\;}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — De Novo Peptide Sequencing Mass Logic

For a peptide of n amino acid residues, the masses of b- and y-ions follow a predictable pattern.

**Step 1:** The protonated peptide mass is:
$$[M+H]^{+} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Residue Mass}_i) + 18.0106$$
The 18.0106 Da term accounts for the two terminal H atoms and one extra H on the protonation site.

**Step 2:** The bₖ ion (containing residues 1 through k from the N-terminus) has mass:
$$b_{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (\text{Residue Mass}_i) + 1.0079$$

**Step 3:** The yₙ₋ₖ ion (containing residues k+1 through n from the C-terminus) has mass:
$$y_{n-k} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} (\text{Residue Mass}_i) + 18.0106 + 1.0079$$

**Step 4:** The **complementarity relationship** is the cornerstone of sequencing:
$$b_{k} + y_{n-k} = [M+H]^{+} + 1 \;(\text{approximately } [M+H]^{+})$$

More precisely:
$$b_{k} + y_{n-k} = M + 2H \;(\text{or } M + 2 \times 1.0079)$$

> [!NOTE] **Why This Matters in Biotechnology**
> Software like **Sequest, Mascot, MaxQuant, and PEAKS** uses these exact mass relationships to identify proteins from MS/MS spectra. The mass difference between consecutive b-ions (or y-ions) is matched against a database of amino acid residue masses to reconstruct the peptide sequence.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A compound analyzed by EI-MS shows M⁺• at m/z = 100 and a prominent McLafferty fragment at m/z = 72. Determine the mass of the neutral alkene lost and identify the likely functional group.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Mass of neutral alkene lost:
$$M_{\text{neutral}} = M - m/z_{\text{fragment}} = 100 - 72 = 28 \;\text{Da}$$

Step 2 — A neutral loss of 28 Da corresponds to **ethylene (CH₂=CH₂, 28.031 Da)**.

Step 3 — Conclusion: The compound contains a **carbonyl group with a γ-hydrogen**, characteristic of **ketones, aldehydes, esters, or amides** with an alkyl chain of at least three carbons.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake in KTU Exams**
> Students often confuse the **molecular ion (M⁺•)** with the **base peak (the most intense peak)**. The base peak is set to **100% relative abundance** for normalization; it is **not** necessarily the molecular ion. Always look for the **highest m/z peak that is also a reasonable fragmentation pattern** to identify the molecular ion.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Ionization and Analyzer for a Biotec Application

| Application | Recommended Ionization | Recommended Analyzer | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protein molecular weight | ESI | TOF or Q-TOF | Multiply charged ions of intact protein |
| Peptide sequencing (proteomics) | ESI (nano-LC) | Orbitrap, Q-TOF, ion trap | MS/MS of peptides |
| MALDI imaging of tissue | MALDI | TOF | Spatial resolution, tolerance to salts |
| Small drug quantification | ESI or APCI | Triple quadrupole (MRM) | Sensitivity and selectivity |
| Volatile metabolites (fatty acids) | EI (GC-MS) | Single quadrupole | Universal, library searchable |
| Native protein complexes | Nano-ESI | TOF or Orbitrap (high m/z mode) | Preserves non-covalent interactions |
| Steroid hormones in serum | APCI | Triple quadrupole | Less polar, APCI more efficient than ESI |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between hard ionization and soft ionization techniques in mass spectrometry. Give one example for each and state which is preferred for analyzing intact proteins.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hard Ionization** techniques transfer high energy (typically 50–100 eV) to the analyte, causing **extensive fragmentation** and yielding structural information but often destroying the molecular ion. **Example: Electron Impact (EI)** at 70 eV, which produces the radical cation M⁺• and a rich fragmentation pattern used for library matching.
>
> **Soft Ionization** techniques transfer minimal energy, preserving the **intact molecular ion** with little or no fragmentation. **Example: Electrospray Ionization (ESI)**, which produces multiply charged [M+nH]ⁿ⁺ ions suitable for biomolecules.
>
> For analyzing **intact proteins** (which are large, polar, and thermally labile), **soft ionization (ESI or MALDI)** is preferred because hard ionization would completely fragment the protein and no molecular ion would be observed.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Scheme):**
- Definition of hard + soft: 1 mark
- Examples: 1 mark
- Justification for proteins: 1 mark

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**Explain the Nitrogen Rule in mass spectrometry. A compound has a molecular ion at m/z = 137. Predict whether it contains an even or odd number of nitrogen atoms and justify.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Nitrogen Rule** states that for a compound composed of C, H, O, N, S, halogens, etc., the **nominal mass of the molecular ion (M⁺•) is even if the compound contains an even number of nitrogen atoms (including zero)**, and **odd if the compound contains an odd number of nitrogen atoms**.
>
> This is because nitrogen has a nominal atomic mass of 14 (an even number), but its valence contributes **3 bonds** (odd). Each nitrogen therefore shifts the parity of the total mass.
>
> **Application:** For a compound with M⁺• = **137 (odd)** m/z, the compound must contain an **odd number of nitrogen atoms (1, 3, 5, …)**. The most common case is **one nitrogen atom**, so the compound is likely a primary or secondary amine, an amide, or a nitrile.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Statement of the rule: 1 mark
- Reasoning: 1 mark
- Application to the given m/z: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of a **GC-MS system**. Discuss the role of EI as an ionization source and explain why it is universally coupled with GC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An unknown ketone has the molecular formula C₆H₁₂O (M = 100 Da). Its EI mass spectrum shows major fragment ions at **m/z = 85, 72, 57, 43**, and **29**. Identify the structure of the compound and explain the formation of each fragment using **α-cleavage** and **McLafferty rearrangement**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram of GC-MS (see Section 4.3) and working:**
- **Carrier gas (He, 1 mL/min)** sweeps the vaporized sample through the GC column.
- **Injector port (200–320 °C)** vaporizes the liquid sample.
- **Capillary column** (5% phenyl-methylpolysiloxane, 30 m) separates analytes based on boiling point and polarity.
- **Transfer line (320 °C)** delivers eluting compounds to the MS.
- **EI source (70 eV electrons)** ionizes analytes into M⁺•.
- **Quadrupole analyzer** separates ions by m/z using oscillating electric fields.
- **Electron multiplier detector** amplifies the ion signal.
- **Data system** plots Total Ion Chromatogram (TIC) and individual mass spectra.

**Why EI for GC:** Both EI and GC require **volatile, thermally stable, gas-phase** analytes. EI's high energy (70 eV) produces **reproducible fragmentation patterns** that can be matched against universal libraries (e.g., **NIST, Wiley**) containing >200,000 spectra — making GC-EI-MS the gold standard for **forensic toxicology, environmental analysis, and metabolomics of volatile compounds**.

**(b) Structure determination for C₆H₁₂O (M = 100):**

Step 1 — Degree of unsaturation:
$$\text{DoU} = \frac{2(6) + 2 - 12}{2} = 1$$
One degree of unsaturation → consistent with one **carbonyl (C=O)** group (i.e., a ketone).

Step 2 — McLafferty rearrangement:
- M = 100; fragment at m/z = 72.
- Neutral loss = 100 − 72 = **28 Da (ethylene, CH₂=CH₂)**.
- This is **diagnostic of a McLafferty rearrangement** of a ketone with a γ-hydrogen and a propyl chain.

Step 3 — α-cleavage fragments:
- m/z = 85 → loss of **CH₃ (15 Da)** from M.
- m/z = 57 → loss of **C₃H₇ (43 Da, propyl)** from M.
- m/z = 43 → **CH₃C≡O⁺ (acetyl cation)** — characteristic of methyl ketones.
- m/z = 29 → **C₂H₅⁺ or CHO⁺** — terminal alkyl fragment.

Step 4 — Proposed structure: **2-Hexanone (CH₃–CO–CH₂–CH₂–CH₂–CH₃)**.
- McLafferty: M − 28 = 72 (enol of acetone, [CH₂=C(OH)–CH₃]⁺•).
- α-cleavage at C1–C2 bond: M − 15 (CH₃•) = 85.
- α-cleavage at C2–C3 bond: M − 43 (C₃H₇•) = 57 (acylium ion CH₃–C≡O⁺).
- m/z = 43 is the **base peak** (most stable acylium ion).
- m/z = 29 is C₂H₅⁺ from further fragmentation.

**Final Answer:** The compound is **2-hexanone (methyl butyl ketone)**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- GC-MS components and EI: 4 marks
- Why EI for GC: 3 marks
- (b) Degree of unsaturation: 1 mark
- McLafferty analysis: 2 marks
- α-cleavage analysis: 2 marks
- Final structure: 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, instrumentation, and working of Electrospray Ionization (ESI)** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the **Coulombic explosion model** and explain why ESI is the method of choice for proteomics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle of Tandem Mass Spectrometry (MS/MS)** with a triple quadrupole (QqQ) instrument. With a clear diagram, explain the four primary scan modes — **Product Ion Scan, Precursor Ion Scan, Neutral Loss Scan, and MRM** — and state one specific biotechnological application for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ESI Principle and Instrumentation:**

**Principle:** ESI produces ions by **nebulizing a liquid sample through a high-voltage (2–5 kV) capillary**, creating a fine aerosol of charged droplets. As the solvent evaporates, droplet size decreases until the **Rayleigh limit** is reached, at which point **Coulombic repulsion** exceeds surface tension, causing the droplet to undergo **Coulombic explosion** and eject smaller charged droplets. Ultimately, fully desolvated **multiply charged gas-phase ions** are produced.

**Instrumentation (see Section 4.4 for diagram):**
- **Syringe pump** (1–1000 μL/min flow).
- **Stainless steel capillary** held at high voltage.
- **Nebulizer gas (N₂)** assists droplet formation.
- **Heated capillary / desolvation region** (100–300 °C) evaporates solvent.
- **Skimmer / ion optics** focuses ions into the mass analyzer.

**Coulombic Explosion Model (detailed):**
1. Liquid elutes from the LC column containing analyte molecules.
2. The high voltage at the capillary tip creates a strong electric field, causing **charge separation** (positive charges accumulate at the tip).
3. The liquid is **nebulized** into a fine spray of **positively charged droplets** (Taylor cone formation).
4. As solvent evaporates, the surface charge density increases.
5. When the **Rayleigh limit** is reached:
$$Q_{R} = 8\pi \sqrt{\epsilon_{0} \gamma R^{3}}$$
(where γ is surface tension, R is droplet radius), the droplet undergoes **Coulombic fission**, ejecting many smaller charged droplets.
6. Repeated evaporation-fission cycles eventually release **fully desolvated, multiply protonated analyte ions** [M+nH]ⁿ⁺.

**Why ESI for Proteomics:**
- Generates **multiply charged ions** of intact proteins, bringing large m/z values (e.g., 50 kDa protein with 50 charges → m/z = 1001) within the range of standard analyzers.
- **Soft ionization** preserves non-covalent interactions, enabling study of protein–ligand and protein–protein complexes.
- **Easy LC coupling** (nano-ESI at 200 nL/min) is ideal for trace-level proteomics.
- Compatible with **MS/MS fragmentation** for peptide sequencing (b-/y-ions).

**(b) Tandem MS (QqQ) and Scan Modes (see Section 4.5 diagram):**

**Principle:** A triple quadrupole consists of **Q1 (MS1) + Q2 (collision cell) + Q3 (MS2)**. A precursor ion is selected in Q1, fragmented in Q2 via **Collision-Induced Dissociation (CID)** with argon gas, and the products are analyzed in Q3. The two stages of mass analysis provide unmatched **selectivity and sensitivity**.

**Four Scan Modes:**

1. **Product Ion Scan (Q1 fixed, Q3 scanning):** Q1 selects a single precursor; Q3 scans all products. Used for **structural elucidation of an unknown metabolite** (e.g., confirming the identity of a drug metabolite by comparing its MS/MS spectrum to literature).

2. **Precursor Ion Scan (Q1 scanning, Q3 fixed):** Q3 is fixed at a diagnostic fragment m/z; Q1 scans all precursors. Used for **screening phosphorylated peptides** (precursors losing 80 Da = HPO₃ or 98 Da = H₃PO₄) in phosphoproteomics.

3. **Neutral Loss Scan (Q1 and Q3 scanning in parallel with constant offset Δm):** Detects all precursors losing a specific neutral fragment. Used for **screening glycoconjugates** (neutral loss of 162 Da = glucose; 146 Da = deoxyhexose; 203 Da = N-acetylhexosamine).

4. **Multiple Reaction Monitoring (MRM) (Q1 and Q3 both fixed):** Monitors a specific precursor → product transition with high duty cycle. Used for **quantitative bioanalysis** of drug levels in pharmacokinetic studies (FDA-mandated for new drug approvals) and **clinical newborn screening** for inborn errors of metabolism.

**Mark Distribution:**
- (a) ESI principle + diagram: 3 marks
- Coulombic explosion model: 2 marks
- Proteomics justification: 2 marks
- (b) MS/MS principle + QqQ: 2 marks
- Four scan modes with applications: 5 marks (1.25 each)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme**
> 1. **Confusing M⁺• (molecular ion) with the base peak:** The base peak is the most intense peak (set to 100%), not necessarily M⁺•. Always identify M⁺• by the highest reasonable m/z in the spectrum.
> 2. **Forgetting that ESI gives [M+H]⁺ (not M⁺•):** When asked for molecular mass from an ESI spectrum, subtract 1 (for the added proton) to get the neutral mass M.
> 3. **Mixing up b-ions and y-ions:** b-ions retain the N-terminus; y-ions retain the C-terminus. The mass difference between b₁ and b₂ = residue mass of the 2nd amino acid from the N-terminus.
> 4. **Calling APCI "Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Ionization" but describing it as MALDI:** APCI uses a **corona discharge**, not a laser.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the role of the collision gas (Ar or N₂) in MS/MS:** Without CID gas, there is no fragmentation, and Q3 receives only the intact precursor.
> 6. **In LC-MS, not distinguishing between ESI and APCI applications:** APCI is preferred for **less polar, smaller molecules (e.g., steroids, lipids)**; ESI is for **polar, larger molecules (peptides, proteins)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Summary**
> - **MS measures m/z**, not mass directly. The ion must be **charged and in the gas phase**.
> - **Five universal stages:** Sample introduction → Ionization → Mass analysis → Detection → Data processing.
> - **Hard ionization (EI, FAB)** fragments extensively → structural info.
> - **Soft ionization (ESI, CI, APCI, MALDI)** preserves the molecular ion → molecular weight.
> - **EI is the GC-MS standard**; 70 eV electrons; library-matchable spectra.
> - **ESI is the LC-MS standard**; produces multiply charged ions; ideal for proteins/peptides.
> - **MALDI uses a UV-absorbing matrix** (e.g., α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid, sinapinic acid); coupled to TOF.
> - **APCI uses corona discharge**; for less polar, thermally stable compounds.
> - **Fragmentation rules:** α-cleavage (heteroatom), McLafferty (γ-H + carbonyl), retro Diels-Alder (cyclic olefins), loss of H₂O/CO/CO₂.
> - **Nitrogen rule:** Even N → even m/z (M⁺•); Odd N → odd m/z.
> - **Stevenson's rule:** Charge stays with the lower-ionization-energy fragment.
> - **Even-electron rule:** Even-electron ions (e.g., [M+H]⁺) lose neutral molecules, not radicals.
> - **b-ions and y-ions** are the language of peptide sequencing by MS/MS.
> - **GC-MS components:** Injector → Column (oven) → Transfer line → EI source → Quadrupole → Detector.
> - **LC-MS components:** HPLC → ESI probe (2–5 kV) → Ion optics → Analyzer → Detector.
> - **Triple quadrupole (QqQ) scan modes:** Product ion, Precursor ion, Neutral loss, MRM.
> - **MRM/SRM** is the gold standard for **quantitative bioanalysis** (pharmacokinetics, newborn screening).
> - **TOF formula:** $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$ — lighter ions arrive first.
> - **Magnetic sector formula:** $m/z = B^{2}r^{2}/(2V)$ — high-resolution accurate mass.
> - **Fragmentation in MS/MS** uses **Collision-Induced Dissociation (CID)** with inert gas (Ar) at 10–50 eV.
> - **Biotechnology applications:** Proteomics (LC-ESI-MS/MS), metabolomics (LC-Q-TOF), newborn screening (LC-MS/MS MRM), intact protein mass (MALDI-TOF), tissue imaging (MALDI-imaging MS), volatile metabolites (GC-MS), environmental contaminants (GC-MS and LC-MS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-Ray Diffraction Crystallography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** X-Ray Diffraction Crystallography ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **X-Ray Diffraction Crystallography (XRD/XRDC)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to determine the atomic and molecular structure of a crystal by measuring the diffraction patterns produced when a beam of monochromatic X-rays interacts with the regularly spaced lattice planes of a crystalline material. In the context of biotechnology, X-ray crystallography is the cornerstone technique for elucidating the three-dimensional structures of biological macromolecules such as proteins, nucleic acids, viruses, and complex biomolecular assemblies at near-atomic resolution (often below 2 Å). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** X-ray crystallography bridges analytical chemistry, physics, and molecular biology. It is the only technique that has directly provided the "structures of life" — DNA double helix (Franklin, Watson, Crick), hemoglobin (Perutz), myoglobin (Kendrew), and insulin (Hodgkin). > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle:** The wavelength of X-rays (**0.1 Å to 100 Å**, typically **1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m**) is comparable to the interatomic spacing in crystals (typically **1–4 Å**), making them ideal for probing crystalline order through wave interference phenomena. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo in a Marble Hall" Imagine standing in a vast, perfectly tiled marble hall and shouting a sound of a specific pitch. If the wavelength of your voice matches the spacing of the tiles on the floor, the sound waves reflecting off each tile will arrive at your ears in phase, producing a louder "echo" from specific directions. If the wavelength does not match the tile spacing, the echoes cancel each other out (destructive interference). **X-ray diffraction works the same way:** - **The marble hall** = The 3D crystal lattice (atoms arranged in repeating planes). - **Your voice** = The incident X-ray beam of fixed wavelength **λ**. - **The echo** = Constructive interference peaks recorded by a detector. - **The direction of the echo** = The angle **θ** from which we can calculate the spacing **d** between atomic planes. By rotating the crystal and recording echoes from every direction, we can computationally reconstruct the exact positions of every "tile" (atom) — essentially "seeing" the molecular architecture. ### 1.3 Generation of X-Rays X-rays are produced in a specialized vacuum tube (the **Coolidge tube**) where high-energy electrons strike a metal target (anode). Two types of spectra are produced: 1. **Continuous Spectrum (Bremsstrahlung / "Braking Radiation")** — produced by the sudden deceleration of electrons in the strong electric field of the target nuclei. The energy lost appears as a continuous range of X-ray wavelengths, with a minimum wavelength determined by the tube voltage. 2. **Characteristic Spectrum** — produced when high-velocity electrons knock out inner-shell (K-shell) electrons of the target atoms. Outer electrons fall to fill the vacancy, emitting X-rays of energy equal to the difference between the two electron shells (e.g., **Kα** and **Kβ** lines). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plank's Constant:** **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** — used to relate photon energy to wavelength via **E = hν = hc/λ**. ### 1.4 Moseley's Law The frequency of the characteristic Kα line is related to the atomic number of the target element: $$\nu = a(Z - b)^2$$ where **a** and **b** are constants for a given spectral series, and **Z** is the atomic number. This law is fundamental for choosing target materials in X-ray tubes. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Crystal Geometry — The Unit Cell A **crystal lattice** is an infinite array of points in 3D space generated by three translation vectors **a**, **b**, **c** (lattice parameters) with angles **α**, **β**, **γ** between them. The smallest repeating unit is called the **unit cell**. **Seven Crystal Systems (Bravais Lattices):** 1. Cubic (a = b = c; α = β = γ = 90°) 2. Tetragonal (a = b ≠ c; α = β = γ = 90°) 3. Orthorhombic (a ≠ b ≠ c; α = β = γ = 90°) 4. Hexagonal (a = b ≠ c; α = β = 90°, γ = 120°) 5. Trigonal (Rhombohedral) 6. Monoclinic (a ≠ b ≠ c; α = γ = 90° ≠ β) 7. Triclinic (a ≠ b ≠ c; α ≠ β ≠ γ ≠ 90°) ### 2.2 Miller Indices Miller indices **(h k l)** are a notation system used to identify specific sets of parallel crystallographic planes. They are derived as the reciprocals of the intercepts of the plane with the crystallographic axes (in units of lattice parameters). > [!NOTE] > **Procedure for Miller Indices:** > 1. Find the intercepts of the plane along the x, y, z axes (in units of a, b, c). > 2. Take the reciprocals of these intercepts. > 3. Clear fractions to obtain the smallest set of integers. > 4. Enclose in parentheses: **(h k l)**. **For biological macromolecules**, Miller indices are used to label the reflections collected on the diffractometer, e.g., a reflection labeled **(1 0 0)** corresponds to first-order diffraction from planes parallel to the bc-face. ### 2.3 Bragg's Law — The Heart of XRD In **1913, W.H. Bragg and W.L. Bragg** (father and son, Nobel Prize 1915) proposed that crystal diffraction could be explained as a reflection of X-rays from successive parallel planes of atoms, with constructive interference occurring only when the path difference between rays is an integral multiple of the wavelength. **Path difference = 2d sin θ** For constructive interference: **2d sin θ = nλ**, where **n** is the order of diffraction (an integer: 1, 2, 3, ...). ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Variables | Unit / Value | |---|---------|-------------------------|--------------| | 1 | **nλ = 2d sin θ** | Bragg's Law; n = order, λ = wavelength, d = interplanar spacing, θ = Bragg angle | d in Å, λ in Å, θ in degrees | | 2 | **d = nλ / (2 sin θ)** | Rearranged Bragg's Law for interplanar spacing | Å (angstrom) | | 3 | **sin θ = nλ / (2d)** | Form useful for determining max order of diffraction | Dimensionless | | 4 | **n_max ≈ 2d / λ** | Maximum observable order (since sin θ ≤ 1) | Integer | | 5 | **E = hν = hc / λ** | Photon energy of X-ray | Joules (J) | | 6 | **λ_min = hc / eV** | Minimum wavelength (Duane-Hunt limit); V = tube voltage | Å | | 7 | **ν = a(Z − b)²** | Moseley's Law; Z = atomic number of target | Frequency (Hz) | | 8 | **1/d² = (h²/a²) + (k²/b²) + (l²/c²)** | Cubic system interplanar spacing relation | Å⁻² | | 9 | **N = ρ N_A V_cell / M** | Number of unit cells per gram; ρ = density, V_cell = unit cell volume, M = molar mass | Dimensionless | | 10 | **F(hkl) = Σ f_j exp[2πi(hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)]** | Structure factor for reflection (hkl); f_j = atomic scattering factor, (x_j, y_j, z_j) = fractional coordinates | Dimensionless | | 11 | **I(hkl) ∝ ∣F(hkl)∣²** | Intensity of a reflection is proportional to squared structure factor magnitude | Counts (detector units) | | 12 | **ρ_crystal = Z M / (N_A V_cell)** | Crystal density from lattice parameters | g/cm³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on table formatting:** All vertical bars in equations above use LaTeX \vert or are mathematical contexts. Units and values are placed in separate columns to preserve markdown table integrity. ### 2.5 Why and How of Bragg's Diffraction **The "Why"** — When X-rays strike a crystal, they are scattered by the electron clouds surrounding each atom. Because the atoms in a crystal are arranged with periodic order, the scattered waves from different atoms have well-defined phase relationships. Constructive interference (bright spots) occurs only at specific angles determined by the geometry of the lattice. **The "How"** — Two parallel lattice planes separated by distance d reflect X-rays. The lower plane reflects after the upper plane; the extra path traveled is **2d sin θ**. Constructive interference requires this path difference to be an integer multiple of **λ**. ### 2.6 X-Ray Diffraction Methods (Experimental Techniques) #### A) Laue Method - **Geometry:** Polychromatic (white) X-ray beam, stationary single crystal. - **Output:** Spots arranged on curves called **Laue cones**, lying on the diffraction pattern. - **Use:** Symmetry determination, orientation of crystals, quick screening. - **Type:** Transmission (Laue) or back-reflection (rare). #### B) Rotating Crystal Method - **Geometry:** Monochromatic X-ray beam, single crystal rotated about a fixed axis (usually along a crystallographic axis). - **Output:** Reflections arranged on **layer lines** parallel to the rotation axis. - **Use:** Determining unit cell parameters, recording 3D data sets. #### C) Powder (Debye-Scherrer) Method - **Geometry:** Monochromatic X-ray beam, polycrystalline sample (thousands of randomly oriented microcrystals). - **Output:** Cones of diffraction; on a flat detector, these appear as **rings** (Debye rings). - **Use:** Phase identification, lattice parameter measurement, qualitative/quantitative analysis of powdered solids (e.g., protein powders, mineral samples). #### D) Protein Crystallography (Modern) - Uses **rotating anode generators** or **synchrotron radiation**. - Crystals of protein mounted in a **glass capillary** or flash-cooled to **100 K** (cryocrystallography). - Detectors: **CCD, image plates, pixel detectors** (e.g., PILATUS, EIGER). - Data analyzed by software such as **MOSFLM, XDS, HKL-2000, PHENIX, CCP4**. ### 2.7 Engineering & Biotechnology Utility - **Drug Discovery:** Structure-Based Drug Design (SBDD) — visualizing drug-binding pockets in proteins (e.g., HIV protease inhibitors). - **Structural Biology:** Determination of 3D structures for proteins, DNA, RNA, and viruses deposited in the **Protein Data Bank (PDB)**. - **Quality Control in Biopharmaceuticals:** Polymorph identification in crystalline drug formulations. - **Materials Science:** Characterization of biomaterials (hydroxyapatite, bone scaffolds, scaffolds for tissue engineering). - **Forensic Science:** Identification of crystalline substances in forensic samples. - **Geobiology & Paleontology:** Mineralogy of fossils, biominerals (shells, bones, teeth). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law We start by considering two parallel planes of atoms in a crystal, separated by a perpendicular distance **d**. A monochromatic X-ray beam of wavelength **λ** is incident on the planes at an angle **θ** (the Bragg angle, measured from the plane itself, NOT from the normal). **Step 1: Draw the geometry.** Two rays, **Ray 1** and **Ray 2**, strike atoms P and Q on the first and second planes respectively. Both are reflected according to the law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). **Step 2: Compute the path difference.** The path of Ray 2 inside the crystal is longer than Ray 1 by the path **PQ + QR**, where the perpendiculars from P drop to the incident and reflected rays through points M and N. From the geometry of the right triangles PMQ and QNR: $$PM = d \sin \theta$$ $$QR = d \sin \theta$$ **Step 3: Sum the extra path.** Total extra path traveled by Ray 2 (compared to Ray 1): $$\Delta = PM + QR = d \sin \theta + d \sin \theta = 2 d \sin \theta$$ **Step 4: Apply the constructive interference condition.** For constructive interference (the two reflected waves to be in phase), the path difference must be an integral multiple of the wavelength: $$\Delta = n \lambda$$ **Step 5: Equate and simplify.** Substituting the expression for Δ: $$2 d \sin \theta = n \lambda$$ This is **Bragg's Law**, where **n = 1, 2, 3, ...** is the order of diffraction. **Step 6: Solve for the interplanar spacing.** Rearranging for **d**: $$d = \frac{n \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}$$ **Step 7: Solve for the Bragg angle.** Solving for **sin θ**: $$\sin \theta = \frac{n \lambda}{2 d}$$ **Step 8: Impose physical constraint.** Since **sin θ ≤ 1**, there is a maximum observable order: $$n_{max} \leq \frac{2d}{\lambda}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** This constraint explains why soft X-rays (longer wavelength) are needed for crystals with larger d-spacings — typical of protein crystals where unit cell dimensions can be 50–500 Å. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation **Problem:** Monochromatic X-rays of wavelength **λ = 1.5418 Å** (Cu Kα radiation) are diffracted by a crystal with interplanar spacing **d = 4.0 Å**. Calculate: (a) The Bragg angle for the first-order reflection. (b) The maximum order of diffraction possible. **Given:** - λ = 1.5418 Å - d = 4.0 Å - n = 1 for part (a); to find n_max for part (b) **Part (a): Bragg's Law for n = 1** $$2 d \sin \theta = n \lambda$$ $$\sin \theta = \frac{n \lambda}{2 d} = \frac{(1)(1.5418)}{2(4.0)} = \frac{1.5418}{8.0} = 0.1927$$ $$\theta = \arcsin(0.1927) = 11.11°$$ The observed diffraction angle **2θ = 22.22°**. **Part (b): Maximum order** $$n_{max} = \frac{2d}{\lambda} = \frac{2(4.0)}{1.5418} = 5.188$$ Since n must be an integer, the **maximum observable order is n = 5**. **Verification for n = 5:** $$\sin \theta = \frac{(5)(1.5418)}{2(4.0)} = \frac{7.709}{8.0} = 0.9636$$ $$\theta = 74.6° \quad \text{(valid, as sin θ < 1)} ✓$$ For n = 6: $$\sin \theta = \frac{(6)(1.5418)}{2(4.0)} = \frac{9.2508}{8.0} = 1.156 \quad \text{(impossible, since } \sin \theta \leq 1\text{)} ✗$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important result for KTU:** A Cu Kα source (λ ≈ 1.54 Å) can typically observe reflections up to about n = 5 for d = 4 Å. For protein crystallography, longer wavelengths (e.g., λ = 0.98 Å at synchrotrons) are often preferred for higher resolution. ### 3.3 Derivation of Duane-Hunt Limit (Minimum Wavelength) When an electron of charge **e** is accelerated through a potential **V**, it acquires kinetic energy **eV**. In the most energetic X-ray producing event, all this kinetic energy is converted to a single photon: $$eV = h\nu_{max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$$ Solving for **λ_min**: $$\lambda_{min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$ Substituting constants: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**: $$\lambda_{min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) V}$$ $$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} V} = \frac{1.241 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ m}$$ Converting to angstroms (1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m): $$\lambda_{min}(\text{Å}) = \frac{12400}{V(\text{volts})} = \frac{12.4}{V(\text{kV})}$$ **Worked Example:** A Coolidge tube operates at **50 kV**. The minimum wavelength is: $$\lambda_{min} = \frac{12.4}{50} = 0.248 \text{ Å}$$ ### 3.4 Interplanar Spacing Formula for Cubic System For a cubic unit cell with lattice parameter **a**, the interplanar spacing for a set of planes (h k l) is: $$\frac{1}{d^2} = \frac{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}{a^2}$$ **Derivation sketch:** The perpendicular distance from the origin to the plane (h k l) — which is d itself — can be found from the plane equation **hx + ky + lz = a** and the formula for the distance from the origin to the plane: $$d = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$ For other systems, the formula generalizes to include the angles and unequal axes: $$\frac{1}{d^2} = \frac{h^2}{a^2 \sin^2 \alpha} + \frac{k^2}{b^2 \sin^2 \beta} + \frac{l^2}{c^2 \sin^2 \gamma} + \frac{2hk \cos \gamma}{ab \sin \alpha \sin \beta} + \cdots$$ (For full triclinic system, all six cross-terms appear; this is beyond the scope of the typical KTU Module 3 question.) ### 3.5 Structure Factor — Why Some Reflections Are Absent (Systematic Absences) The **structure factor** describes how the scattered X-rays from all atoms in the unit cell combine: $$F(hkl) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \exp\left[2\pi i (h x_j + k y_j + l z_j)\right]$$ where: - **f_j** = atomic scattering factor of the j-th atom (depends on atomic number Z and scattering angle) - **(x_j, y_j, z_j)** = fractional coordinates of the j-th atom - **N** = total number of atoms in the unit cell The **intensity** of the diffracted beam is proportional to: $$I(hkl) \propto \vert F(hkl) \vert^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** When **F(hkl) = 0**, the reflection is "absent" (no diffracted intensity). This happens for specific (hkl) combinations depending on the centering of the lattice: > - **Body-centered (I):** h + k + l = odd → absent > - **Face-centered (F):** h, k, l mixed parity → absent > - **Primitive (P):** no systematic absences > > This forms the basis of lattice type determination from observed diffraction patterns. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Bragg X-Ray Spectrometer ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HV Power Supply
30 to 60 kV] --> B[Coolidge X-Ray Tube] B1[Cathode Tungsten Filament
heated by low voltage] --> B B2[Anode Target Cu Mo W etc] --> B B3[Vacuum Enclosure 10 to the minus 6 Torr] --> B B -->|Collimated X-Ray Beam lambda| C[Slit S1 Collimator] C -->|Monochromatic Parallel Beam| D[Rotating Crystal Mount
Sample Stage] D -->|Diffracted Beam at 2 theta| E[Slit S2 Analyzer] E --> F[X-Ray Detector
Geiger Muller or Scintillation Counter] F --> G[Amplifier and Pulse Counter] G --> H[Recorder / Computer Data Acquisition] H --> I[Bragg Angle 2 theta Plot
Intensity versus Angle] subgraph SourceModule A B1 B2 B3 end subgraph OpticsModule C E end subgraph SampleModule D end subgraph DetectionModule F G H end style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Bragg Diffraction Geometry — Path Difference Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incident X-Ray Beam
Wavelength lambda] -->|Angle theta| P(Atom on Plane 1

Hanging Drop or Sitting Drop] C --> D{Protein Crystals

Flash Cool to 100 K] F --> G[Data Collection
