## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**Define a reference electrode. List any four essential characteristics it must possess.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **reference electrode** is a half-cell with a **known, stable, and reproducible potential** that is **independent of the analyte solution** composition. It is used as a constant potential benchmark against which the potential of an indicator electrode is measured in potentiometric analysis. *(1 mark)*

The four essential characteristics are: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
1. **Constant and known potential** — independent of analyte.
2. **Reversible behavior** — must obey the Nernst equation.
3. **High exchange current density** — minimal polarization under small current.
4. **Long-term stability** — potential drift should be negligible over the measurement duration.

*(Any four of: non-polarizable, fast equilibrium, mechanical/chemical robustness, large temperature tolerance, ease of fabrication.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**Write the half-cell reaction and the Nernst equation for a saturated calomel electrode (SCE). State its potential at 25 °C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Half-cell reaction:** *(1 mark)*
$$Hg_2Cl_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^-(aq)$$

**Nernst equation (25 °C):** *(1 mark)*
$$E_{SCE} = E^0_{Hg_2Cl_2/Hg} - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}\left([Cl^-]^2\right) = 0.268 - 0.05916 \log_{10}[Cl^-]$$

**Standard potential (saturated KCl, 25 °C):** *(1 mark)*
$$\boxed{E_{SCE} = +0.244\ \text{V vs. SHE}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Reference Electrode Fundamentals + Construction**

`[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of the Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)**. Write the half-cell reaction and derive the Nernst expression for its potential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**, **SCE**, and **Ag/AgCl electrode** on the basis of: (i) potential at 25 °C, (ii) ruggedness, (iii) temperature stability, and (iv) typical application. Why is the Ag/AgCl electrode preferred in **biological/clinical potentiometry**? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A glass tube with two compartments. The lower compartment holds a small pool of **pure mercury (Hg)**, over which a paste of **mercurous chloride (calomel, Hg₂Cl₂) mixed with mercury** is placed. The upper compartment is filled with **saturated KCl solution**, which serves as the salt bridge. A **platinum wire** sealed into the bottom makes electrical contact with the mercury. A **porous glass frit or asbestos wick** at the bottom allows ionic contact with the external analyte solution.

**Working (2 marks):** The half-reaction at the calomel/Hg interface is:
$$Hg_2Cl_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^-(aq)$$

Since saturated KCl maintains a *fixed* $a_{Cl^-}$, the potential is **pinned at +0.244 V** vs. SHE at 25 °C.

**Nernst derivation (2 marks):** With $n = 2$, $E^0 = +0.268$ V, and $Q = [Cl^-]^2$:
$$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - \frac{0.05916}{2}\log_{10}[Cl^-]^2 = 0.268 - 0.05916\log_{10}[Cl^-]$$
For saturated KCl at 25 °C ($[Cl^-] \approx 4.16$ M, $a_{Cl^-} \approx 2.41$): $E = +0.244$ V vs. SHE.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | SHE | SCE | Ag/AgCl |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) E at 25 °C (sat. KCl)** | 0.000 V | +0.244 V | +0.199 V |
| **(ii) Ruggedness** | Fragile (gas cylinder needed) | Moderate (Hg spill risk) | **High** (solid-state) |
| **(iii) Temperature stability** | Excellent (defined at all T) | Moderate ($-0.65$ mV/°C) | **Good** ($-0.55$ mV/°C) |
| **(iv) Typical application** | Fundamental standard; thermodynamic studies | Routine lab pH, titration | **Biosensors, clinical ISE, implanted probes** |

*(1 mark for tabulated data; 1 mark for application row)*

**Why Ag/AgCl is preferred in clinical/biological potentiometry (2 marks):**
1. **Solid-state construction** → no glass breakage, no mercury toxicity, miniaturizable.
2. **Biocompatibility** → body fluid is essentially NaCl; the Ag/AgCl/KCl junction is chemically compatible.
3. **Stable potential at body temperature (37 °C)** with small, predictable temperature coefficient.
4. **Compatible with sterilization and autoclaving** for reusable medical probes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Quantitative & Numerical**

`[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]`

**(a)** A glass electrode is used as the indicator in a cell with an **SCE reference**. The measured EMF of the cell is **0.482 V** at 25 °C. Calculate the **pH of the unknown solution**. *(Given: $E^0$ of glass electrode = +0.400 V vs. SHE, slope factor $\beta = 0.05916$ V/pH at 25 °C.)* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **liquid-junction potential** and explain, with the Henderson equation conceptually, how it introduces error in potentiometric measurements. State **two methods to minimize** this error. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Write the cell EMF equation (1 mark):**
$$E_{cell} = E_{glass} - E_{SCE} = (E^0_{glass} - 0.05916 \cdot pH) - 0.244$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the measured value (1 mark):**
$$0.482 = (0.400 - 0.05916 \cdot pH) - 0.244$$

**Step 3 — Solve for pH (3 marks):**
$$0.482 + 0.244 = 0.400 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$$
$$0.726 = 0.400 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$$
$$0.05916 \cdot pH = 0.400 - 0.726 = -0.326$$
$$pH = \frac{-0.326}{0.05916} = -5.51\ \text{(pH is negative!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Reality Check:** A negative pH is physically possible (very concentrated strong acid) but for routine lab solutions (pH 0–14) the values above are inconsistent. KTU examiners often set up such questions to test your **algebraic discipline** — show every step, do not silently flip the sign, and conclude explicitly that this corresponds to an unusually acidic solution. Re-check whether the cell convention is $E_{ind} - E_{ref}$ or $E_{ref} - E_{ind}$. In most textbooks, the glass electrode equation is $E_{cell} = E_{glass} - E_{SCE}$ with $E_{glass}$ *decreasing* as pH *increases*, so a *lower* $E_{cell}$ means a *higher* pH. If the answer comes out unreasonable, the convention used in your course may differ — re-state the convention you are using.

**Step 4 — Cross-check by adopting alternative convention** (using $E_{cell} = E_{SCE} - E_{glass}$):
$$0.482 = 0.244 - (0.400 - 0.05916\cdot pH)$$
$$0.482 - 0.244 = -0.400 + 0.05916\cdot pH$$
$$0.238 + 0.400 = 0.05916\cdot pH$$
$$pH = \frac{0.638}{0.05916} = 10.78$$

*Note:* For this numerical exercise, the second convention gives a pH value within the normal analytical range. KTU papers accept either convention provided the student clearly states it. *(2 marks for correct final answer with explicit convention statement.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **liquid-junction potential (LJP)**, $E_j$, is the small potential difference that develops at the interface between two electrolyte solutions of different composition (e.g., saturated KCl in the reference and the analyte solution) when they are brought into contact through a porous frit or salt bridge.

**Origin (1 mark):** It arises because ions of different mobilities ($\mu_+$, $\mu_-$) diffuse across the junction at different rates, producing a transient charge separation. For KCl (where $\mu_{K^+} \approx \mu_{Cl^-}$), LJP is minimized — this is why KCl is the preferred salt-bridge electrolyte.

**Henderson equation (conceptual, 2 marks):**
$$E_j = -\frac{RT}{F} \sum_i \int_a^b \frac{t_i}{z_i} d\ln a_i$$
where $t_i$ is the transport number and $z_i$ the charge of species $i$. The integral runs across the junction. Although KTU rarely asks for the full derivation, students must state that $E_j$ is proportional to the **difference in transport numbers** of the cation and anion.

**Errors introduced (1 mark):** LJP adds an *unknown* offset to the measured EMF, corrupting both the absolute value and the slope of calibration curves — most critically in **low-ionic-strength** and **biological** samples.

**Methods to minimize (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Use a salt bridge of equitransferent electrolyte** (e.g., saturated KCl where $\mu_{K^+} \approx \mu_{Cl^-}$) so that the LJP is $< 1$ mV.
2. **Use a high-ionic-strength background electrolyte** in the analyte (e.g., TISAB buffer for fluoride ISE) to suppress analyte-side diffusion potentials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** 
> 1. **Confusing SCE and SHE potentials** — students frequently write $E_{SCE} = 0.000$ V. SHE = 0.000 V; **SCE = +0.244 V**.
> 2. **Sign convention errors** — if asked *"potential of X vs. SCE"*, the answer must be reported relative to SCE = 0 in that scale, not in the SHE scale.
> 3. **Forgetting the `2e⁻` in the SCE half-reaction** — using $n = 1$ instead of $n = 2$ yields a $0.05916$ factor instead of $0.02958$. This is the most common 3-mark deduction in B-section numerics.
> 4. **Writing $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \log[Cl^-]$ without showing the simplification** — full marks require explicit $\log(x^2) = 2\log x$ step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reference electrode = fixed-potential benchmark** used in potentiometry to complete the cell.
- **Three references to memorize:** SHE (0.000 V, primary), SCE (+0.244 V, sat. KCl), Ag/AgCl (+0.199 V, sat. KCl).
- **SHE construction essentials:** Pt-black, $a_{H^+} = 1$, $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm — assigned 0.000 V by convention at *all* temperatures.
- **SCE half-reaction:** $Hg_2Cl_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^-(aq)$; $n = 2$, $E^0 = +0.268$ V.
- **Ag/AgCl half-reaction:** $AgCl(s) + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag(s) + Cl^-(aq)$; $n = 1$, $E^0 = +0.222$ V.
- **Nernst equation (25 °C, decimal):** $E = E^0 - \dfrac{0.05916}{n}\log_{10}Q$ — use this form in 95% of KTU problems.
- **Properties of an ideal reference:** constant, reversible, obeys Nernst, low polarization, stable over time, rugged.
- **Ag/AgCl is the biological/clinical workhorse** because it is solid-state, biocompatible, miniaturizable, and tolerant of body-temperature operation.
- **Cell EMF sign:** $E_{cell} = E_{indicator} - E_{reference}$ (reduction potentials); voltmeter is high-impedance ($>10^{12}\ \Omega$).
- **LJP** is minimized by **equitransferent** salt-bridge electrolytes (KCl) and by **TISAB-type** ionic-strength adjusters in the analyte.
- **Temperature coefficients:** SCE $\approx -0.65$ mV/°C ; Ag/AgCl $\approx -0.55$ mV/°C ; SHE $\equiv 0$.
- **Salt bridge ≠ electronic conductor** — it completes the *ionic* circuit; electrons flow *only* through the external wire.
- **A reference electrode should never be the site of the analytical reaction** — its job is *only* to provide a stable potential reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Selective and Molecular Selective Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ion Selective and Molecular Selective Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Ion Selective and Molecular Selective Electrodes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ion Selective Electrode (ISE) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ion Selective Electrode (ISE):** An *electrochemical transducer* (specifically, a potentiometric sensor) that develops a **membrane potential** whose magnitude is a **Nernstian function of the activity (or, with care, concentration) of a specific target ion** in an external test solution, in the presence of other, potentially interfering ions.

In KTU terminology, the ISE is treated as the practical work-horse of **direct potentiometry** in Module 4, and the standard expression used in textbooks and lab manuals is the **Nicolsky–Eisenman equation**:

$$
E_{ISE} = E^{0'} + S \log \left( a_{i} + \sum_{j \neq i} K_{ij} \, a_{j}^{z_{i}/z_{j}} \right)
$$

where the slope factor

$$
S = \frac{2.303 \, R T}{z_{i} F}
$$

reduces, at **25 °C (298.15 K)**, to the memorable value **0.05916 V / z_i** (i.e., **59.16 mV per decade** of activity for a monovalent ion).

### 1.2 Molecular Selective Electrode — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molecular Selective Electrode (MSE):** A *potentiometric or amperometric* sensing device in which a **biochemical recognition element** (typically an enzyme, antibody, or whole cell) is coupled to a transducer so that the **concentration of a specific neutral molecule** (glucose, urea, cholesterol, penicillin, lactate, creatinine) is transduced into a measurable electrical signal.

In the 2024 syllabus, the MSE is positioned as the *biologically extended* counterpart of the ISE — where the ISE recognises **charged species** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, F⁻, NO₃⁻), the MSE recognises **neutral molecules** via a biorecognition layer.

### 1.3 Intuitive / Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — ISE = "Smart Lock."** Think of the ion-selective membrane as a door that opens *only* for one particular "key" ion. When the right key (say, Ca²⁺) inserts, the door opens a tiny electrical "doorbell" (membrane potential) rings — the louder the ring, the more keys were inserted. A Na⁺ ion is the wrong key, but it can sometimes jiggle the lock weakly; the **selectivity coefficient $K_{ij}$** measures how easily a stranger key (j) can fake open the lock meant for the real key (i).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — MSE = "Translator at a UN Booth."** The biorecognition layer (e.g., **glucose oxidase**) is like a translator who *only* understands the language of one delegate ("glucose"). The delegate whispers, the translator turns the whisper into a voltage (H₂O₂ oxidation current or pH change near a glass membrane). The original message (concentration) is recovered from the output voltage/current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Gas-Sensing Electrode = "Balloon Probe."** A dissolved gas (CO₂, NH₃) diffuses through a thin gas-permeable membrane (think of a tiny balloon) into an internal buffer. The internal buffer's pH shifts, and a **glass pH electrode** inside reads the change. So the gas is "interrogated" through pH — a clever, indirect way to measure a neutral, dissolved gas.

### 1.4 Standard Constants & Metrics (boxed for rapid recall)

> **Universal Constants Used Throughout This Module**
> - **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (molar gas constant)
> - **F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (Faraday constant)
> - **Nernstian slope at 25 °C: 59.16 mV / z_i per decade** (for monovalent ions)
> - **Activity (a)** ≈ **concentration (C) × activity coefficient (γ)**; in dilute solutions γ → 1, so a → C.
> - **Detection limit** of a practical ISE: typically **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹⁰ M**.
> - **Selectivity coefficient (K_ij)** — dimensionless; smaller = more selective.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Potentiometric Cell

A complete ISE measurement is performed using a **galvanic cell** with **two reference electrodes** and the ISE in the middle:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Ref}_{\text{ext}} \, \Vert \, \text{Sample} \, \vert \, \text{Membrane} \, \vert \, \text{Internal Fill} \, \vert \, \text{Ref}_{\text{int}}}_{\text{ISE}}
$$

The measurable quantity is the **cell EMF (E_cell)**, which contains:
1. The **junction potential** at the external salt bridge
2. The **membrane potential (E_M)** — the analytical signal
3. The **internal reference offset (E_ref,int)** — a constant

The constant terms collapse into a single lumped constant $E^{0'}$, giving the working equation:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{0'} + S \log a_{i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two references?** Any single-electrode potential is *not measurable in absolute terms* — only **differences** between two half-cells are measurable. The salt bridge (often KCl saturated) keeps the **liquid-junction potential** as small and reproducible as possible.

### 2.2 Classification of ISEs (KTU-Required Taxonomy)

| # | Class | Recognition Element | Classic Examples | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crystalline (solid-state) membrane** | Single crystal or pressed pellet of an ionic salt | LaF₃ crystal (doped with Eu²⁺); Ag₂S / AgCl pellet | F⁻, Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻, S²⁻, CN⁻, Ag⁺ |
| 2 | **Glass membrane** | Hydrated silicate glass (SiO₂ + metal oxides) | pH glass (Lyon/Corning 0150); Na⁺-responsive (NAS 11-18) | H⁺ (pH), Na⁺, K⁺, Li⁺, Ag⁺ |
| 3 | **Liquid (ion-exchanger) membrane** | Hydrophobic ionophore dissolved in a plasticised polymer | Ca²⁺ ISE (ETH 1001); K⁺ ISE (valinomycin); NO₃⁻ (tridodecylammonium) | Ca²⁺, K⁺, NO₃⁻, ClO₄⁻, BF₄⁻ |
| 4 | **Gas-sensing (Severinghaus-type) electrode** | Gas-permeable hydrophobic membrane + internal buffer | NH₃ electrode; CO₂ electrode; SO₂ electrode | NH₃, CO₂, SO₂, NO_x |
| 5 | **Enzyme (bio-catalytic) electrode** | Immobilised enzyme + electrochemical probe | Glucose oxidase (GOx) on Pt; urease on NH₃ ISE | Glucose, urea, cholesterol, lactose, lactate |

> Items 1–4 are conventionally called **ISEs**; item 5 belongs to the **Molecular Selective Electrode / Biosensor** family in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of an ISE

1. **Conditioning of the membrane** — soak in a solution containing the target ion (establishes hydrated gel layer / surface equilibria).
2. **Sample contact** — sample solution bathes the outer membrane face.
3. **Selective binding / ion exchange** at the *outer gel layer* — only the primary ion $i$ (and a kinetically small amount of interferent $j$) binds.
4. **Charge separation** — the difference in diffusion rates / binding constants across the membrane produces a **phase-boundary potential**.
5. **Internal reference equilibration** — the inner face of the membrane contacts an internal fill of fixed activity, holding the inner potential constant.
6. **Signal transduction** — the cell EMF is read by a high-impedance voltmeter (input impedance > 10¹² Ω) to draw *negligible current* and not perturb equilibrium.
7. **Calibration** — EMF plotted vs $\log a_i$; slope $S$ and intercept $E^{0'}$ extracted.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formula symbols obey strict markdown rules — vertical bars use `\vert` not `|`.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{0'} + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{z_{i}F}\,\log a_{i}$ | **Pure-target Nernst equation** | V |
| 2 | $E = E^{0'} + S\,\log a_{i}, \quad S = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{z_{i}F}$ | Slope factor definition | V / decade |
| 3 | $S_{25^{\circ}\text{C}} = \dfrac{0.05916}{z_{i}}\ \text{V/decade}$ | Slope at 25 °C | V |
| 4 | $E = E^{0'} + S\,\log\!\left(a_{i} + \sum_{j\neq i}K_{ij}\,a_{j}^{z_{i}/z_{j}}\right)$ | **Nicolsky–Eisenman** (with interferents) | V |
| 5 | $\log\gamma_{\pm} = -A\,z_{+}\vert z_{-}\vert\,\sqrt{I}$ | **Debye–Hückel** (activity coefficient) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $a_{i} = \gamma_{i}\,C_{i}$ | Activity ↔ concentration | — |
| 7 | $\Delta E = S\,\Delta \log a_{i}$ | Incremental sensitivity (linear in $\log a$) | V |
| 8 | $\text{Error from }T = \Delta T:\ \Delta E \approx 0.1984\,\text{mV/K}\cdot\Delta T$ | Temperature drift coefficient | mV/K |
| 9 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{ISE}} - E_{\text{ref}} + E_{j}$ | Full cell EMF | V |
| 10 | $\text{Selectivity ratio: }\ \text{log}\,K_{ij} < -2$ | "Acceptable" interference criterion | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Operational Caveats (Mark-Loss Traps in the Exam)

- **Temperature appears in the slope $S$** — never write a value of $S$ without stating the temperature.
- **Activity, not concentration**, is the true dependent variable for the Nernst equation. In dilute solutions, $a \approx C$, but in serum/urine/industrial brines this assumption is **fatal**.
- **Selectivity coefficient is concentration-, not activity-, dependent** when reported by some manufacturers — always state the medium and the **Fixed Interference Method (FIM)** vs **Separate Solution Method (SSM)**.
- **High-impedance voltmeter** is mandatory because an ISE's membrane resistance is **10 MΩ to 1 GΩ**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biotechnology

- Clinical chemistry analysers (ISE module of blood-gas/electrolyte analysers measure **Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, pH, pCO₂, pO₂** in <60 s on <200 μL of whole blood).
- Fermentation broth monitoring (online K⁺, NH₄⁺ probes for yeast/glutamate production).
- Environmental monitoring of drinking water (F⁻ ISE for dental fluorosis prevention; NO₃⁻ ISE for agricultural runoff).
- Food industry (Na⁺ in diet products; Ca²⁺ in dairy; NO₃⁻ in cured meats).
- Biosensors for diabetes self-monitoring (glucose oxidase electrodes are the largest-selling biosensor in human history).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for an ISE

We begin with a generic **half-cell** in which a metal M is in equilibrium with its ion M^(z+):

$$
\text{M}^{z+}(\text{aq},\,a_{i}) + z\,e^{-} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{M(s)}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the Gibbs free energy change of the half-reaction.**

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{i}^{\,?}
$$

For an electrode half-reaction, the chemical free energy change is set against the electrical work $zFE$:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{i} - zFE
$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition.** At the electrode surface, $\Delta G = 0$ (no net reaction — current is *zero* in a potentiometric measurement):

$$
0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{i} - zFE
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that the standard free energy is related to the standard potential** by $\Delta G^{\circ} = -zFE^{\circ}$:

$$
0 = -zFE^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{i} - zFE
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for E.**

$$
zFE = -zFE^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{i}
$$

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln a_{i}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to base-10 log** using $\ln x = 2.303\log_{10}x$:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log a_{i}
$$

By convention, define the *anodic positive* form of the ISE cell potential (KTU standard):

$$
\boxed{\,E_{ISE} = E^{0'} + S\,\log a_{i}\,,\qquad S = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{z_{i}F}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $S$ at 25 °C (T = 298.15 K).**

$$
S_{25^{\circ}\text{C}} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{1 \times 96485}\ \text{V}
$$

$$
S_{25^{\circ}\text{C}} = \dfrac{5705.78}{96485}\ \text{V} = 0.05916\ \text{V/decade}
$$

For a **divalent** ion ($z = 2$):

$$
S_{25^{\circ}\text{C,\,}z=2} = \dfrac{0.05916}{2} = 0.02958\ \text{V/decade} = 29.58\ \text{mV/decade}
$$

For a **trivalent** ion ($z = 3$):

$$
S_{25^{\circ}\text{C,\,}z=3} = 19.72\ \text{mV/decade}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Nicolsky–Eisenman Equation (with Interferent)

**Setup.** Suppose an ISE for ion $i$ (charge $z_i$) is exposed to solution containing both $i$ and an interfering ion $j$ (charge $z_j$). The **membrane potential** has two contributions — one from each ion's exchange equilibrium at the outer gel layer.

**Step 1 — Single-ion membrane potential.**

For ion $i$ alone, the potential across the membrane is:

$$
E_{M} = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{z_{i}F}\ln a_{i}(\text{external})
$$

**Step 2 — Add a second ion-exchanging species.** When an interferent $j$ is also present, the charge-flux-balance condition (each ion crossing independently) yields the *summed* response, weighted by selectivity:

$$
E_{M} = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{z_{i}F}\ln\!\left[ a_{i} + K_{ij}\,a_{j}^{\,z_{i}/z_{j}} \right]
$$

This is the **Nicolsky–Eisenman equation** in its most commonly taught form.

**Step 3 — Convert to base-10 log.**

$$
E_{M} = E^{0} + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{z_{i}F}\,\log\!\left[ a_{i} + K_{ij}\,a_{j}^{\,z_{i}/z_{j}} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the selectivity coefficient's physical meaning.** $K_{ij}$ can be re-expressed as:

$$
K_{ij} = \dfrac{U_{j}}{U_{i}}\,\dfrac{k_{j}}{k_{i}}\,\dfrac{[B]^{\beta_{j}-\beta_{i}}}{[A]^{\alpha_{j}-\alpha_{i}}}
$$

where $U$ = mobility, $k$ = partition coefficient, and the bracketed terms are the membrane-site concentrations. **For KTU exams, only the operational form matters.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical rule of thumb:** A "well-designed" ISE satisfies $\log K_{ij} \le -2$ (i.e., the electrode is at least **100 times** more responsive to the primary ion than to the interferent).

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Numerical Sensitivity

> **Problem.** A fluoride ISE is calibrated at 25 °C using standard solutions. The observed potentials are:
>
> | $C_{\text{F}^{-}}$ (M) | $E$ (mV) |
> |---|---|
> | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ | +120.0 |
> | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | +60.5 |
> | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | +1.5 |
>
> Determine the **slope**, **Nernstian check**, and the **concentration of F⁻ in an unknown** whose measured $E$ = +90.0 mV.

**Step 1 — Check Nernstian slope (monovalent, F⁻: $z = -1$, but magnitude of slope depends on |z|).**

$$
\Delta E = E_{10^{-2}} - E_{10^{-3}} = 120.0 - 60.5 = 59.5\ \text{mV/decade}
$$

$$
\Delta E = E_{10^{-3}} - E_{10^{-4}} = 60.5 - 1.5 = 59.0\ \text{mV/decade}
$$

**Average slope $\bar{S} = 59.25$ mV/decade.** The theoretical value at 25 °C is **59.16 mV/decade**; the electrode is **Nernstian within experimental error (≈ 0.15 %)**. ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $E^{0'}$.** Using the $10^{-2}$ M point:

$$
E^{0'} = E - S\log a_{i} = 120.0 - 59.16 \times (-2.00) = 120.0 + 118.32 = 238.32\ \text{mV}
$$

(Equivalently, $E^{0'}$ is the y-intercept when $\log a_i = 0$, i.e., $a_i = 1$.)

**Step 3 — Solve for unknown concentration.**

$$
E = E^{0'} + S\log C_{\text{F}^{-}}
$$

$$
90.0 = 238.32 + 59.16 \times \log C_{\text{F}^{-}}
$$

$$
\log C_{\text{F}^{-}} = \dfrac{90.0 - 238.32}{59.16} = \dfrac{-148.32}{59.16} = -2.507
$$

$$
C_{\text{F}^{-}} = 10^{-2.507} = 3.11 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{M} \;\approx\; \mathbf{3.1\ mM}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (use the lower point, 10⁻⁴ M):**

$$
E_{\text{calc}} = 238.32 + 59.16 \times (-4) = 238.32 - 236.64 = 1.68\ \text{mV}
$$

Observed: +1.5 mV. Residual: −0.18 mV, within experimental error. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Interference (Quantitative)

> **Problem.** A Ca²⁺ ISE ($z_i = +2$) has selectivity coefficient $K_{\text{Ca,Na}} = 10^{-3}$ for Na⁺ ($z_j = +1$). The sample contains $a_{\text{Ca}^{2+}} = 10^{-3}$ and $a_{\text{Na}^{+}} = 0.10$. What is the *apparent* calcium activity that the ISE would report?

**Step 1 — Apply Nicolsky–Eisenman.**

$$
E = E^{0'} + \dfrac{0.02958}{1}\log\!\left[10^{-3} + (10^{-3})(0.10)^{2/1}\right]
$$

$$
E = E^{0'} + 0.02958\,\log\!\left[10^{-3} + 10^{-3} \times 0.01\right]
$$

$$
E = E^{0'} + 0.02958\,\log\!\left[10^{-3} + 10^{-5}\right]
$$

$$
E = E^{0'} + 0.02958\,\log\!\left[1.01 \times 10^{-3}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the "true" reading (no interference):**

$$
E_{\text{true}} = E^{0'} + 0.02958\,\log(10^{-3}) = E^{0'} - 0.08874\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate error:**

$$
\Delta E = 0.02958\,\log(1.01) \approx 0.02958 \times 0.00432 = 0.000128\ \text{V} = 0.128\ \text{mV}
$$

**Inferred error in $a_{\text{Ca}^{2+}}$:**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta a}{a} \approx 10^{0.128/29.58} - 1 = 10^{0.00433} - 1 \approx 1.00\%
$$

**Conclusion:** At this concentration ratio, the Na⁺ interference is **small (~1 %)** because the selectivity is good and Na⁺ concentration is not overwhelmingly large.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse scenario — what if $a_{\text{Na}^{+}}$ were 0.50 M (e.g., serum)?** The interference term becomes $10^{-3} \times 0.50^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ — already **25 %** of the calcium signal. In clinical chemistry, this is why serum Ca²⁺ measurements use a **preliminary dilution** with an ionic-strength adjuster (ISA).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Complete ISE Measurement Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High-Impedance Voltmeter] --> B[Read E_cell in mV]
    B --> C{Apply Nernst or Nicolsky-Eisenman}
    C --> D[Convert log a_i to a_i]
    D --> E[Apply Activity Coefficient Correction]
    E --> F[Final Concentration Report]
    G[External Reference Electrode] --> H[Reference Salt Bridge]
    H --> I[Sample Solution]
    I --> J[Outer Ion-Selective Membrane]
    J --> K[Inner Fill Solution of Fixed Activity]
    K --> L[Internal Reference Electrode]
    G --> A
    L --> A
    classDef sensor fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0B5FFF,color:#0B3D99;
    classDef meter fill:#FFF6E0,stroke:#B7791F,color:#7C4A03;
    classDef output fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#1F8B4C,color:#14532D;
    class G,H,I,J,K,L sensor
    class A,B,C meter
    class D,E,F output
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Classification Tree of Potentiometric Chemical Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Potentiometric Chemical Sensors]
    Q --> R[Ion Selective Electrodes]
    Q --> S[Molecular Selective Electrodes and Biosensors]
    R --> R1[Crystalline Membrane]
    R --> R2[Glass Membrane]
    R --> R3[Liquid Membrane with Ionophore]
    R --> R4[Gas Sensing Severinghaus Type]
    R1 --> R1a[LaF3 for Fluoride]
    R1 --> R1b[Ag2S AgCl for Chloride]
    R2 --> R2a[pH Glass Electrode]
    R2 --> R2b[NAS Glass for Sodium]
    R3 --> R3a[Valinomycin for Potassium]
    R3 --> R3b[ETH 1001 for Calcium]
    R4 --> R4a[NH3 Gas Sensor]
    R4 --> R4b[CO2 Gas Sensor]
    S --> S1[Enzyme Electrode]
    S --> S2[Microbial Whole Cell Electrode]
    S --> S3[Immuno-electrode]
    S1 --> S1a[Glucose Oxidase on Pt Clark Type]
    S1 --> S1b[Urease on NH3 Gas ISE]
    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#5C2E0A;
    classDef ise fill:#D6E9FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,color:#0B2545;
    classDef mse fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#1F6B1F,color:#0D3F0D;
    classDef leaf fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#888888,color:#222222;
    class Q root
    class R,R1,R2,R3,R4,R1a,R1b,R2a,R2b,R3a,R3b,R4a,R4b ise
    class S,S1,S2,S3,S1a,S1b mse
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L leaf
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Operating Sequence of an Enzyme-Based Molecular Selective Electrode (Glucose Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1 Glucose diffuses from sample] --> step2[Step 2 Glucose contacts immobilised GOx layer]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Enzymatic reaction produces H2O2 and gluconic acid]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 H2O2 diffuses to Pt working electrode]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 H2O2 oxidised at fixed +0.7 V vs Ag/AgCl reference]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Anodic current proportional to glucose concentration]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Current converted to concentration via calibration]
    classDef chem fill:#FCEEEE,stroke:#9B1B1B,color:#5C0A0A;
    classDef elec fill:#EEF6FF,stroke:#0B3D99,color:#062954;
    classDef out fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#1F8B4C,color:#0D3F0D;
    class step1,step2,step3 chem
    class step4,step5,step6 elec
    class step7 out
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Equivalent Circuit Representation of an ISE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[External Ref Half Cell] --> A2[Junction Potential Ej]
    A2 --> A3[Membrane Capacitance Cm in parallel with Rm 10 to 1000 MOhm]
    A3 --> A4[Membrane Phase Boundary Potential E_M]
    A4 --> A5[Internal Reference Half Cell]
    A5 --> A6[Cell EMF measured by voltmeter with input impedance > 1e12 ohm]
    A1 --> A6
    classDef passive fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B5A2B,color:#4A2E0B;
    classDef active fill:#D8E8FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,color:#0B2545;
    classDef meter fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#A11B1B,color:#5C0A0A;
    class A2,A3,A4 passive
    class A1,A5 active
    class A6 meter
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
> **Q1. (3 marks)** Define **selectivity coefficient** ($K_{ij}$) of an ISE and explain its significance with the help of the **Nicolsky–Eisenman equation**.

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The selectivity coefficient $K_{ij}$ is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the *response of an ISE designed for primary ion $i$ to an interfering ion $j$*.
- **Equation (1 mark):** $E = E^{0'} + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{z_{i}F}\,\log\!\left(a_{i} + K_{ij}\,a_{j}^{z_{i}/z_{j}}\right)$.
- **Significance (1 mark):** A *smaller* $K_{ij}$ means *better* selectivity. Conventionally, $\log K_{ij} \le -2$ is required for acceptable ISE performance; the coefficient is essential for predicting the *interference error* in mixed-ion solutions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. (3 marks)** Differentiate between an **Ion Selective Electrode (ISE)** and a **Molecular Selective Electrode (MSE)** with two suitable examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabulated):**

| Feature | ISE | MSE |
|---|---|---|
| Target species | Charged ions (H⁺, Na⁺, F⁻, Ca²⁺) | Neutral molecules (glucose, urea, cholesterol) |
| Recognition element | Ion-selective membrane (glass, crystal, ionophore) | Biorecognition layer (enzyme, antibody, whole cell) |
| Examples (KTU-mandated) | Fluoride ISE (LaF₃ crystal); pH glass electrode | Glucose oxidase electrode; urease-based NH₃ electrode |
| Transduction | Direct potentiometric | Potentiometric (indirect) or amperometric |

(Any 2 of these rows × 1.5 marks is a fair model answer. **Total 3 marks**.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (EITHER / OR format)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** — Answer **either** Q3A *or* Q3B.

---

#### **Question 3A (14 marks)**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a glass-membrane pH electrode**. Derive the Nernst equation relating cell EMF to hydrogen-ion activity.

**Model Answer (7 marks — distribution):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** A typical glass electrode consists of:
   - A thin **sodium silicate / lithium silicate glass membrane** (≈ 0.1 mm thick) shaped as a bulb at the tip.
   - An **internal fill** of **0.1 M HCl** (saturated with AgCl).
   - An **internal reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl wire dipping into the internal fill.
   - A robust outer body (epoxy / glass shaft) with shielded cable to a high-impedance voltmeter.
   - The glass must be **hydrated** — soaking in water creates a gel layer (≈ 10 nm thick) on both faces; without this layer, the electrode is unresponsive.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - **Outer gel layer** exchanges H⁺ with sample; **inner gel layer** exchanges H⁺ with internal HCl of fixed activity.
   - The two phase-boundary potentials at the inner and outer gel layers differ → the difference is the **membrane potential** $E_M$.
   - This $E_M$ is a **Nernstian function** of the *outer* H⁺ activity:
     $$ E_M = E^{0} + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F}\log a_{H^{+}}(\text{sample}) $$
   - At 25 °C: $E_M = E^{0} - 0.05916\,\text{pH}$.

3. **Nernst derivation (2 marks):** Already provided in Section 3.1 of these notes; the examiner expects the four-step algebraic path ending in $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log a_{H^{+}}$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A **fluoride ISE** is used to determine F⁻ in a water sample. Calibration data at 25 °C are:

| $C_{\text{F}^{-}}$ (M) | $E$ (mV) |
|---|---|
| $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | 178 |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 119 |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | 60 |
| $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1 |

Calculate (i) the **slope** and **Nernstian check**, (ii) the **concentration of F⁻** in the unknown water sample that gave $E = 90$ mV, and (iii) comment on whether the electrode is **Nernstian**.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Slope and Nernstian check (3 marks):**

Differences:
- $E(10^{-2}) - E(10^{-3}) = 1 - 60 = -59$ mV → 59 mV per decade
- $E(10^{-3}) - E(10^{-4}) = 60 - 119 = -59$ mV → 59 mV per decade
- $E(10^{-4}) - E(10^{-5}) = 119 - 178 = -59$ mV → 59 mV per decade

**Average slope = 59.0 mV/decade.**
**Theoretical slope at 25 °C (monovalent) = 59.16 mV/decade.**
**% deviation = |59.0 − 59.16| / 59.16 × 100 % = 0.27 %.** Within experimental error → **Nernstian**. ✓

**(ii) Concentration of unknown (3 marks):**

Using linear regression: $E = E^{0'} - S \log C$ (slope is negative because of the convention that anion E decreases with increasing activity; we use magnitude here).

Better, use the **Nernst form** with sign convention:
$$ E = E^{0} - 0.05916\,\log C_{\text{F}^{-}} $$

Pick point $C = 10^{-3}$ M, $E = 60$ mV:
$$ 60 = E^{0} - 0.05916 \times (-3) = E^{0} + 0.17748 \ \text{V} $$
$$ E^{0} = 60 - 177.48 = -117.48\ \text{mV} $$

Solve for unknown:
$$ 90 = -117.48 - 0.05916\,\log C_{\text{F}^{-}} \ \Rightarrow\ \log C_{\text{F}^{-}} = \dfrac{-(90 + 117.48)}{0.05916} = \dfrac{-207.48}{59.16} = -3.507 $$

$$ C_{\text{F}^{-}} = 10^{-3.507} = \mathbf{3.11 \times 10^{-4}\ M} \approx \mathbf{0.31\ mM} $$

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):** The slope (59.0 mV/decade) is within **0.3 %** of the Nernstian value; the electrode is therefore **truly Nernstian** and suitable for quantitative work.

---

#### **Question 3B (14 marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **classification, construction, and working of Enzyme electrodes** as Molecular Selective Electrodes. Describe in detail the **Glucose oxidase (GOx) electrode** with the relevant biochemical reaction and the **Clark oxygen electrode** principle.

**Model Answer (7 marks — distribution):**

1. **Classification of Enzyme Electrodes (1 mark):**
   - *Potentiometric* (e.g., urease immobilised on NH₃ gas ISE) — measures product-induced potential change.
   - *Amperometric* (e.g., GOx on Pt) — measures current from a redox product at fixed potential.

2. **General Construction (2 marks):**
   - An **enzyme layer** (immobilised by physical entrapment in a gel, covalent cross-linking with glutaraldehyde, or electrochemical polymerisation) is coated on the surface of an electrochemical transducer.
   - A **diffusion-limiting membrane** (e.g., cellulose acetate, Nafion) on the outer face controls substrate flux and excludes interferents.
   - The transducer is typically a **Pt disc working electrode** with an **Ag/AgCl reference** and a **Pt counter** in a 3-electrode cell.

3. **Working — GOx Electrode (3 marks):**
   - **Enzymatic reaction:**
     $$ \text{Glucose} + \text{O}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{Gluconic acid} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{2} $$
   - **Amperometric detection modes:**
     - **Mode A — Oxygen consumption (classical Clark):** measure the *decrease* in O₂ reduction current at the Pt cathode (held at −0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl).
     - **Mode B — H₂O₂ oxidation (modern):** hold Pt at **+0.7 V** vs Ag/AgCl; current is proportional to H₂O₂ (and thus to glucose).
   - **Clark oxygen electrode** (1962, awarded Nobel 1992): a Pt cathode and Ag/AgCl anode behind an **O₂-permeable Teflon membrane**. At −0.6 V, O₂ is reduced:
     $$ \text{O}_{2} + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$
     The current is diffusion-limited and proportional to dissolved O₂ (and indirectly, in a GOx sensor, to glucose).

4. **Why GOx is a good model (1 mark):** High specificity for β-D-glucose, stable, pH optimum ≈ 7, no cofactor regeneration required (uses dissolved O₂), cheap.

**(b) (7 marks)** A **gas-sensing NH₃ electrode** is used to determine urea via coupling to a urease reaction. The internal fill of the electrode is 0.01 M NH₄Cl. The sample (10 mL) is mixed with 0.5 mL of urease solution (10 U/mL). Urea is hydrolysed completely to NH₄⁺ and the NH₃ produced diffuses through the gas-permeable membrane. (i) Write the relevant reactions. (ii) If the electrode is calibrated with a 1 mM NH₄Cl standard (giving E = −30 mV) and the sample gives E = +10 mV, calculate the urea concentration in the sample, given the Nernst slope S = 59 mV/decade at 25 °C.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Reactions (2 marks):**
- **Enzymatic hydrolysis (urease):**
  $$ \text{CO(NH}_{2})_{2} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{urease}} 2\,\text{NH}_{3} + \text{CO}_{2} $$
- **At the gas-permeable membrane:** NH₃ (g) → NH₃ (aq) in internal fill; then equilibrium
  $$ \text{NH}_{3} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_{4}^{+} + \text{OH}^{-} $$
- The internal **pH glass electrode** senses the rise in [OH⁻] (or [NH₃] in the gas-sensing mode).

**(ii) Calculation (5 marks):**

The electrode responds to NH₃ activity. Calibration: $C_{\text{NH}_{4}\text{Cl (std)}} = 1\ \text{mM}$, $E_{\text{std}} = -30\ \text{mV}$, $E_{\text{unk}} = +10\ \text{mV}$.

Step 1 — Compute the difference:
$$ \Delta E = E_{\text{unk}} - E_{\text{std}} = +10 - (-30) = +40\ \text{mV} $$

Step 2 — Apply Nernst:
$$ \Delta E = S\,\log\!\left(\dfrac{C_{\text{unk}}}{C_{\text{std}}}\right) $$

(Note: depending on the sign convention the gas-sensing electrode's $E$ *increases* with increasing NH₃ — consistent with a basic shift in pH of the internal fill.)

$$ 40 = 59 \times \log\!\left(\dfrac{C_{\text{unk}}}{1\ \text{mM}}\right) $$

$$ \log C_{\text{unk}} - \log(1\ \text{mM}) = \dfrac{40}{59} = 0.678 $$

$$ \log C_{\text{unk}} = 0.678 + \log(10^{-3}) = 0.678 - 3 = -2.322 $$

$$ C_{\text{unk}} = 10^{-2.322} = \mathbf{4.76 \times 10^{-3}\ M} \approx 4.76\ \text{mM (NH}_{3}\text{)} $$

Step 3 — Convert NH₃ to urea (1 mol urea → 2 mol NH₃):
$$ C_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{4.76\ \text{mM}}{2} = \mathbf{2.38\ \text{mM}} $$

Step 4 — Account for dilution (sample 10 mL + enzyme 0.5 mL → 10.5 mL final). If the question is *strict* about the analytical concentration, divide by the dilution factor (10/10.5 = 0.952):
$$ C_{\text{urea,\,original}} = 2.38 \times 0.952 = \mathbf{2.27\ \text{mM}} $$
(If the dilution is ignored, **2.38 mM** is also acceptable; the examiner should award full credit if the working is shown.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on ISE/MSE questions in KTU 2024 Scheme exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting units on S** — must write **V/decade** or **mV/decade**; state the temperature (25 °C).
> 2. **Confusing Nernst and Nicolsky–Eisenman** — Nernst = one ion only; Nicolsky = with interferent $j$ and the **exponent $z_i/z_j$** on $a_j$.
> 3. **Sign convention errors on anion electrodes** — F⁻, Cl⁻ give *decreasing* E with increasing activity if you use the metal/ion convention; clearly state the convention.
> 4. **Confusing the role of activity vs concentration** — for KTU Module 4, "concentration" is acceptable if the medium is dilute and the answer is "approximately $a$", but you must mention the **Debye–Hückel** relationship in definition questions.
> 5. **In glucose-oxidase questions, missing the +0.7 V polarisation potential** for H₂O₂ oxidation, or omitting the **O₂-permeable Teflon** in the Clark electrode description.
> 6. **In derivation questions, skipping the equilibrium condition $\Delta G = 0$** — this is the *core* step and earns 1 of 2 marks on its own.
> 7. **Forgetting to write 1 mol urea → 2 mol NH₃** in the urea / urease / NH₃ electrode question (a classic 1-mark deduction).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read the night before the KTU exam):**
> - **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{0'} + S \log a_i$; $S = 2.303RT/zF = 0.05916/z$ V at 25 °C.
> - **Nicolsky–Eisenman:** $E = E^{0'} + S\,\log\!\left(a_{i} + \sum K_{ij}\,a_{j}^{z_{i}/z_{j}}\right)$; the **selectivity coefficient** is dimensionless.
> - **ISE types (4):** crystalline (LaF₃, Ag₂S), glass (H⁺, Na⁺), liquid-membrane with ionophore (K⁺-valinomycin, Ca²⁺-ETH 1001), gas-sensing (Severinghaus — NH₃, CO₂).
> - **MSE / Biosensor types:** enzyme (GOx, urease), whole-cell, immuno-electrode.
> - **Glucose oxidase reaction:** Glucose + O₂ → Gluconic acid + H₂O₂; detected amperometrically at **+0.7 V** (H₂O₂ oxidation) or at **−0.6 V** (O₂ reduction — Clark mode).
> - **Clark oxygen electrode:** Pt cathode at −0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl, O₂-permeable **Teflon** membrane; reaction O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O.
> - **Urease reaction:** Urea + H₂O → 2 NH₃ + CO₂; 1 mol urea yields 2 mol NH₃.
> - **Detection limit of practical ISE:** 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹⁰ M; **response time:** 1–30 s.
> - **Selectivity criterion:** $\log K_{ij} \le -2$ is "good enough" for routine analysis.
> - **High-impedance voltmeter** is required because membrane resistance is **10 MΩ to 1 GΩ**.
> - **Activity = γ × C**; **Debye–Hückel** gives $\log\gamma_{\pm} = -Az_{+}\vert z_{-}\vert \sqrt{I}$.
> - **KTU-asked keywords to remember:** Nicolsky–Eisenman, selectivity coefficient, Severinghaus, Clark electrode, ionophore, valinomycin, immobilisation, **Nernstian slope**, ionic-strength adjuster (ISA), activity vs concentration.
> - **Always state temperature** when writing $S$ — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrument for Potentiometric Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instrument for Potentiometric Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Potentiometry** is a branch of electroanalytical chemistry that measures the **electromotive force (EMF) / cell potential (Ecell)** of an electrochemical cell under conditions of **zero current flow** (I = 0). The instruments used for potentiometric studies constitute a complete measurement chain comprising: **(i) a reference electrode of constant and reproducible potential, (ii) an indicator (working) electrode whose potential responds selectively to the activity of the analyte ion, (iii) a high-impedance voltmeter (potentiometer / electrometer / pH meter / ion-meter), and (iv) a salt bridge to maintain charge neutrality while preserving liquid-junction integrity.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Zero-Current Condition:** Unlike voltammetric methods, potentiometric measurements require that the net current through the cell be effectively zero. This ensures that the measured potential corresponds to the true **thermodynamic equilibrium potential** governed solely by the Nernst equation, with no contribution from over-potentials (η) due to polarization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of potentiometry like **measuring the "social pressure" between two personalities in a conversation**:

- Imagine two electrodes sitting in a liquid. The **reference electrode** is like a calm, neutral person who never changes their mood regardless of the surroundings — their "voltage" stays fixed.
- The **indicator electrode** is like a sensitive friend whose mood shifts based on the concentration of a specific ion (H⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) in the solution.
- The voltmeter / pH meter acts as a **silent, infinitely patient observer** who simply *listens* (without disturbing the conversation) and reports the *difference in mood* (potential) between the two electrodes.
- The **salt bridge** is like a telephone line that maintains harmony — it keeps the two "personalities" electrically connected without mixing their thoughts (solutions).

The numerical difference between the two electrode potentials gives you the **analytical signal** — directly related to the **logarithm of the analyte activity** via the **Nernst Equation**.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics

The following standard reference values are used universally in potentiometric calculations and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Constants Used in Potentiometry**
> - **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C·mol⁻¹**
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **Standard Temperature, T = 298.15 K (25 °C)**
> - **RT/F at 25 °C = 0.02569 V = 25.69 mV**
> - **(2.303 RT)/F at 25 °C = 0.05916 V ≈ 0.0592 V (often rounded to 0.059 V)**

## 1.4 Classification of Potentiometric Instruments

The complete instrumentation chain for potentiometric studies can be classified into the following hierarchical categories:

| S.No. | Instrument Category | Specific Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reference Electrodes** | Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE), Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl), Mercury/Mercurous Sulphate | Provide a **constant, known, and stable** potential (Eref = constant) |
| 2 | **Indicator Electrodes** | Glass electrode, Hydrogen electrode, Metal/Metal-ion electrodes | Develop a potential (**Eind**) that varies as a function of analyte activity |
| 3 | **Ion-Selective Electrodes (ISEs)** | pH glass, fluoride ISE, Na⁺-ISE, K⁺-ISE, Ca²⁺-ISE, ion-selective field-effect transistors (ISFETs) | Selective measurement of specific ionic activities |
| 4 | **Measuring Devices** | Manual potentiometer, Digital pH meter, Ion-meter, Electrometer (high input impedance > 10¹² Ω) | Measure Ecell with minimal current drain |
| 5 | **Auxiliary Components** | Salt bridge (KCl, KNO₃, NH₄NO₃), Stirrer, Temperature compensation probe, Calibration buffers | Maintain electrical continuity and accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Galvanic Cell Architecture in Potentiometry

A potentiometric cell is essentially a **galvanic cell (electrochemical cell)** that has been deliberately designed such that the measurement apparatus draws **negligible current** from it. The general cell representation is:

**Reference Electrode ‖ Analyte Solution ‖ Indicator Electrode**

The double vertical lines (`‖`) denote a **salt bridge / liquid junction**, which serves two critical purposes:
1. **Completes the electrical circuit** by permitting ion flow (charge balance).
2. **Prevents direct mixing** of the reference and analyte half-cells (chemical isolation).

The cell EMF is given by:

$$E_{cell} = E_{indicator} - E_{reference} + E_{junction}$$

For an ideally functioning cell, $E_{junction} \approx 0$ (achieved by using a salt bridge containing a high-equimobility electrolyte such as KCl where $\mu_{K^+} \approx \mu_{Cl^-}$).

## 2.2 The Nernst Equation — The Heart of Potentiometry

For any reversible redox half-reaction of the type:

$$Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$$

The **Nernst Equation** gives the equilibrium electrode potential:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$$

In base-10 logarithmic form (preferred for analytical work):

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize at 25 °C (298.15 K):** $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$. Thus the equation becomes:
> $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

## 2.3 Specialized Forms of the Nernst Equation for ISEs

### (a) pH Glass Electrode (H⁺-responsive glass membrane)

For the glass electrode responding to hydrogen ion activity:

$$E_{glass} = E_{glass}^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log a_{H^+}$$

Since $pH = -\log a_{H^+}$:

$$\boxed{E_{glass} = E_{glass}^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH = E_{glass}^{\circ} + 0.05916 \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}}$$

### (b) General Cation-Responsive ISE (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)

For a generic cation $M^{n+}$:

$$E_{ISE} = E_{ISE}^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log a_{M^{n+}}$$

### (c) Anion-Responsive ISE (e.g., F⁻, Cl⁻, NO₃⁻)

For a generic monovalent anion $A^{-}$:

$$E_{ISE} = E_{ISE}^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log a_{A^-}$$

### (d) Combined Cell EMF for pH Measurement

$$E_{cell} = E_{glass} - E_{ref} = (E_{glass}^{\circ} - E_{ref}) + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

Defining $K' = (E_{glass}^{\circ} - E_{ref})$ as the **asymmetry / system constant**:

$$E_{cell} = K' + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

This two-parameter linear equation ($K'$ and slope $S = 2.303RT/F$) is determined experimentally using **two standard buffer solutions** of known pH (calibration).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Equation | Description | Validity / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log \dfrac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$ | Nernst Equation (general) | All temperatures; n = electrons transferred |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log \dfrac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$ | Nernst Equation at 25 °C | T = 298.15 K |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{indicator} - E_{reference} + E_{junction}$ | Cell EMF definition | Convention: indicator − reference |
| 4 | $E_{cell} = K' + \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH$ | pH electrode calibration | Two-parameter linear fit |
| 5 | $E_{ISE} = E_{ISE}^{\circ} + \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log a_{M^{n+}}$ | Cation-responsive ISE | M = Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, etc. |
| 6 | $E_{ISE} = E_{ISE}^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log a_{A^-}$ | Anion-responsive ISE | A = F⁻, Cl⁻, NO₃⁻ |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 0.05916 \text{ V/decade}$ | Nernstian slope at 25 °C | For n = 1 reactions |
| 8 | $S_{n} = \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \text{ V/decade}$ | Nernstian slope for n-electron | Divalent: 0.0296 V/decade |
| 9 | $a_i = \gamma_i \cdot c_i$ | Activity–concentration relation | γ = activity coefficient (γ → 1 in dilute solutions) |
| 10 | $K'_{sp} = a_{Ag^+} \cdot a_{Cl^-}$ | Solubility product (F⁻ ISE example) | Used in indirect ISE methods |
| 11 | $E_{junction} = -\dfrac{RT}{F} \sum \dfrac{t_i z_i}{z_i} \ln a_i$ | Liquid junction potential | t_i = transport number; minimised by KCl bridge |
| 12 | $pX = \pm \dfrac{(E_{cell} - K')}{S}$ | Analyte concentration from cell EMF | + for cations, − for anions |

## 2.5 The Three "Why's" of Potentiometric Instrumentation Design

**Why a high-impedance voltmeter?**
The internal resistance of a glass membrane is typically **50–500 MΩ**. A voltmeter of ordinary impedance (~10 MΩ) would draw current, polarize the electrode, and cause the measured potential to be lower than the true Nernstian value. Hence, electrometers and modern pH meters have input impedances exceeding **10¹² Ω**, ensuring current drain remains < 1 pA.

**Why two-point calibration?**
The glass electrode exhibits an "asymmetry potential" due to imperfections in membrane manufacture (different inner/outer surface conditions). This shifts $E^{\circ}$ unpredictably. Calibration with two buffers of known pH (e.g., pH 4.01 and pH 6.86) determines both $K'$ and the slope $S$ simultaneously, eliminating this systematic error.

**Why a salt bridge with equitransferent ions?**
The liquid junction potential arises whenever two solutions of different composition meet. KCl is used because $t_{K^+} \approx t_{Cl^-} \approx 0.49$, making the sum $(t_+ - t_-) \approx 0$ and thereby minimizing $E_{junction}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biotechnology

- **Fermentation monitoring:** Online pH probes (steam-sterilizable glass electrodes) track culture pH in real time for optimal microbial growth and recombinant protein production.
- **Blood gas analysis:** pH, pCO₂, and pO₂ electrodes diagnose respiratory/metabolic disorders in clinical pathology.
- **Environmental monitoring:** Fluoride, nitrate, and ammonium ISEs test drinking water and soil leachates.
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Argentometric ISEs measure chloride in injectables; fluoride ISEs control toothpaste formulations.
- **Biomedical implants:** ISFET-based pH sensors for gastric and intracranial monitoring.
- **Food industry:** Sodium and potassium ISEs for salt-content analysis in cheese, processed meats, and beverages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic Principles

### Step 1: Start with the Gibbs Free Energy Relation

For a reversible electrochemical cell, the change in Gibbs free energy is related to the cell potential by:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

This is because electrical work done by the cell equals $W_{elec} = -nFE_{cell}$, and at constant T and P, $\Delta G = W_{max,rev}$.

### Step 2: Relate $\Delta G$ to Reaction Quotient Q

From classical thermodynamics:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** (activities raised to stoichiometric powers) and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is the standard free-energy change.

### Step 3: Combine the Two Equations

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$-nFE_{cell} = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

At standard conditions ($\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ}$ and $E = E^{\circ}$):

$$-nFE^{\circ} = \Delta G^{\circ}$$

Substituting back:

$$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 4: Solve for $E_{cell}$

$$nFE_{cell} = nFE^{\circ} - RT \ln Q$$

Dividing both sides by $nF$:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Step 5: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** for the cell. For a half-cell reaction, the same form applies with $Q$ replaced by the appropriate activity ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Transition:** The Nernst equation is therefore a *direct thermodynamic consequence* of the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation applied to electrochemical work. It is *not* an empirical fit — it is theoretically exact for reversible electrodes at equilibrium.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Slope at 25 °C

Insert the numerical values:
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $T = 298.15$ K
- $F = 96,485$ C·mol⁻¹
- $2.303 = \ln(10)$

Compute the numerator:

$$2.303 \times R \times T = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15$$

Performing the multiplication step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
8.314 \times 298.15 &= 2478.82 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \\
2.303 \times 2478.82 &= 5708.74 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \\
\frac{5708.74}{96485} &= 0.05916 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}\bigg|_{25^{\circ}C} = 0.05916 \text{ V} \approx 59.16 \text{ mV per decade}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Calibration Equation for a pH Glass Electrode

### Step 1: Half-Cell Reaction at the Glass Membrane

The glass membrane develops a potential that depends on the **boundary potential** established by the difference in H⁺ activity across a thin hydrated gel layer:

$$E_{boundary} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log \frac{a_{H^+, \text{outer}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inner}}}$$

The inner solution has a **fixed** H⁺ activity (typically 0.1 M HCl), so its logarithm is a constant absorbed into $E_{glass}^{\circ}$.

### Step 2: Indicator Electrode Potential

$$E_{glass} = E_{glass}^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log a_{H^+, \text{outer}}$$

Using the pH definition $pH = -\log a_{H^+}$:

$$E_{glass} = E_{glass}^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH \quad \text{[sign convention used by some texts]}$$

OR equivalently (with the more common sign convention in pH measurement):

$$E_{glass} = E_{glass}^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C, slope = +59.16 mV/pH)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention:** The glass electrode behaves as if it were a cation-responsive electrode. Therefore, the measured $E_{cell}$ **increases** with pH. This means alkaline solutions give more positive readings — a frequent source of confusion in KTU answer scripts.

### Step 3: Combine with Reference Electrode

$$E_{cell} = E_{glass} - E_{ref} = (E_{glass}^{\circ} - E_{ref}) + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot pH$$

Define the **system constant**:

$$K' = E_{glass}^{\circ} - E_{ref}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = K' + S \cdot pH \quad \text{where} \quad S = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}}$$

This is a straight line in $E_{cell}$ vs. pH space with slope $S$ and intercept $K'$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A fluoride ISE and SCE are used to measure F⁻ in a water sample. The cell EMF is **+217.4 mV** at 25 °C. A standard solution containing 0.0010 M F⁻ gives a reading of **+158.2 mV** under identical conditions. Calculate the fluoride ion concentration in the sample, assuming Nernstian response (slope = 59.16 mV per decade for a monovalent ion).

### Solution:

**Step 1:** The cell EMF for an anion-responsive ISE follows:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log a_{F^-} = E^{\circ} - S \log [F^-]$$

(taking $\gamma_{F^-} \approx 1$ in dilute aqueous solutions).

**Step 2:** For the standard solution:

$$158.2 = E^{\circ} - 59.16 \cdot \log(0.0010) = E^{\circ} - 59.16 \cdot (-3)$$

$$158.2 = E^{\circ} + 177.48$$

$$E^{\circ} = 158.2 - 177.48 = -19.28 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** For the unknown sample:

$$217.4 = -19.28 - 59.16 \cdot \log [F^-]_{sample}$$

$$59.16 \cdot \log [F^-]_{sample} = -19.28 - 217.4 = -236.68$$

$$\log [F^-]_{sample} = \frac{-236.68}{59.16} = -4.000$$

$$[F^-]_{sample} = 10^{-4.000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$$

### Step 4: Final Answer:

$$\boxed{[F^-]_{sample} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M} = 0.10 \text{ mg/L (F⁻)}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Key Electrodes

| Parameter | Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) | Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) | Glass Electrode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Half-Reaction** | $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_2(g)$ | $Hg_2Cl_2 + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2Hg + 2Cl^-$ | $AgCl + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag + Cl^-$ | $H^+_{outer} \rightleftharpoons H^+_{inner}$ (boundary potential) |
| **Standard Potential (E°)** | **0.000 V** (defined) | **+0.244 V** (vs. SHE) at 25 °C | **+0.222 V** (vs. SHE) at 25 °C (sat. KCl) | N/A (membrane-based) |
| **Type** | Primary reference | Secondary reference | Secondary reference | Indicator |
| **Response to** | H⁺ activity | [Cl⁻] constant (saturated KCl) | [Cl⁻] constant | H⁺ activity (pH) |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Low | Moderate (Cl⁻ solubility varies) | Low | High (requires compensation) |
| **Working pH Range** | Full range | Full range | Full range | pH 0 – 14 (with alkaline error > pH 12) |
| **Drawbacks** | Fragile, requires H₂ gas, Pt poisoning | KCl crystallization at frit | Light-sensitive | Alkaline error, acid error, high impedance |

## 3.6 Calibration Strategy (Two-Point Method)

Given two standard buffers with pH₁ and pH₂ (with measured EMFs E₁ and E₂):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= K' + S \cdot pH_1 \\
E_2 &= K' + S \cdot pH_2
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting:

$$E_2 - E_1 = S \cdot (pH_2 - pH_1)$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{pH_2 - pH_1}}$$

Then:

$$K' = E_1 - S \cdot pH_1$$

For an unknown sample with measured EMF $E_x$:

$$\boxed{pH_x = \frac{E_x - K'}{S}}$$

This is the **standard operating protocol** for all pH and ion-meter measurements in KTU laboratory practicals.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Potentiometric Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Reference Half-Cell]
        refwire[Pt or Hg Contact Wire]
        refinner[Inner Reference Solution e.g. saturated KCl]
        refelem[Reference Element e.g. Hg2Cl2 or AgCl]
        refwire --> refinner --> refelem
    end

    subgraph A2[Indicator Half-Cell]
        indwire[Internal Reference Electrode Ag/AgCl]
        indinner[Inner Solution e.g. 0.1 M HCl]
        indmemb[Selective Membrane Glass Crystal or Polymer]
        indwire --> indinner --> indmemb
    end

    subgraph A3[Sample Compartment]
        sample[Analyte Solution Stirred Thermostatted]
    end

    subgraph A4[Measurement Unit]
        sb[High Impedance Salt Bridge e.g. KCl]
        volt[Electrometer or Digital pH Meter Input Impedance greater than 10 to power 12 ohm]
        disp[Digital Display Ecell in mV or pH]
    end

    refelem -.Junction.- sb
    sb -.Connects to.- sample
    sample --> indmemb
    indmemb --> indinner
    indinner --> indwire
    indwire --> volt
    refwire --> volt
    volt --> disp
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Glass Electrode Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Thin Glass Membrane pH Sensitive 0.1 mm thick] --> node2[Outer Hydrated Gel Layer H plus responsive]
    node1 --> node3[Inner Hydrated Gel Layer H plus responsive]
    node2 --> node4[Outer Test Solution a H plus unknown]
    node3 --> node5[Inner 0.1 M HCl Reference Solution]
    node5 --> node6[Internal Ag AgCl Electrode]
    node6 --> node7[Coaxial Cable to High Impedance Voltmeter]
    node4 --> node2
    node2 -.Boundary Potential.-> node1
    node1 -.Boundary Potential.-> node3
    node3 --> node5
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Pt Wire Lead] --> nodeB[Mercury Hg Liquid]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Mercurous Chloride Hg2Cl2 Paste]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Saturated KCl Solution]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Porous Frit or Ceramic Junction Contact with Sample]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Side Arm for KCl Refill]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Potentiometric Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Start Measurement Protocol] --> nodeB[Power on pH Meter Allow 15 min Warm up]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Calibrate with Buffer 1 e.g. pH 4.01]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rinse Electrode with Distilled Water]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Calibrate with Buffer 2 e.g. pH 6.86]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Verify Slope 95 to 102 percent Nernstian]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Rinse and Immerse in Sample]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Stir Gently and Record Ecell]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Apply Calibration Equation to Compute pH or pX]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Report Result with Confidence Interval]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — ISE Measurement Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeK[IS Ion Selective Electrode Develops E_ISE] --> nodeL[Reference Electrode Develops E_ref]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Both Connected to Differential Amplifier with Guarded Input]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Microprocessor Computes pX using Nernst Equation]
    nodeO --> nodeP[LCD Display Shows Concentration Activity or pX]
```
