This is why ASV is the gold standard for trace heavy-metal detection (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺ in drinking water). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam Pattern (Part A):** 3-mark questions require a *concise definition or one-shot explanation*, typically 3–5 sentences. Marks are awarded for: (1) accurate definition, (2) the key equation (if applicable), and (3) one practical example or diagram. ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024]** > **Define voltammetry. Mention the role of a supporting electrolyte.** **Model Answer:** Voltammetry is an electroanalytical technique in which the **current flowing through an electrochemical cell** is measured as a function of the **applied potential** between a working and reference electrode. The resulting current-vs-potential plot is the **voltammogram**, which provides both qualitative (peak potential) and quantitative (peak current) information about electroactive analytes. A **supporting electrolyte** (e.g., KCl, KNO₃, or a buffer at high concentration, typically ≥ 100× the analyte concentration) is added to suppress the **migration** of charged analytes in the electric field. This ensures that mass transport occurs **only by diffusion**, simplifying the analysis and making the measured current diffusion-controlled. *(Word count: ~95 — appropriate for a 3-mark answer)* ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]** > **State the Randles–Ševčík equation. What is its significance in cyclic voltammetry?** **Model Answer:** The Randles–Ševčík equation for a reversible redox couple at 25 °C is: $$i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\, n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C v^{1/2}$$ where $i_p$ = peak current, $n$ = electrons transferred, $A$ = electrode area, $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $C$ = bulk concentration, $v$ = scan rate. **Significance:** The equation establishes that **$i_p$ is directly proportional to the analyte concentration $C$** (quantitative basis) and to the **square root of scan rate $v^{1/2}$**. The latter dependence is the diagnostic criterion for a **diffusion-controlled reversible process** — if a plot of $i_p$ vs $v^{1/2}$ is linear and passes through the origin, the electron transfer is Nernstian-reversible. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Sub-parts test depth of conceptual understanding, derivations, instrumentation diagrams, and applications. ### Question B-A — Choice 1 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, instrumentation, and working** of **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)**. Draw a typical cyclic voltammogram and label its key features. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Randles–Ševčík equation** and discuss how CV can be used to determine whether a redox couple is **reversible, quasi-reversible, or irreversible**. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Principle (2 marks):** In CV, the working electrode potential is **swept linearly with time** in the forward direction (e.g., from $E_1$ to $E_2$) and then **reversely** back to $E_1$, producing a triangular waveform. The current is recorded as a function of potential, yielding a cyclic voltammogram. For a reversible couple $Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$: - During the forward sweep, the analyte is **reduced** at the cathodic peak potential $E_{pc}$, producing a cathodic peak current $i_{pc}$. - During the reverse sweep, the product generated in the forward sweep is **re-oxidized**, producing an anodic peak current $i_{pa}$. **Instrumentation (3 marks):** A CV experiment requires: - A **potentiostat** — the electronic heart that controls potential and measures current. - A **three-electrode cell**: working electrode (Pt, Au, Glassy Carbon), reference electrode (Ag/AgCl or SCE), and counter electrode (Pt wire). - A **supporting electrolyte** (e.g., 0.1 M KCl) to suppress migration. - A **function generator** to provide the triangular waveform. - A **data acquisition system** (computer with ADC). **Working and Voltammogram (2 marks):** The characteristic parameters extracted from the voltammogram are: - **$E_{pa}$** (anodic peak potential) - **$E_{pc}$** (cathodic peak potential) - **$i_{pa}$** and **$i_{pc}$** (peak currents) - **$\Delta E_p = E_{pa} - E_{pc}$** - **$E_{1/2} = (E_{pa} + E_{pc})/2$** ≈ formal potential $E°'$ For a **reversible** one-electron process at 25 °C: $\Delta E_p \approx 59$ mV and $i_{pa}/i_{pc} \approx 1$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation (4 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.2 for the full step-by-step derivation)* Starting from Fick's Second Law with a linear potential ramp, solving for the concentration profile near the planar electrode, applying Fick's First Law to obtain the flux, and integrating to obtain the total charge passed at the peak, the final result is: $$i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\, n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C v^{1/2}$$ **Diagnosis of reversibility (3 marks):** | Criterion | Reversible | Quasi-reversible | Irreversible | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\Delta E_p$ | $59/n$ mV (constant with $v$) | $> 59/n$ mV (increases with $v$) | No return peak | | $i_p/v^{1/2}$ | Constant (independent of $v$) | Decreases with $v$ | Linear in $v$ (adsorption) | | $i_{pa}/i_{pc}$ | $\approx 1$ | $< 1$ | 0 | ### Question B-B — Choice 2 (Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **principle and types of Polarography**. Derive the **Ilkovič equation** and explain its significance in quantitative analysis. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Anodic Stripping Voltammetry (ASV)** with a neat block diagram. How is it used for **trace analysis of heavy metals** in environmental samples? #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Principle of Polarography (2 marks):** Polarography is a specialized branch of voltammetry that employs a **Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME)** as the working electrode. The DME consists of a mercury reservoir connected to a glass capillary from which mercury drops form and fall at regular intervals (drop life $t \approx 2$–6 s). The continuously renewing surface provides **reproducibility** and a **fresh, uncontaminated electrode** for each drop, eliminating poisoning effects. As the potential is scanned, the polarogram shows characteristic **sigmoidal waves** (not peaks), with the limiting current $i_d$ (diffusion current) proportional to concentration and the **half-wave potential $E_{1/2}$** identifying the analyte. **Types of Polarography (2 marks):** | Type | Feature | Improvement | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **DC Polarography (Classic)** | Linear DC ramp; current measured at end of drop life | Original method; detection limit ~10⁻⁵ M | | **Tast Polarography** | Current sampled near end of drop life | Reduces oscillations | | **Pulse Polarography** | Pulses superimposed on DC ramp | Detection limit ~10⁻⁸ M | | **Differential Pulse Polarography** | Differential current measured | Sharp peaks; resolution enhanced | | **AC Polarography** | AC signal superimposed | Capacitive current suppression | **Ilkovič Equation Derivation (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.3 for the full step-by-step derivation)* The Ilkovič equation relates the average diffusion current to the analyte concentration: $$\bar{i}_d = 607\, n\, D^{1/2}\, m^{2/3}\, t^{1/6}\, C$$ This equation is the **quantitative foundation** of polarography: by measuring $\bar{i}_d$ at known $m$ and $t$, and using a calibration constant, $C$ can be determined. The equation explains why mercury flow rate $m$ and drop time $t$ must be carefully controlled for reproducible analysis. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **ASV Principle (2 marks):** ASV is a **two-step** technique: 1. **Deposition Step:** The working electrode (typically an **HMDE** or **Mercury Film Electrode** on a glassy carbon substrate) is held at a **sufficiently negative potential** for a fixed time (e.g., 60–600 s). During this step, metal cations (e.g., Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺) in the solution are **reduced and concentrated** as amalgams in the mercury: $$M^{n+} + ne^- + \text{Hg} \rightarrow M(\text{Hg})$$ 2. **Stripping Step:** The potential is scanned in the **positive (anodic) direction**. The deposited metal is **re-oxidized** back into solution: $$M(\text{Hg}) \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^- + \text{Hg}$$ The peak current during stripping is proportional to the bulk concentration: $$i_p = K \cdot C \cdot \tau_{\text{dep}} \cdot v^{1/2}$$ where $\tau_{\text{dep}}$ is the deposition time. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Deposition Cell
Hg film or HMDE
Negative potential E_dep]:::dep nodeB[Stirrer
enhances mass transport during deposition]:::stir nodeC[Stripping Sweep
Linear ramp in positive direction]:::strip nodeD[Data Acquisition
Peak currents ipa ipb ipc]:::data nodeE[Quantification
ipa proportional to C in ppb]:::quant nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE classDef dep fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000 classDef stir fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef strip fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef data fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 classDef quant fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff ``` **Heavy-Metal Trace Analysis (3 marks):** ASV is the **gold standard** for detecting trace heavy metals in environmental water samples. The pre-concentration factor (typically 100–1000×) brings detection limits down to **0.1–1 ppb** (parts per billion), well below the WHO limits for Pb (10 ppb), Cd (3 ppb), and Cu (2000 ppb). Standard additions method is used for matrix-matched quantification. Common applications include analysis of **drinking water, river water, industrial effluents, blood lead levels**, and **soil extracts**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting the Supporting Electrolyte:** A common 1-mark loss comes from omitting to mention the role of supporting electrolyte in suppressing migration. *Always state this when describing any voltammetric setup.* > > 2. **Confusing Peak Current with Diffusion Current:** In CV, you have *peak current* $i_p$ (Randles–Ševčík, with $v^{1/2}$ dependence). In polarography, you have *diffusion/limiting current* $i_d$ (Ilkovič, with $m^{2/3} t^{1/6}$ dependence). Mixing them up costs full marks. > > 3. **Wrong Reference Electrode Polarity:** The convention is **E (V) vs Reference Electrode (Ag/AgCl or SCE)**. Plotting against SHE without specifying will lose marks. The **cathodic** current is conventionally plotted **upward (positive)** in CV; verify your textbook before the exam. > > 4. **Skipping Boundary Conditions in Derivations:** When deriving Cottrell or Ilkovič, you **must** explicitly state the three boundary conditions. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for BCs. > > 5. **Forgetting Units and Prefactors:** The Randles–Ševčík constant $2.69 \times 10^5$ assumes specific units ($A$ in cm², $C$ in mol/cm³, $D$ in cm²/s, $v$ in V/s). Using wrong units gives the wrong numerical answer. *Always write units before plugging numbers.* > > 6. **Not Labelling the Voltammogram:** A voltammogram without labels ($i_p$, $E_p$, $\Delta E_p$, $E_{1/2}$) loses 1–2 marks. Always provide a fully labelled diagram. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Walking Into the Exam Hall** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - **Voltammetry:** Measurement of current *i* as a function of applied potential *E*. - **Voltammogram:** The *i* vs *E* plot. - **Three-electrode cell:** WE + RE + CE, controlled by a potentiostat. ### 📐 Must-Know Equations - **Nernst Equation:** $E = E° + (RT/nF)\ln([Ox]/[Red])$ - **Cottrell Equation:** $i(t) = nFA C \sqrt{D} / \sqrt{\pi t}$ - **Randles–Ševčík (CV):** $i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5}) n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C v^{1/2}$ - **Ilkovič (Polarography):** $\bar{i}_d = 607 n D^{1/2} m^{2/3} t^{1/6} C$ - **Peak Separation (CV, reversible):** $\Delta E_p = 59/n$ mV at 25 °C ### 🔬 Must-Know Techniques - **DC Polarography** → DME → Ilkovič equation → sigmoidal waves. - **Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV)** → linear scan → single peak. - **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)** → triangular scan → forward + reverse peaks. - **Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)** → pulses → sharp peaks, low DL. - **Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV)** → square waveform → fastest, lowest DL. - **Anodic Stripping Voltammetry (ASV)** → deposition + stripping → ppb detection. ### 🎯 Diagnosis Cues - **$i_p \propto v^{1/2}$** → Diffusion-controlled reversible process. - **$i_p \propto v$** → Surface-adsorbed species (thin-layer behavior). - **$\Delta E_p = 59/n$ mV, constant with $v$** → Reversible. - **$\Delta E_p > 59/n$ mV, increases with $v$** → Quasi-reversible. - **No return peak** → Irreversible. ### 🧪 Key Applications - **Heavy-metal trace analysis** (Pb, Cd, Cu, Zn) → ASV. - **Drug and pharmaceutical assays** → DPV, SWV. - **Biosensors** (glucose, dopamine) → Amperometry (constant-potential voltammetry). - **Redox characterization of new compounds** → CV. - **DNA damage and biomolecular interaction studies** → DPV with redox indicators. ### ⚙️ Practical Reminders - **Always add supporting electrolyte** (≥ 100× analyte). - **Deoxygenate the solution** with N₂ or Ar purge (O₂ gives reduction peaks at ~ –0.3 V and ~ –1.0 V vs SCE). - **Maintain constant temperature** (25 °C standard) for reproducible measurements. - **Use proper cleaning protocols** for working electrodes (polishing with alumina slurry for glassy carbon).

topics.title: Cyclic and Pulse Voltametry: Applications of Voltammetry, Study of Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cyclic and Pulse Voltammetry: Applications of Voltammetry, Study of Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)** is a versatile electroanalytical (potentiostatic) technique in which the working electrode potential is linearly swept **triangularly** between two switching potentials (E₁ and E₂) while the resulting current at the working electrode is recorded as a function of the applied potential. The triangular excitation gives a forward (cathodic) scan followed by a reverse (anodic) scan, producing a characteristic **i vs E** profile called a *cyclic voltammogram*.

The technique was popularized by **Nicholson & Shain (1964)** and is governed by mass transport via semi-infinite planar diffusion to a stationary electrode (in unstirred solution). It is used for:

- Qualitative identification of redox couples
- Determination of formal redox potentials $E^{0'}$
- Estimation of electron transfer reversibility
- Measurement of diffusion coefficients
- Evaluation of heterogeneous rate constants $k^{0}$
- Investigation of coupled chemical reactions (EC, ECE mechanisms)

---

## 1.2 Pulse Voltammetry — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Voltammetry** is a class of voltammetric techniques in which small potential pulses (of fixed amplitude) are superimposed on a linearly increasing (or decreasing) potential staircase. Because the current is sampled **twice** (before and at the end of each pulse), the non-faradaic charging current is largely subtracted out, dramatically improving the **signal-to-noise ratio** and lowering detection limits to the **nanomolar (10⁻⁹ M)** or even **picomolar (10⁻¹² M)** range.

The two principal variants are:

| Variant | Pulse Shape | Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| **Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)** | Small constant-height pulses on a linear ramp | Difference of two currents $\Delta i$ |
| **Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV)** | Symmetric square pulses on a staircase | Forward minus reverse current |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hill-and-Valley" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for CV:** Imagine a hiker walking up a hill (potential increases) and then back down (potential decreases). At the top of the hill, she reaches a peak current when the electrochemical reaction rate is maximum — this is the **cathodic peak** ($i_{pc}$). On the way down, when the reverse reaction kicks in, she encounters the **anodic peak** ($i_{pa}$). The *shape*, *separation*, and *symmetry* of these peaks tell us whether the redox system behaves like a perfectly elastic spring (reversible), a sticky spring (quasi-reversible), or a broken spring (irreversible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Pulse Voltammetry:** Compare CV (loud noisy stadium) vs. DPV (using noise-cancelling headphones). The "noise" is the **charging (capacitive) current**, while the "music" is the **faradaic current** of interest. Pulses allow the instrument to listen right when the music is loudest and to subtract the steady background hum.

---

## 1.4 Stripping Voltammetry — Special Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stripping Voltammetry** is an extremely sensitive two-step pulse-based technique. In the **deposition step**, the analyte (e.g., Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺) is preconcentrated onto the working electrode (often a hanging mercury drop or mercury film electrode) by reduction at a fixed potential for a controlled time. In the **stripping step**, the deposited metal is re-oxidized (anodic stripping) by a potential scan, and the resulting peak current is proportional to the original analyte concentration. Detection limits as low as **10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻¹¹ M** are routinely achieved — a million-fold enhancement over direct voltammetry.

Variants include **Anodic Stripping Voltammetry (ASV)**, **Cathodic Stripping Voltammetry (CSV)**, and **Adsorptive Stripping Voltammetry (AdSV)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cyclic Voltammetric Waveform

The triangular waveform is characterized by:

- **Initial potential** $E_i$
- **Switching potential 1** $E_{\lambda 1}$ (where scan direction reverses)
- **Switching potential 2** $E_{\lambda 2}$ (often same as $E_i$ for one full cycle)
- **Scan rate** $\nu$ (V/s)
- **Number of cycles** $N$

The forward scan (e.g., from +0.5 V to –0.5 V for a reduction) reduces species **O** to **R**:

$$O + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons R$$

The reverse scan oxidizes **R** back to **O**.

---

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Framework

### (a) Planar Diffusion — The Basis of CV Theory

For a stationary planar electrode in unstirred solution, Fick's Second Law applies:

$$\frac{\partial C_O(x,t)}{\partial t} = D_O \frac{\partial^{2} C_O(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}}$$

with initial and boundary conditions:

$$C_O(x,0) = C_O^{*} \quad ; \quad C_O(\infty,t) = C_O^{*}$$

The Cottrell solution (potential-step boundary condition) gives the current as a function of time. When the potential is swept linearly with scan rate $\nu$, the resulting peak current for a **reversible system** is given by the **Randles–Sevcik equation**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examination** — Master these.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description / Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\, n^{3/2}\, A\, D^{1/2}\, C^{*}\, \nu^{1/2}$ | **Randles–Sevcik** for reversible CV at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | A |
| 2 | $\Delta E_p = E_{pa} - E_{pc} = \dfrac{59}{n}\,\text{mV}$ | Reversibility criterion at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | mV |
| 3 | $E^{0'} = \dfrac{E_{pa} + E_{pc}}{2}$ | Formal redox potential | V |
| 4 | $\dfrac{i_{pa}}{i_{pc}} = 1$ | Reversible one-electron couple | dimensionless |
| 5 | $i_p \propto \nu^{1/2}$ | Diffusional control (reversible) | — |
| 6 | $i_p \propto \nu$ | Surface-adsorbed species | — |
| 7 | $\psi = \dfrac{k^{0}}{\sqrt{\pi D \nu n F / RT}}$ | Nicholson–Shain dimensionless parameter | — |
| 8 | $\Delta E_p \approx \dfrac{59}{n} + \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\left[0.780 + \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{D}}{k^{0}}\right) + \ln\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{nF\nu}{RT}}\right)\right]$ | Quasi-reversible $\Delta E_p$ | mV |
| 9 | $I_p = \dfrac{n^{2}F^{2}}{4RT}\,A\,\Gamma\,\nu$ | Surface-confined (Laviron) | A |
| 10 | $i_{p,DPV} = \dfrac{n^{2}F^{2}}{4RT}\,A\,\Gamma\,\Delta E\, \dfrac{D^{1/2}}{\pi^{1/2}t_p^{1/2}}$ | Differential pulse peak current | A |
| 11 | $i_{p,SWV} \propto nFA\,D^{1/2}\,\nu^{1/2}$ | Square wave voltammetry peak current | A |
| 12 | $E_{SWV} - E_{CV}^{0'} \approx \dfrac{28}{n}\,\text{mV}$ | SWV peak position (reversible) | mV |

Where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $A$ = electrode area (cm²)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $C^{*}$ = bulk concentration (mol/cm³)
- $\nu$ = scan rate (V/s)
- $\Gamma$ = surface coverage (mol/cm²)
- $\alpha$ = transfer coefficient
- $k^{0}$ = heterogeneous rate constant (cm/s)
- $\Delta E$ = pulse amplitude
- $t_p$ = pulse width

---

## 2.4 Pulse Voltammetry — Operational Logic

### Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)

1. A linear potential ramp of step $\Delta E_s$ is applied.
2. At the end of each step, a **pulse** of amplitude $\Delta E_p$ (typically 10–100 mV) is superimposed.
3. Current sampled **before** the pulse (at $t_1$) and **at the end** of the pulse (at $t_2$).
4. The differential current $\Delta i = i(t_2) - i(t_1)$ is plotted vs. applied potential.

**Why?** The faradaic current decays slowly ($t^{-1/2}$), while the charging current decays much faster (exponentially). Subtracting two closely timed samples eliminates most of the charging current.

### Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV)

1. A staircase of step $\Delta E_s$ is combined with a symmetric square pulse of amplitude $E_{sw}$.
2. Forward and reverse currents $i_f$ and $i_r$ are sampled at the end of each forward and reverse pulse.
3. Net current $\Delta i = i_f - i_r$ is plotted.
4. SWV is faster (full scan in seconds) and more sensitive than DPV.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biotechnology / Biosensors:** Detection of dopamine, ascorbic acid, glucose, DNA, neurotransmitters.
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Assay of drug molecules, study of redox behavior of APIs.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Trace heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As) in water by ASV.
- **Materials Science:** Characterization of new electrode materials, graphene, MXenes, conducting polymers.
- **Energy:** Evaluation of battery electrodes, supercapacitors, fuel cell catalysts.
- **Corrosion Science:** Passivation film studies, coating evaluation.
- **Catalysis:** Electron-transfer kinetics of molecular catalysts and enzymes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Randles–Sevcik Equation (Reversible CV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation assumes a **reversible** redox couple $O + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons R$ at a planar stationary electrode under semi-infinite linear diffusion, with both species soluble in the electrolyte.

### Step 1: Linear Potential Sweep as a Function of Time

The applied potential varies linearly with time:

$$E(t) = E_i - \nu t \quad \text{(for cathodic sweep)}$$

This converts the boundary condition at the electrode surface into a time-dependent Nernstian condition.

### Step 2: Surface Concentrations under Nernstian Equilibrium

Because the reaction is reversible, the surface concentrations obey Nernst equation at all times:

$$E(t) = E^{0'} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{C_O(0,t)}{C_R(0,t)}\right)$$

### Step 3: Dimensionless Variable Substitution

Introduce the dimensionless potential:

$$\theta = \dfrac{nF}{RT}\left(E(t) - E^{0'}\right) = \dfrac{nF}{RT}(E_i - \nu t - E^{0'})$$

and the dimensionless rate parameter:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{nF\nu t}{RT}$$

### Step 4: Solution of Fick's Second Law

The semi-infinite diffusion problem with a moving boundary (due to the time-varying potential) is solved by Laplace transformation. The current density at the electrode surface is given by the integral:

$$i(t) = nFAD_O^{1/2}\left(\dfrac{nF\nu}{RT}\right)^{1/2} C_O^{*} \cdot \chi(\sigma)$$

where $\chi(\sigma)$ is a dimensionless current function that depends on $\sigma$.

### Step 5: Finding the Maximum (Peak)

The function $\chi(\sigma)$ reaches a maximum at the peak position $\sigma_p = nF(E_p - E^{0'})/RT$, with maximum value $\chi(\sigma_p) = 0.4463$.

### Step 6: Substitution to Yield the Randles–Sevcik Equation

$$\boxed{\,i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\, n^{3/2}\, A\, D_O^{1/2}\, C_O^{*}\, \nu^{1/2}\,}$$

(At $T = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $i_p$ in A, $A$ in cm², $D_O$ in cm²/s, $C_O^{*}$ in mol/cm³, $\nu$ in V/s.)

### Step 7: Diagnostic Use

Since $i_p \propto \nu^{1/2}$, plotting $\log(i_p)$ vs. $\log(\nu)$ should yield a **straight line of slope 0.5** for a diffusion-controlled reversible system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Reversibility Criterion $\Delta E_p = 59/n$ mV

### Step 1: Cathodic Peak Position

From the Nernstian boundary condition and the peak condition $d\chi/d\sigma = 0$, the cathodic peak potential is:

$$E_{pc} = E^{0'} - \dfrac{0.0285}{n}\,\text{V} \approx E^{0'} - \dfrac{28.5}{n}\,\text{mV (at 25\,°C)}$$

### Step 2: Anodic Peak Position

By symmetry (the reverse sweep has the same mathematics, but the sweep direction is reversed):

$$E_{pa} = E^{0'} + \dfrac{0.0285}{n}\,\text{V} \approx E^{0'} + \dfrac{28.5}{n}\,\text{mV}$$

### Step 3: Subtract

$$\Delta E_p = E_{pa} - E_{pc} = \dfrac{57.0}{n}\,\text{mV} \approx \dfrac{59}{n}\,\text{mV at 25\,°C}$$

Thus, for a **one-electron reversible couple**, $\Delta E_p \approx 59$ mV. For a two-electron couple, $\Delta E_p \approx 29$ mV.

---

## 3.3 Nicholson–Shain Diagnostics for Reversibility

The dimensionless parameter $\psi$ is defined as:

$$\psi = \dfrac{k^{0}}{\sqrt{\pi D \nu n F / RT}}$$

where $D$ is taken as the geometric mean $D = \sqrt{D_O D_R}$.

| Regime | Criterion | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversible** | $\psi \geq 7$ | $\Delta E_p \approx 59/n$ mV, ratio $i_{pa}/i_{pc} = 1$ |
| **Quasi-reversible** | $7 > \psi > 10^{-3}$ | $\Delta E_p$ increases with $\nu$ |
| **Irreversible** | $\psi \leq 10^{-3}$ | No reverse peak observed |

By measuring $\Delta E_p$ experimentally and consulting the **Nicholson table** of $\psi$ vs. $\Delta E_p$, the heterogeneous rate constant $k^{0}$ can be calculated.

---

## 3.4 Surface-Confined Species (Laviron Analysis)

When a redox-active species is **immobilized** on the electrode surface (e.g., in a SAM or adsorbed film), the peak current follows a linear dependence on $\nu$ instead of $\nu^{1/2}$:

$$\boxed{\,i_p = \dfrac{n^{2}F^{2}}{4RT}\,A\,\Gamma\,\nu\,}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the surface coverage in mol/cm².

### Diagnostic Plot

Plot $\log(i_p)$ vs. $\log(\nu)$:

- **Slope = 0.5** → diffusion-controlled (species in solution)
- **Slope = 1.0** → surface-confined (adsorbed species)

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Pulse Voltammetric Peak Current

For DPV, the peak current is derived by solving the diffusion equation for a series of potential steps with superimposed pulses. The result is:

$$i_{p,DPV} = \dfrac{n^{2}F^{2}}{4RT}\,A\,\Gamma\,\Delta E\, \dfrac{D^{1/2}}{\pi^{1/2}t_p^{1/2}} + \text{(diffusion term)}$$

where $\Delta E$ is the pulse amplitude and $t_p$ is the pulse duration. The peak height increases with decreasing pulse width and increasing amplitude.

For SWV, peak current is roughly **4× higher** than DPV at comparable conditions, with a peak width of $\sim 90/n$ mV, giving excellent resolution of closely spaced peaks.

---

## 3.6 Stripping Voltammetry — Concentration Enhancement

If the deposition time is $t_d$, the volume of solution effectively sampled is $V_{dep}$ and the electrode area is $A$:

$$C_{surface} = \dfrac{C_{bulk}\, V_{dep}}{A \cdot \delta_{film}}$$

The enrichment factor is typically $10^{2}$ to $10^{3}$, accounting for the million-fold sensitivity enhancement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cyclic Voltammetry Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PG[Potentiostat Function Generator] --> WE[Working Electrode]
    WE --> CE[Counter Electrode Auxiliary]
    PG --> RE[Reference Electrode Saturated Calomel or Ag AgCl]
    WE -->|Current i t| AM[Current Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    AM --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System Computer]
    DAQ --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing Peak Detection]
    DSP --> DISP[Display Cyclic Voltammogram i vs E]
    RE -->|High impedance feedback| PG
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pulse Voltammetry Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXC[Excitation Waveform Generator]
        RAMP[Linear Potential Ramp]
        PULSE[Pulse Superimposer DPV or SWV]
        RAMP --> PULSE
    end
    EXC --> CELL[Electrochemical Cell Three Electrode]
    CELL -->|Faradaic + Charging| IAMP[Current to Voltage Converter]
    IAMP --> SAMP[Sampler Two Points per Pulse]
    SAMP --> DIFF[Differential Amplifier Subtraction]
    DIFF --> FILT[Low Pass Filter]
    FILT --> REC[Recorder Display i vs E]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Interpreting a Cyclic Voltammogram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Record Cyclic Voltammogram] --> B{Is there a reverse peak?}
    B -- No --> C[Irreversible System No back electron transfer]
    B -- Yes --> D{Measured Delta E_p in mV}
    D -- Equals 59 over n --> E[Reversible System Use Randles Sevcik]
    D -- Greater than 59 over n and increases with nu --> F[Quasi reversible Use Nicholson Shain]
    D -- Less than 59 over n --> G[Possibly multi electron or adsorption]
    E --> H{Plot log i_p vs log nu}
    F --> H
    H -- Slope 0.5 --> I[Diffusion controlled Species in solution]
    H -- Slope 1.0 --> J[Surface confined Species adsorbed on electrode]
    I --> K[Calculate D using Randles Sevcik]
    I --> L[Calculate k0 using Nicholson Shain]
    J --> M[Calculate surface coverage Gamma using Laviron]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Study of Surfaces (Modified Electrodes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BARE[Bare Electrode Glassy Carbon Au Pt] --> MOD[Surface Modification]
    MOD --> SAM[Self Assembled Monolayer Thiol on Au]
    MOD --> POLY[Conducting Polymer Polyaniline PEDOT]
    MOD --> NANO[Nanomaterial Graphene MXene CNT]
    MOD --> BIO[Biomolecule Enzyme Antibody DNA]
    SAM --> CV1[Run CV in redox probe]
    POLY --> CV1
    NANO --> CV1
    BIO --> CV1
    CV1 --> ANA[Analyze Peak Currents and Potentials]
    ANA --> KIN[Determine Electron Transfer Kinetics]
    ANA --> SURF[Determine Surface Coverage]
    ANA --> APP[Determine Sensor Response to Analyte]
```

---

## 4.5 Stripping Voltammetry Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Solution with Trace Metal Ions] --> B[Deposition Step Apply E_dep for time t_d]
    B --> C[Metal Ions Reduced and Accumulated on Electrode Surface]
    C --> D[Equilibration Period]
    D --> E[Stripping Scan Linear or Pulse]
    E --> F[Record Stripping Peak Current i_p]
    F --> G{Calibrate with Standards}
    G --> H[Determine Concentration using i_p proportional to C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define cyclic voltammetry. Mention any two diagnostic criteria used to identify a reversible redox system from a cyclic voltammogram.**

**Model Answer:**

**Cyclic voltammetry (CV)** is an electroanalytical technique in which the working electrode potential is swept linearly with time between two switching potentials using a triangular waveform, and the resulting current is recorded as a function of applied potential. It is used to study redox behavior of chemical and biological species.

**Diagnostic criteria for reversibility:**
1. **Peak separation** $\Delta E_p = E_{pa} - E_{pc} \approx 59/n$ mV at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is independent of scan rate $\nu$.
2. **Peak current ratio** $i_{pa}/i_{pc} \approx 1$ (unity) for a one-electron reversible couple.
3. **Formal potential** $E^{0'} = (E_{pa} + E_{pc})/2$ is independent of $\nu$.
4. Peak current $i_p \propto \nu^{1/2}$.

(Any two criteria earn full credit.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is differential pulse voltammetry? Why is it more sensitive than linear sweep voltammetry?**

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)** is a pulse voltammetric technique in which small constant-amplitude potential pulses (typically 10–100 mV) are superimposed on a linear potential ramp. The current is sampled twice per pulse — once before the pulse and once at the end — and the difference $\Delta i = i(t_2) - i(t_1)$ is plotted versus applied potential.

**Reasons for higher sensitivity than LSV:**
1. **Charging current elimination:** The capacitive (non-faradaic) current decays exponentially with time after each pulse, while the faradaic current decays as $t^{-1/2}$ (more slowly). Sampling at two closely spaced time points effectively subtracts the charging current.
2. **Improved signal-to-noise ratio:** The differential measurement rejects baseline drift and DC offsets.
3. **Lower detection limits:** Typically **10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁸ M** in DPV vs. **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶ M** in LSV.
4. **Sharp, well-defined peaks:** Better resolution of closely spaced redox couples.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often write the Randles–Sevcik equation **without specifying the temperature** (must be 25 °C) and units. Marks are deducted.
> - Confusing **diffusion-controlled** ($i_p \propto \nu^{1/2}$) with **surface-controlled** ($i_p \propto \nu$) systems. Know the difference and the diagnostic plots.
> - In Nicholson–Shain problems, students forget to take the geometric mean $D = \sqrt{D_O D_R}$. Always mention this assumption.
> - For stripping voltammetry, students neglect to mention the **deposition time** $t_d$, which is critical for sensitivity calculations.
> - Units must be consistent: $A$ in cm², $D$ in cm²/s, $C$ in mol/cm³, $i$ in A. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit errors.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**(a)** Derive the **Randles–Sevcik equation** for a reversible cyclic voltammetric peak current. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the diagnostic criteria used to distinguish between **reversible, quasi-reversible, and irreversible** electron-transfer processes from a cyclic voltammogram. Include the role of the **Nicholson–Shain** working curve. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Randles–Sevcik Derivation** (refer to detailed derivation in Section 3.1):

**Assumptions:**
- Planar, stationary working electrode
- Semi-infinite linear diffusion
- Excess supporting electrolyte (eliminates migration)
- Nernstian equilibrium at electrode surface (reversible kinetics)
- $D_O \approx D_R = D$
- Single electron-transfer step (or reversible multi-electron)
- No coupled chemical reactions
- Temperature $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$

The current density at the electrode is given by:

$$i(t) = nFAD^{1/2}\left(\dfrac{nF\nu}{RT}\right)^{1/2}C_O^{*} \chi(\sigma)$$

where $\chi(\sigma)$ reaches a maximum of 0.4463 at $\sigma_p = nF(E_p - E^{0'})/RT = -0.854$ (for cathodic peak).

Substituting constants at $T = 298$ K:

$$\boxed{\,i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\,n^{3/2}\,A\,D^{1/2}\,C_O^{*}\,\nu^{1/2}\,}$$

**(b) Diagnostic Criteria:**

| Property | Reversible | Quasi-reversible | Irreversible |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta E_p$ | $59/n$ mV | $> 59/n$ mV, increases with $\nu$ | No reverse peak |
| $i_{pa}/i_{pc}$ | $\approx 1$ | $< 1$ at high $\nu$ | 0 |
| $E^{0'}$ vs. $\nu$ | Independent | Shifts with $\nu$ | Shifts |
| $i_p$ vs. $\nu^{1/2}$ | Linear through origin | Linear through origin | Linear through origin |
| $i_p$ vs. $\nu$ | Linear (with $\nu^{1/2}$) | — | Linear (with $\nu^{1/2}$) |

**Nicholson–Shain Working Curve:** The dimensionless parameter $\psi = k^{0}/\sqrt{\pi D \nu nF/RT}$ is computed from the experimental $\Delta E_p$ using a published table. The heterogeneous rate constant $k^{0}$ is then extracted.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the principle and instrumentation of **Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV)**. Compare its advantages with **Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the application of cyclic voltammetry in **(i)** study of adsorption on electrode surfaces and **(ii)** determination of heterogeneous electron-transfer rate constant $k^{0}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Square Wave Voltammetry:**

**Waveform:** SWV combines a staircase potential of step height $\Delta E_s$ with a symmetric square pulse of amplitude $E_{sw}$ applied for the last half of each step. The pulse alternates in sign (+ and –) between successive steps.

**Sampling:** The forward current $i_f$ is measured at the end of the forward pulse, and the reverse current $i_r$ at the end of the reverse pulse. The net current $\Delta i = i_f - i_r$ is plotted vs. the staircase potential.

**Advantages over DPV:**
- **Speed:** Full scan takes only a few seconds (vs. minutes for DPV), enabling study of transient systems.
- **Sensitivity:** Peak current is roughly 4× larger; detection limits in sub-ppb range.
- **Reversibility diagnostics:** Forward and reverse components can be plotted separately, providing kinetic information.
- **Resolution:** Peak width is small ($\sim 90/n$ mV).
- **Rejection of charging current** is more efficient due to symmetrical pulse waveform.

**(b) Applications of CV:**

**(i) Study of Adsorption:** When a redox species is adsorbed on the electrode surface, the peak current scales **linearly** with scan rate $\nu$ (not $\nu^{1/2}$):

$$i_p = \dfrac{n^{2}F^{2}}{4RT}\,A\,\Gamma\,\nu$$

A plot of $\log i_p$ vs. $\log \nu$ with **slope = 1.0** confirms adsorption. From the slope, the surface coverage $\Gamma$ (mol/cm²) is calculated. Laviron's theory also gives the apparent rate constant of the surface-confined electron transfer from the variation of $E_p$ with $\nu$.

**(ii) Determination of $k^{0}$:**
- Record CV at multiple scan rates.
- Measure $\Delta E_p$ at each $\nu$.
- Use the Nicholson–Shain working curve to find $\psi$.
- Compute $k^{0}$:

$$k^{0} = \psi\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi D \nu n F}{RT}}$$

Alternative: Use the **Butler–Volmer** formalism with a fit of the full voltammogram. For reversible cases ($\Delta E_p \approx 59/n$ mV), the system is too fast for accurate $k^{0}$ measurement by CV; in such cases, use **impedance spectroscopy** or **ultramicroelectrodes** with high scan rates.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CV is a *screening* technique** — fast, qualitative, gives redox potential, reversibility, and number of electrons.
- **Pulse voltammetry (DPV, SWV)** is used when *quantitative trace analysis* is needed — 100–1000× more sensitive.
- **Randles–Sevcik** $i_p = 2.69 \times 10^{5} n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C^{*} \nu^{1/2}$ is the **most tested equation**.
- $\Delta E_p = 59/n$ mV is the hallmark of a **reversible** couple at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- $i_p \propto \nu^{1/2}$ ⇒ **diffusion control**; $i_p \propto \nu$ ⇒ **surface adsorption**.
- **Nicholson–Shain working curve** gives $k^{0}$ from $\Delta E_p$ vs. $\nu$.
- **Stripping voltammetry** gives ppt-level detection for heavy metals by **preconcentration**.
- **Laviron equation** for adsorbed species: $i_p = (n^2 F^2 / 4RT)\,A\,\Gamma\,\nu$.
- **SWV is faster and more sensitive than DPV**; both suppress charging current.
- **Study of surfaces** with modified electrodes (SAMs, polymers, nanomaterials, biomolecules) uses CV with redox probes like $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{3-/4-}$ to extract $\Gamma$ and $k^{0}$.
- Always mention **temperature ($25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$), units (A, cm², cm²/s, mol/cm³, V/s), and supporting electrolyte excess** for full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Probe Microscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scanning Probe Microscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Scanning Probe Microscopes (SPM) — Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Scanning Probe Microscopes (SPM) constitute a class of high-resolution surface analysis instruments that image the topography and properties of a sample surface by raster-scanning a sharp physical probe (typically terminating in a single atom or a sharp tip) across the specimen while monitoring a position-dependent physical interaction (such as tunneling current, interatomic force, capacitance, or magnetic interaction) between the tip and the sample surface.*

The resolution of SPMs is **not limited by the diffraction of light** (which caps optical microscopy at ~200 nm) but by the **radius of curvature of the probe tip** and the **decay length of the physical interaction** being measured. This is why SPMs can routinely achieve **sub-nanometer (atomic-scale) resolution**, and in some cases true **atomic resolution (~0.1 nm lateral, ~0.01 nm vertical)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of SPM:** The Scanning Probe Microscopy family was born in **1981** with the invention of the Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) by **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer** at IBM Zürich (Nobel Prize in Physics, 1986). STM was followed in 1986 by the Atomic Force Microscope (AFM), also by Binnig (with Quate and Gerber).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Feeling in the Dark"

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a dark room** and asked to "read" the shape of a tiny sculpted object (a raised letter "A") placed on a table. You cannot use your eyes. The only way to know its shape is to drag your **fingertip** across it and feel the bumps, edges, and slopes.

- Your **fingertip** is the **probe tip**.
- Your **brain's sense of touch** is the **feedback detector** (current, force, etc.).
- Your **slow hand motion in a grid pattern** is the **raster scan**.
- The **map of "high" and "low" points** your finger traces is the **topographic image**.

This is precisely how a Scanning Probe Microscope works — it "feels" the surface atom-by-atom (or molecule-by-molecule) instead of using light to "see" it.

### Classification of Scanning Probe Microscopes

| Family Member | Physical Interaction Probed | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **STM** — Scanning Tunneling Microscope | Quantum tunneling current | Atomic (~0.1 nm lateral) |
| **AFM** — Atomic Force Microscope | Interatomic van der Waals / repulsive force | Sub-nm (~0.2 nm lateral) |
| **MFM** — Magnetic Force Microscope | Magnetic dipole-dipole force | ~10–50 nm |
| **EFM** — Electrostatic Force Microscope | Electrostatic (Coulomb) force | ~10–50 nm |
| **SNOM/NSOM** — Scanning Near-field Optical Microscope | Evanescent optical field | ~20–50 nm (breaks diffraction limit) |
| **KPFM** — Kelvin Probe Force Microscope | Contact potential difference | ~10 nm |
| **SCM** — Scanning Capacitance Microscope | Capacitance change | ~10 nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two principal instruments emphasized in Module 4 are the **STM** and the **AFM**, including their modes of operation, biological applications, advantages, and limitations. Mastery of both is **mandatory** for ESE and internal assessments.

### Why SPM is Revolutionary for Biotechnology

Traditional electron microscopy (SEM/TEM) requires **vacuum, dehydration, and heavy metal staining** — a destructive, non-physiological workflow that kills live biological specimens. SPM, particularly **AFM**, can image samples under **ambient air, liquid (physiological buffer), and even at physiological temperatures**, opening the door to:

- Imaging of **live cells, bacteria, viruses**.
- Real-time observation of **biomolecular folding/unfolding** (e.g., single-protein force spectroscopy).
- Mapping of **cell membrane stiffness** (mechanobiology).
- Visualization of **DNA, proteins, lipid bilayers** at nanometer scale.

The **physical constants** governing SPM physics are universally consistent:

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Electron charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Vacuum permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Typical tunneling current (STM):** **0.1–10 nA**
- **Typical tunneling bias voltage (STM):** **0.01–3 V**
- **Typical tip-sample distance (STM):** **0.3–1.0 nm**
- **Typical AFM cantilever force constant:** **0.01–100 N/m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Working Principle & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) — Deep Theory

### Principle: Quantum Mechanical Tunneling

The STM relies on the **quantum mechanical tunneling effect**. Classically, an electron bound inside a metal cannot escape unless given enough energy to overcome the work function barrier. Quantum mechanically, however, the electron's **wavefunction** does not terminate abruptly at the metal surface — it decays exponentially into the vacuum (or gap). If a second conductor is brought within a few angstroms, the wavefunctions **overlap**, and electrons "tunnel" through the classically forbidden gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Work function ($\phi$):** The minimum energy required to liberate an electron from a solid surface into vacuum. For most clean metals, $\phi \approx 4\text{–}5$ eV.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. A **bias voltage $V_b$** (typically 10 mV – 3 V) is applied between the conducting tip (often a single-atom-sharp tungsten or Pt–Ir wire) and the conducting sample.
2. When the tip is brought to within ~0.3–1.0 nm, a **tunneling current $I_t$** flows.
3. A **piezoelectric tube scanner** (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) raster-scans the tip across the surface in $x$ and $y$ (or scans the sample).
4. The current $I_t$ is measured and fed to a **feedback controller** that adjusts the $z$-piezo (height) to keep $I_t$ **constant** (constant-current mode) — or the height is kept constant and the current is recorded (constant-height mode).
5. The set of $z$-values (or current values) constitutes the **topographic image**.

### Why the current is so sensitive to distance

The tunneling current depends **exponentially** on the gap width $d$:

$$I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$$

where:
- $I_t$ = tunneling current (A)
- $V_b$ = applied bias voltage (V)
- $d$ = tip–sample separation (Å or nm)
- $\phi$ = average work function (eV)
- $k$ = a constant, $k = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m_e}}{\hbar} \approx 0.512$ Å$^{-1}$·eV$^{-1/2}$ (when $\phi$ in eV, $d$ in Å)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** A change in $d$ of just **0.1 nm** changes $I_t$ by roughly an **order of magnitude (~10×)**. This extreme sensitivity is the *physical origin* of STM's atomic resolution.

## 2.2 The Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) — Deep Theory

### Principle: Interatomic Force Detection

The AFM measures the **force** between a sharp tip (mounted on the free end of a flexible microfabricated cantilever) and the sample surface. The force originates from **interatomic potentials** — primarily **repulsive Pauli exclusion forces** at very short range (< 0.5 nm), **van der Waals attractive forces** at intermediate range (0.5–10 nm), and various long-range forces (capillary, electrostatic, magnetic, chemical) at > 10 nm.

The most commonly used interatomic approximation is the **Lennard-Jones Potential**:

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

where:
- $r$ = instantaneous interatomic distance (m)
- $\varepsilon$ = depth of the potential well (J) — a characteristic energy
- $\sigma$ = distance at which $U(r) = 0$ (m)

The **force** is the negative gradient:

$$F(r) = -\frac{dU}{dr} = 24\varepsilon \left[\frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$$

The force is **strongly repulsive** for $r < \sigma$ (Pauli exclusion) and **weakly attractive** in the van der Waals tail.

### Cantilever Mechanics — Hooke's Law for AFM

The cantilever behaves as a Hookean spring. The **deflection $z$** of the cantilever end is related to the force $F$ via:

$$F = k_c \cdot z$$

where $k_c$ is the cantilever spring constant (N/m). Typical $k_c$ ranges:

- **Contact mode:** $k_c \approx 0.01\text{–}1$ N/m (soft cantilevers)
- **Tapping mode:** $k_c \approx 1\text{–}100$ N/m (stiffer cantilevers)

The **deflection** is usually measured by **optical beam deflection**: a laser bounces off the back of the cantilever onto a position-sensitive photodetector (PSPD / quadrant photodiode).

### AFM Operating Modes — Detailed Logic

#### (i) Contact Mode (Static Mode)
- Tip is in **permanent repulsive contact** with the surface.
- Force held **constant** (feedback adjusts $z$).
- Lateral (friction/shear) forces may damage soft biological samples.
- **Best for:** Hard, flat samples (mica, silicon, some crystals).

#### (ii) Non-Contact Mode (Dynamic Mode)
- Cantilever oscillates near its **resonant frequency** (~ 100–500 kHz) with small amplitude (~1–5 nm).
- Tip–sample distance is on the order of **5–15 nm** (in the attractive regime).
- Van der Waals / electrostatic forces shift the **resonant frequency** (frequency modulation) or **amplitude** (amplitude modulation).
- **Best for:** Delicate samples, but tip contamination is a major issue in ambient conditions.

#### (iii) Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact Mode)
- Cantilever oscillates with **larger amplitude** (typically 20–100 nm) such that the tip **touches** the surface once per oscillation cycle.
- Eliminates lateral shear force → **ideal for soft biological samples** (cells, proteins, DNA).
- **Best for:** Biological imaging in air or liquid — the **default choice** in biotechnology.

#### (iv) Force Spectroscopy Mode
- The tip is held stationary in $x, y$, while the $z$-piezo extends and retracts.
- The **force–distance curve** is recorded, yielding nanomechanical information: adhesion, elasticity (Young's modulus via Hertz model), unfolding forces of single proteins, receptor–ligand binding forces.
- **Biotech relevance:** Single-molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS) on proteins, DNA unzipping, antigen–antibody unbinding.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Engineering / Biological Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_t \propto V_b \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$ | $I_t$ (A), $V_b$ (V), $d$ (Å), $\phi$ (eV) | STM signal; basis of exponential z-sensitivity |
| 2 | $k = \sqrt{2m_e}/\hbar \approx 0.512$ Å$^{-1}$·eV$^{-1/2}$ | Constants only | Numerical evaluation of STM decay |
| 3 | $\Delta I/I_t \approx -2k\sqrt{\phi}\,\Delta d$ | Dimensionless ratio | Quick estimate of current change per 0.1 nm step |
| 4 | $F = k_c \cdot z$ | $F$ (N), $k_c$ (N/m), $z$ (m) | Cantilever deflection → force in AFM |
| 5 | $U(r) = 4\varepsilon\!\left[\left(\sigma/r\right)^{12} - \left(\sigma/r\right)^{6}\right]$ | $U$ (J), $r$ (m) | Interatomic potential (Lennard-Jones) |
| 6 | $F(r) = -dU/dr$ | $F$ (N) | Force derived from any potential |
| 7 | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{k_c/m^*}$ | $\omega_0$ (rad/s), $m^*$ (kg) | Cantilever resonant frequency |
| 8 | $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_0}{2k_c}\dfrac{\partial F_{ts}}{\partial z}$ | $\Delta f$ (Hz) | Frequency shift in FM-AFM (non-contact) |
| 9 | $E^* = \dfrac{3}{4}F(1-\nu^2)/\sqrt{R\,\delta^3}$ (Hertz) | $E^*$ (Pa), $F$ (N), $R$ (m), $\delta$ (m) | Young's modulus from nanoindentation |
| 10 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert z_i - \bar{z}\vert$ | $R_a$ (nm) | Average surface roughness from SPM image |
| 11 | $f_{\text{scan}} = L/v$ | $L$ (m), $v$ (m/s) | Time to acquire one scan line |
| 12 | Piezo strain: $\Delta L = d_{31}\,V\,L/t$ | $d_{31}$ (m/V), $V$ (V), $L,t$ (m) | Piezo scanner displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Piping Notice:** All vertical bars denoting magnitude (e.g., $\vert z_i - \bar{z}\vert$, $\vert dU/dr\vert$) are rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` macro — this is the KTU-compliant way to avoid breaking the surrounding markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Biotechnology

| Field | SPM Application |
|---|---|
| **Structural biology** | Imaging viruses, membrane proteins, amyloid fibrils |
| **Single-molecule biophysics** | Force spectroscopy of protein folding, DNA mechanics |
| **Drug discovery** | Measuring receptor–ligand unbinding forces |
| **Cell mechanics / mechanobiology** | Mapping Young's modulus of cancer vs. normal cells |
| **Biosensing** | AFM tips functionalized with antibodies → molecular recognition imaging |
| **Nanobiomaterials** | Characterizing scaffolds, hydrogels, nanofibers |
| **Microbiology** | Live-cell imaging of bacteria and biofilms under buffer |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the STM Tunneling Current Sensitivity

### Starting Postulate — Square-Well Approximation of the Tunneling Barrier

We approximate the potential barrier between tip and sample as a 1-D square well of width $d$ and height $\phi$ (the average work function). An electron of mass $m_e$ and energy $E < \phi$ approaches from the left.

### Step 1 — Schrödinger Equation Inside the Barrier

Inside the classically forbidden region, the time-independent Schrödinger equation is:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + \phi\,\psi = E\,\psi$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Identify the Decay Constant

Move the energy term to the left:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = (E - \phi)\,\psi$$

Since $E < \phi$, $(E - \phi) < 0$. Define the **decay constant** $\kappa$:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e(\phi - E)}}{\hbar}$$

### Step 3 — Wavefunction Inside the Barrier

Substituting, the wavefunction inside the barrier is:

$$\psi(z) = A\,e^{-\kappa z} + B\,e^{+\kappa z}$$

For a barrier that is thick enough that the wave cannot re-emerge with significant amplitude at the far side, we keep only the decaying branch:

$$\psi(z) \approx A\,e^{-\kappa z}$$

> **Logic note:** The growing branch $e^{+\kappa z}$ is discarded because it would diverge as $z \to \infty$ and has no physical meaning in a thick barrier.

### Step 4 — Transmission Probability

The probability of finding the electron past the barrier decays as $\vert\psi(d)\vert^2$. The **transmission coefficient** is therefore:

$$T \propto \vert\psi(d)\vert^2 = \vert A\vert^2 e^{-2\kappa d}$$

With $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e(\phi - E)}/\hbar$ and approximating $E \ll \phi$, take $\phi - E \approx \phi$:

$$T \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\sqrt{2m_e\phi}}{\hbar}\, d\right)$$

### Step 5 — Tunneling Current

The current is proportional to the transmission probability and to the applied bias $V_b$ (which raises one Fermi sea above the other, populating empty states for tunneling):

$$I_t = C\,V_b \exp\!\left(-\frac{2\sqrt{2m_e\phi}}{\hbar}\, d\right)$$

Combining constants into a single empirical coefficient, this matches the **KTU formula**:

$$I_t \propto V_b \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right),\quad k = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e}}{\hbar}$$

### Step 6 — Sensitivity Calculation (Numerical Demonstration)

Use the standard numerical value $k \approx 0.512$ Å$^{-1}$·eV$^{-1/2}$ and $\phi \approx 4.5$ eV (typical Pt–Ir tip on graphite):

$$k\sqrt{\phi} = 0.512 \times \sqrt{4.5} = 0.512 \times 2.121 = 1.086 \text{ Å}^{-1}$$

Now compute the ratio of currents for two gaps differing by $\Delta d = 0.1$ nm = 1 Å:

$$\frac{I_t(d+\Delta d)}{I_t(d)} = \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\,\Delta d\right) = \exp(-1.086 \times 1) = e^{-1.086} \approx 0.338$$

Therefore, an outward 0.1 nm movement of the tip reduces the current by a factor of ~3 (i.e., the current changes by ~66%). This demonstrates why STM resolution is **truly atomic**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Cantilever Resonant Frequency in AFM

### Starting Postulate — Simple Harmonic Oscillator

A microfabricated cantilever of effective mass $m^*$ and spring constant $k_c$ behaves as a damped harmonic oscillator. Its undamped resonant angular frequency is:

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{k_c}{m^*}}$$

### Step 1 — Effective Mass Approximation

For a uniform rectangular cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, and density $\rho$:

$$m^* \approx 0.24\,\rho\,L\,w\,t = 0.24\,m_{\text{total}}$$

(The factor 0.24 arises from the modal shape of the first flexural mode.)

### Step 2 — Spring Constant of a Rectangular Cantilever

From Euler–Bernoulli beam theory:

$$k_c = \frac{E\,w\,t^3}{4\,L^3}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus of silicon (~169 GPa for $\langle 100\rangle$ Si).

### Step 3 — Substitute into Resonant Frequency

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{E w t^3 / 4L^3}{0.24 \rho L w t}} = \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{0.96\,\rho\,L^4}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

Take a typical silicon cantilever: $L = 200$ µm, $w = 40$ µm, $t = 2$ µm, $E = 169$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³.

First compute $k_c$:

$$k_c = \frac{(169 \times 10^9)(40 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

Numerator:

$$(169 \times 10^9)(40 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18}) = 169 \times 10^9 \times 320 \times 10^{-24} = 5.408 \times 10^{-11}$$

Denominator:

$$4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$$

Thus:

$$k_c = \frac{5.408 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.69 \times 10^{3} \approx 1.7 \text{ N/m}$$

Then effective mass:

$$m^* = 0.24 \times 2330 \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$m^* = 0.24 \times 2330 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-14} = 0.24 \times 3.728 \times 10^{-11} = 8.95 \times 10^{-12} \text{ kg}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1.69}{8.95 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{1.89 \times 10^{11}} \approx 4.34 \times 10^{5} \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{4.34 \times 10^5}{2\pi} \approx 6.91 \times 10^{4} \text{ Hz} \approx 69 \text{ kHz}$$

This is a typical resonant frequency for a soft contact-mode cantilever. Stiffer tapping-mode cantilevers resonate in the **100–500 kHz** range.

## 3.3 Hertzian Contact Mechanics for Cell Stiffness (AFM Nanoindentation)

### Setup

A spherical AFM tip of radius $R$ is indented into a soft biological sample (e.g., a cell) by a force $F$, producing an indentation depth $\delta$.

### Hertz Equation (Sphere on Flat, No Adhesion)

$$F = \frac{4}{3}\,E^*\,R^{1/2}\,\delta^{3/2}$$

where the **reduced modulus** is:

$$\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1-\nu_{\text{tip}}^2}{E_{\text{tip}}} + \frac{1-\nu_{\text{sample}}^2}{E_{\text{sample}}}$$

For a stiff silicon tip ($E_{\text{tip}} \approx 169$ GPa) indenting a soft biological sample ($E_{\text{sample}} \approx$ 1–100 kPa, $\nu_{\text{sample}} \approx 0.5$), the tip is effectively rigid:

$$E^* \approx \frac{E_{\text{sample}}}{1-\nu_{\text{sample}}^2}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 5 µm-radius AFM tip indents a cell to $\delta = 200$ nm with $F = 1$ nN.

Assume $\nu = 0.5$ so $1 - \nu^2 = 0.75$. Solve for $E^*$:

$$E^* = \frac{3F}{4R^{1/2}\,\delta^{3/2}} = \frac{3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^{1/2} \times (200 \times 10^{-9})^{3/2}}$$

Compute denominator pieces:

$$R^{1/2} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.236 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\delta^{3/2} = (200 \times 10^{-9})^{3/2} = (2 \times 10^{-7})^{1.5} = 2.828 \times 10^{-10.5}$$

Recompute cleanly:

$$\delta^{3/2} = (200 \times 10^{-9})^{1.5} = (2 \times 10^{-7})^{1.5}$$

Take the natural log:

$$\ln\delta^{3/2} = 1.5 \ln(2 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.5(\ln 2 + \ln 10^{-7}) = 1.5(0.6931 - 16.1181) = 1.5(-15.4250) = -23.1375$$

So $\delta^{3/2} = e^{-23.1375} = 9.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m$^{1.5}$ (≈ $9 \times 10^{-11}$ m$^{3/2}$).

Denominator:

$$4 \times 2.236 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.0 \times 10^{-11} = 8.05 \times 10^{-13}$$

Therefore:

$$E^* = \frac{3 \times 10^{-9}}{8.05 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 3727 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.7 \text{ kPa}$$

Then the cell's Young's modulus:

$$E_{\text{sample}} = E^* (1 - \nu^2) \approx 3727 \times 0.75 \approx 2.8 \text{ kPa}$$

This value is consistent with the typical stiffness of **mammalian cells (~1–10 kPa)**, and AFM nanoindentation is now a standard method in **mechanobiology** for distinguishing healthy from cancerous or diseased cells based on their stiffness.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic SPM System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the modular interactions of an SPM instrument. It deliberately uses **block-level functional architecture** (as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol) instead of attempting a literal physical drawing of the microscope head.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Sample Stage] --> TS[Tip Sample Interaction Zone]
    TP[Sharp Probe Tip] --> TS
    TS --> SIG[Interaction Signal Current Force Capacitance]
    SIG --> AMP[Low Noise Preamplifier Transimpedance]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> FB[Feedback Controller PID Loop]
    FB --> PIEZO[Z Piezo Scanner Stack]
    PIEZO --> TS
    FB --> IMG[Image Reconstruction Computer]
    ADC --> IMG
    XYS[X Y Piezo Scanner] --> SP
    IMG --> DISP[Topographic Map Display]
    IMG --> ANA[Quantitative Analysis Roughness Modulus Height]
    LAS[Laser Source] --> CANT[Cantilever Back Side]
    CANT --> PSD[Position Sensitive Photodetector]
    PSD --> AMP
```

### Architecture Explanation

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| **Sample Stage** | Holds the specimen; moved by $XY$ piezo scanner. |
| **Tip–Sample Interaction Zone** | The "heart" where physical interaction occurs. |
| **Interaction Signal** | The measurable output: tunneling current (STM), cantilever deflection (AFM), etc. |
| **Low-Noise Preamplifier** | Amplifies a tiny current (~nA) or a tiny voltage (µV) to a usable level. |
| **ADC** | Digitizes the analog signal for computer processing. |
| **Feedback Controller (PID)** | The "brain" — continuously adjusts $z$ to maintain the chosen setpoint. |
| **Piezo Scanner Stack** | PZT ceramic transducers that produce sub-Å displacements under applied voltage. |
| **Image Reconstruction Computer** | Synchronizes position with signal to render the 2-D / 3-D image. |
| **Laser + Cantilever + PSD** | Optical beam deflection readout — the most common AFM force sensor. |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow of an AFM Operating in Tapping Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRIVE[Dither Piezo Actuator] --> CANT2[Microfabricated Cantilever]
    CANT2 --> OSC[Resonant Oscillation 100 to 500 kHz]
    OSC --> TAP[Intermittent Tip Sample Contact]
    TAP --> AMP2[Amplitude Change]
    AMP2 --> SET[Compare to Setpoint Amplitude]
    SET --> ERR[Error Signal]
    ERR --> PID2[PID Controller]
    PID2 --> ZPIEZO[Z Piezo Height Adjustment]
    ZPIEZO --> TAP
    PID2 --> TRACE[Trace Recording z vs xy]
    TRACE --> TOPO[Topographic Image]
```

### Operating Logic of Tapping Mode (Textual Walk-Through)

1. The dither piezo drives the cantilever near its resonant frequency.
2. The oscillating tip "taps" the surface once per cycle.
3. Surface features **damp** the oscillation amplitude — taller features reduce amplitude more.
4. The amplitude is compared to a chosen **setpoint** (typically ~80–90% of free amplitude).
5. The **error signal** drives the PID controller.
6. The PID adjusts the $z$-piezo to restore amplitude.
7. The $z$-displacement is plotted as a function of $(x, y)$ → topographic image.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: STM vs. AFM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STM
        STM_TIP[Sharp Metallic Tip Pt Ir or W] --> STM_GAP[Vacuum Gap 0.3 to 1.0 nm]
        STM_GAP --> STM_CURR[Tunneling Current 0.1 to 10 nA]
        STM_CURR --> STM_FB[Feedback Maintains Constant Current]
    end

    subgraph AFM
        AFM_TIP[Sharp Si or Si3N4 Tip on Cantilever] --> AFM_FORCE[Interatomic Force 10 to 12 to 10 to 6 N]
        AFM_FORCE --> AFM_LASER[Laser Beam Deflection]
        AFM_LASER --> AFM_PSD[Position Sensitive Detector]
        AFM_PSD --> AFM_FB[Feedback Maintains Constant Force or Amplitude]
    end

    STM_FB --> STM_IMG[Topographic Image]
    AFM_FB --> AFM_IMG[Topographic Image]
```

### Side-by-Side Logical Comparison

| Aspect | STM Block | AFM Block |
|---|---|---|
| Tip | Atomically sharp metal | Sharp Si/Si$_3$N$_4$ on cantilever |
| Signal | Tunneling current | Force (cantilever deflection) |
| Sample requirement | **Must be conductive** | Works on insulators & conductors |
| Environment | Usually UHV or inert | Air, vacuum, **or liquid** |
| Readout | Direct current | Optical beam deflection |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** *Define the term "tunneling current" as it applies to a Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM). Why is this current extremely sensitive to tip–sample separation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The tunneling current is the net electric current that flows between two conductors (tip and sample) separated by a thin insulating gap (~0.3–1.0 nm) due to the **quantum mechanical tunneling** of electrons through the classically forbidden barrier.
>
> **Why extremely sensitive (2 marks):** The tunneling current depends **exponentially** on the gap width $d$:
> $$I_t \propto V_b \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$$
> with $k\sqrt{\phi} \approx 1.0$ Å$^{-1}$. Therefore, every 0.1 nm change in $d$ produces approximately an order-of-magnitude change in $I_t$. This exponential sensitivity is the physical origin of STM's **atomic-scale resolution**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *List any three operating modes of the Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) and state one biotechnological application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode (1 mark each) | Application (½ mark each) |
|---|---|
| **Contact mode** | Imaging of relatively hard biological samples such as DNA deposited on mica. |
| **Tapping (intermittent contact) mode** | High-resolution imaging of live cells, bacteria, and viruses in physiological buffer without damaging them. |
| **Force spectroscopy mode** | Measuring the unbinding force of a single receptor–ligand pair (e.g., biotin–streptavidin) at the single-molecule level. |

> *Alternative valid modes accepted:* Non-contact mode, PeakForce tapping, Lateral force mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the principle, construction, and working of a **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)** with a labeled block diagram. Mention its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the principle and working of an **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)**. Compare **contact, non-contact, and tapping modes** in a tabular form, highlighting their suitability for biological samples. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — STM (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
The STM operates on the principle of **quantum mechanical electron tunneling**. When a sharp metallic tip is brought within ~0.3–1.0 nm of a conducting sample surface under an applied bias voltage $V_b$, electrons tunnel through the vacuum gap, producing a measurable **tunneling current $I_t$** that depends exponentially on the gap distance $d$:

$$I_t \propto V_b \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$$

**Construction (2 marks):**

1. **Tip:** Atomically sharp tungsten (W) or platinum–iridium (Pt–Ir) wire, often electrochemically etched to end in a single atom.
2. **Piezoelectric scanner (PZT):** A tubular or tripod PZT ceramic that provides sub-Ångström $x$, $y$, $z$ positioning.
3. **Coarse positioning mechanism:** Inchworm / slip-stick motors bring the tip to within tunneling range.
4. **Vibration isolation:** Eddy-current dampers, bungee suspension, acoustic enclosure.
5. **Feedback electronics:** Transimpedance amplifier + PID controller.
6. **Computer with display:** Synchronizes raster scan with recorded signal.

**Working (2 marks):**
The tip scans the surface in a raster pattern via the $XY$ piezo. In **constant-current mode**, the feedback loop adjusts the $z$-piezo so that $I_t$ remains fixed; the $z$-displacement trace gives the topography. In **constant-height mode**, $z$ is held fixed and $I_t(x, y)$ is recorded.

**Advantages (½ mark):**
- True atomic resolution (~0.1 nm lateral, ~0.01 nm vertical).
- Real-space, direct topographic imaging.
- Can probe electronic density of states (spectroscopic mode).

**Limitations (½ mark):**
- Sample must be **electrically conducting**.
- Usually requires **ultra-high vacuum** and atomically clean surfaces.
- Limited scan range (~100 µm × 100 µm).
- Slow imaging speed compared with SEM.

---

#### Part (b) — AFM (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
AFM measures the **interatomic force** between a sharp tip mounted on a flexible cantilever and the sample. The cantilever deflects according to Hooke's law, $F = k_c \cdot z$, and this deflection is detected optically. The force has its origin in the **Lennard-Jones interatomic potential**, combining short-range Pauli repulsion and longer-range van der Waals attraction.

**Working (2 marks):**
A laser beam is focused on the back of the cantilever and reflected onto a **position-sensitive photodetector (PSD)**. As the tip interacts with the surface, cantilever bending changes the reflected beam position. A feedback loop adjusts the $z$-piezo to keep the deflection (or oscillation amplitude) constant, recording the topographic map.

**Tabular Comparison of Modes (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact Mode | Non-Contact Mode | Tapping Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tip–sample distance | < 0.5 nm (repulsive) | 5–15 nm (attractive) | 0.5–2 nm (intermittent) |
| Cantilever motion | Static, dragged | Oscillates at resonance | Oscillates at resonance, taps |
| Force regime | Repulsive (Pauli) | Attractive (van der Waals) | Mixed |
| Lateral force | **High** (shear) | Negligible | Negligible |
| Resolution | High (lateral) | Moderate | **Highest** (vertical) |
| Sample damage risk | **High** for soft samples | Low | **Low** |
| Suitability for biology | Limited | Moderate | **Excellent** — preferred for cells, proteins, DNA |
| Environment | Air or liquid | UHV preferred | Air or **liquid** |

> *Suitability for biology (½ mark extra):* Tapping mode is the **gold standard** for biological imaging because it eliminates destructive lateral forces while preserving nanometer resolution in physiological buffer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q: (a)** What are **Scanning Probe Microscopes (SPM)**? Classify the major families of SPM with one example each, and explain why SPMs can resolve features below the optical diffraction limit. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss in detail the **biotechnological applications** of AFM, covering (i) imaging of live cells, (ii) single-molecule force spectroscopy, and (iii) measurement of cell mechanics. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Classification of SPM (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Scanning Probe Microscopes are instruments that image a sample surface by raster-scanning a sharp physical probe across it while detecting a **position-dependent physical interaction** (current, force, capacitance, magnetic field, etc.) between the probe and the sample.

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Family | Interaction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Tunneling-current based | Quantum tunneling | **STM** |
| Force-based | Interatomic force | **AFM**, MFM, EFM, KPFM |
| Optical near-field | Evanescent optical field | **SNOM/NSOM** |
| Electrochemical | Ion current / capacitance | **SECM**, **SCM** |

**Why SPM beats the diffraction limit (3 marks):**

The Rayleigh diffraction limit for an optical microscope is:

$$d_{\text{min}} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{\text{NA}} \approx 200 \text{ nm (for visible light)}$$

In SPM, resolution is governed instead by:

1. The **radius of curvature of the tip apex** (typically 2–10 nm, can be atomically sharp).
2. The **decay length of the physical interaction** being probed. For tunneling current, the decay length is ~0.1 nm; for van der Waals force, ~1 nm. These are orders of magnitude smaller than the wavelength of light.

Therefore, SPM circumvents the diffraction limit because it does not use propagating electromagnetic waves — it uses a **localized, evanescent-like physical interaction** that decays rapidly away from the surface.

---

#### Part (b) — Biotechnological Applications of AFM (7 marks)

**(i) Imaging of Live Cells (2 marks):**
AFM in tapping mode can image live bacteria, mammalian cells, and viruses under physiological buffer without the need for vacuum, staining, or fixation. Real-time processes such as cell division, drug-induced morphological changes, and virus–cell fusion events have been visualized.

**(ii) Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy (3 marks):**
By functionalizing the AFM tip with a specific biomolecule (e.g., a ligand or an antibody), one can grab a single receptor on a cell surface and pull. The **force–distance curve** reveals:
- Unbinding force of a single receptor–ligand pair (e.g., biotin–streptavidin ~160 pN).
- Mechanical unfolding of single protein domains (e.g., titin immunoglobulin domains unfolding in steps of ~20–25 pN).
- Elastic properties of single DNA molecules (overstretching transition at ~65 pN).

**(iii) Measurement of Cell Mechanics (2 marks):**
AFM nanoindentation measures the **Young's modulus** of cells via the Hertz model (derived in Section 3.3 of these notes). Cancer cells are typically **softer** (lower $E$) than healthy counterparts, making AFM a diagnostic tool in **mechanobiology**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Conflating STM and AFM:** A frequent error is stating that "AFM uses tunneling current." This is wrong — AFM uses **force**. Only STM uses tunneling. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong formula reference:** Students often write $I_t \propto d$ or $I_t \propto d^2$. The correct relation is **exponential**: $I_t \propto \exp(-d)$. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Ignoring sample conductivity for STM:** STM cannot image insulators. Forgetting to state this loses a half-mark in Part B answers.
> 4. **Forgetting the Lennard-Jones form:** AFM is best introduced via the $U(r)$ interatomic potential. Skipping it is a hallmark of an incomplete answer.
> 5. **Mixing up tapping and non-contact:** Tapping mode **touches** the surface; non-contact does **not**. This single error often costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Hertz model missing Poisson's ratio:** Always state $\nu$ for both tip and sample; many students omit the tip's contribution (or incorrectly assume the sample is rigid).
> 7. **Diagram without labels:** A block diagram without **labeled arrows** is worth zero in a 7-mark question. Always label: Tip → Sample → Signal → Feedback → Piezo.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Scanning Probe Microscopes**

- [x] **STM** = Scanning Tunneling Microscope → uses **quantum tunneling current**.
- [x] **AFM** = Atomic Force Microscope → uses **interatomic force** measured via cantilever deflection.
- [x] **STM** requires a **conducting sample**; **AFM** works on insulators and biological samples.
- [x] The fundamental equation of STM: $I_t \propto V_b \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$, with $k = \sqrt{2m_e}/\hbar$.
- [x] The tip–sample gap in STM is ~**0.3–1.0 nm**; tunneling current is typically **0.1–10 nA**.
- [x] The fundamental physics of AFM: **Lennard-Jones potential** $U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}]$.
- [x] AFM cantilever obeys Hooke's law: $F = k_c z$, with $k_c \approx 0.01\text{–}100$ N/m.
- [x] **Cantilever resonant frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k_c / m^*}$ — typically 100–500 kHz for tapping mode.
- [x] **Three main AFM modes:** Contact (repulsive, hard samples), Non-contact (attractive, vacuum best), Tapping (intermittent, biological samples).
- [x] **Tapping mode** is the **gold standard for biological imaging** because it eliminates destructive shear forces.
- [x] **AFM force spectroscopy** can measure single-molecule unbinding forces (e.g., biotin–streptavidin ~160 pN).
- [x] **Hertz model for nanoindentation:** $F = \dfrac{4}{3}E^* R^{1/2}\delta^{3/2}$ — used to compute Young's modulus of cells.
- [x] **Cancer cells are softer** than normal cells — measurable by AFM ($E \sim$ 1 kPa vs. ~10 kPa).
- [x] **Optical beam deflection** is the most common AFM readout (laser + position-sensitive photodetector).
- [x] **PZT piezoelectric scanners** provide sub-Ångström positioning in $x$, $y$, $z$.
- [x] **SPM resolution** is governed by **tip radius** and **interaction decay length**, not by optical diffraction.
- [x] **Major SPM family members:** STM, AFM, MFM, EFM, SNOM/NSOM, KPFM, SCM, SECM.
- [x] **Inventors:** Binnig & Rohrer (STM, 1981 — Nobel 1986); Binnig, Quate & Gerber (AFM, 1986).
- [x] **Key biological applications:** live-cell imaging, single-molecule unfolding, DNA mechanics, receptor–ligand unbinding, mechanobiology, amyloid fibril imaging, virus imaging, biosensing with functionalized tips.

> - **Course:** PEBBT415 - ANALYTICAL TECHNIQUES IN BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
