<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)** is a non-optical, surface-sensitive analytical technique that produces real-space, atomically resolved topographic images of electrically conductive surfaces by exploiting the **quantum mechanical tunneling effect** between a sharp metallic probe tip and the specimen under an applied bias voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** STM was invented by **Gerd Binnig** and **Heinrich Rohrer** at IBM Zurich Research Laboratory in **1981** (Nobel Prize in Physics, **1986**). It was the first instrument capable of imaging individual atoms on a surface.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **The "Finger-Sense" Analogy for STM:** Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to read the raised letters of a Braille page using a single fingertip. As you glide your finger across the page, you feel the bumps. The "tunneling current" in STM behaves exactly like your fingertip's sense of touch — when the metallic tip is *closer* to a protruding atom (a "bump"), more current flows; when the tip is over a valley, the current drops. The instrument maps these current variations to build an atomic-scale picture of the surface.

### Key Operational Metrics
- **Lateral resolution:** ~**0.1 nm** (atomic scale)
- **Vertical resolution:** ~**0.01 nm** (picometer range)
- **Required tip-sample gap:** **0.3 – 1.0 nm**
- **Typical bias voltage:** **10 mV – 3 V**
- **Typical tunneling current:** **0.1 nA – 10 nA**
- **Sample requirement:** Must be **electrically conductive** (metals, semimetals, or doped semiconductors)

---

## 1.2 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution scanning probe technique that images surface topography by measuring the **interatomic forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, frictional, magnetic) between a sharp nanometer-scale tip mounted on a flexible microcantilever and the specimen surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** AFM was developed in **1986** by **Binnig, Quate, and Gerber** as an evolutionary extension of STM. Its major advantage is the ability to image **non-conductive biological samples** (DNA, proteins, cells, membranes) in their native, hydrated state.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **The "Stylus-on-a-Spring" Analogy for AFM:** Picture a tiny record-player needle (the tip) attached to a flimsy metal leaf-spring (the cantilever). As the needle tracks the grooves of a vinyl record, the spring flexes up, down, and sideways. A laser beam reflecting off the back of the spring tells the instrument exactly how much it has flexed, allowing reconstruction of the surface. The "flexing" in AFM is caused by invisible atomic forces — the same forces that keep your shoelace knots tied together.

### Key Operational Metrics
- **Lateral resolution:** ~**1 – 5 nm** (sub-nm under ideal conditions)
- **Vertical resolution:** ~**0.01 nm** (Å scale)
- **Cantilever spring constant range:** **0.01 – 50 N/m**
- **Cantilever tip radius:** **2 – 30 nm**
- **Sample requirement:** Works on **conductors AND insulators** (key advantage over STM)
- **Operating environment:** Ambient air, controlled humidity, **liquid immersion**, vacuum, or cryogenic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tested Distinction:** STM requires a *conducting sample* because it measures a quantum current; AFM does not, because it measures *mechanical force*. This is the single most important point examiners use to discriminate between the two techniques in viva-voce and short-answer questions.

---

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Comparison (Examination Ready):**

| Feature | STM | AFM |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal measured** | Tunneling current (nA) | Cantilever deflection (nm) |
| **Sample conductivity** | Required (conductor) | Not required (universal) |
| **Resolution** | Atomic (~0.1 nm lateral) | Nanometer (~1–5 nm lateral) |
| **Working medium** | Usually vacuum or inert gas | Air, liquid, vacuum, cryo |
| **Force/current source** | Quantum tunneling | van der Waals / electrostatic |
| **Tip material** | W or Pt-Ir (etched wire) | Si or Si₃N₄ (etched microfabricated) |
| **Invention year** | 1981 | 1986 |
| **Biological imaging** | Limited (needs conductive coating) | Excellent (native state) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Quantum Tunneling — The Physical Basis of STM

According to classical electrostatics, an electron cannot cross a potential energy barrier higher than its own kinetic energy. However, **quantum mechanics** permits the electron wave-function to extend through (and beyond) a thin barrier with a non-zero probability amplitude. This phenomenon is called **quantum tunneling**.

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle of STM
1. **Sharp metallic tip** (typically W or Pt-Ir, atomically sharp) is brought within **~1 nm** of a conductive sample.
2. A **bias voltage V** is applied between tip and sample.
3. The vacuum gap (~1 nm) acts as a **potential barrier** of height ≈ work function **φ** (typically **4–5 eV** for most metals).
4. Electrons **tunnel** through this barrier from tip to sample (or vice versa, depending on bias polarity).
5. The resulting **tunneling current I_t** is measured.
6. The tip is raster-scanned across the surface in x–y using piezoelectric scanners.
7. Two operating modes exist:
   - **Constant-Height Mode:** Tip is held at fixed z; current is recorded as a function of (x,y). Fast, but risk of tip crash.
   - **Constant-Current Mode:** A feedback loop adjusts the tip's z-height to maintain a constant I_t; the z-displacement is mapped as topography. Safer and more common.

### The "Why" Behind Exponential Distance Dependence
The tunneling probability depends exponentially on the barrier width (the tip–sample gap), making STM exquisitely sensitive to atomic-scale height variations — a **0.1 nm** change in gap distance alters the current by roughly an **order of magnitude**.

---

## 2.2 Interatomic Forces — The Physical Basis of AFM

The total force between tip and sample is the **gradient of the tip–sample interaction potential**:

$$F(z) = -\frac{dU(z)}{dz}$$

The dominant force components are:

- **Short-range repulsive force** (Pauli exclusion, < 0.5 nm tip-sample): Acts when the tip "pushes" into the electron cloud of surface atoms.
- **Long-range attractive van der Waals force** (0.5 – 10 nm): The dominant force in non-contact mode; arises from correlated dipoles.
- **Capillary force** (ambient conditions): Forms a water meniscus between tip and sample; can dominate total force in air.
- **Electrostatic force** (charged samples): F_es = ½ (dC/dz) V².
- **Magnetic / chemical / friction forces** (specialized modes): Magnetic Force Microscopy (MFM), Lateral Force Microscopy (LFM), etc.

### The Lennard-Jones Potential — A Cornerstone Model

The simplest analytic model for tip–sample interaction is the **6–12 Lennard-Jones potential**:

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

Where:
- **r** = instantaneous tip–atom separation (m)
- **ε** = depth of the potential energy well (J)
- **σ** = distance at which the potential is zero (m)

The **6th-power attractive term** models induced-dipole van der Waals dispersion, and the **12th-power repulsive term** models short-range Pauli repulsion. The equilibrium separation r₀ occurs where F = –dU/dr = 0:

$$r_0 = 2^{1/6} \, \sigma \approx 1.122 \, \sigma$$

---

## 2.3 Cantilever Mechanics — Deflection Detection

The AFM cantilever is modeled as a Hookean spring with spring constant **k** (units: N/m). Vertical deflection **z** of the tip is related to force by:

$$F = -k \cdot z$$

For a rectangular cantilever of length L, width w, thickness t, and Young's modulus E, the spring constant is:

$$k = \frac{E \, w \, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$

This cubic dependence on **t** is why cantilever thickness is the most critical fabrication parameter.

### Optical Beam Deflection (OBD) Readout
- A **laser** strikes the back of the cantilever and reflects onto a **position-sensitive photodetector (PSPD)** divided into four quadrants.
- Cantilever flexing shifts the laser spot; the differential signal (Top–Bottom) gives **topography**, and (Left–Right) gives **friction / lateral force**.

> [!NOTE]
> The OBD method, developed by **Meyer and Amer (1988)**, replaced the original STM-based cantilever readout and made modern AFM practical.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Examination)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Mastery Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE / Internal Evaluation:**

| # | Equation | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_t = I_0 \, e^{-2 \kappa d}$ | $I_t$ = tunneling current (A); $d$ = tip–sample gap (m) | Exponential distance dependence of STM current |
| 2 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 m \varphi}}{\hbar}$ | $m$ = electron mass (kg); $\varphi$ = work function (eV); $\hbar$ = reduced Planck constant (J·s) | Decay constant inside the barrier (m⁻¹) |
| 3 | $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34} \ \mathrm{J \cdot s}$ | — | Reduced Planck constant (numerical) |
| 4 | $\Delta I_t / I_t \approx -2 \kappa \cdot \Delta d$ | — | Sensitivity: ~one order per 0.1 nm |
| 5 | $U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ (\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6} \right]$ | $r$ (m); $\varepsilon$ (J); $\sigma$ (m) | Lennard-Jones 6–12 potential |
| 6 | $r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \, \sigma$ | — | Equilibrium tip–atom separation |
| 7 | $F(z) = -dU(z)/dz$ | $F$ (N); $U$ (J); $z$ (m) | Force from potential energy gradient |
| 8 | $F = -k \cdot z$ | $k$ (N/m); $z$ (m) | Hooke's law for cantilever |

| 10 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$ | $f_0$ (Hz); $m^{*}$ = effective mass (kg) | Cantilever resonant frequency (tapping mode) |
| 11 | $Q = f_0 / \Delta f$ | $Q$ = quality factor (dimensionless) | Cantilever sharpness of resonance |
| 12 | $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_0}{2 k} \cdot \dfrac{dF}{dz}$ | — | Frequency shift ↔ force gradient (NC-AFM) |

### Real-World Engineering / Biotechnology Utility
- **STM:** First ever visualization of the Si(111)-7×7 reconstruction (1983); manipulation of individual xenon atoms on nickel (1990); atomic-scale electronics research.
- **AFM:** Imaging **DNA double helices**, **protein unfolding mechanics (force spectroscopy)**, **live cell membranes**, **virus morphology (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins)**, **single-molecule ligand–receptor binding**, and **biomaterial surface roughness** for implant design.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Formulas 1 & 2:** "**Tunneling current decays exponentially with distance; the decay constant κ scales with the square root of the work function**." For exam derivations, always begin from the 1-D rectangular barrier Schrōdinger solution.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tunneling Current in STM (Bardeen–Tersoff–Hamann Framework)

### Starting Point: 1-D Time-Independent Schrōdinger Equation
Consider a potential barrier $V(z)$ separating tip and sample. For a single electron of energy E in a region of width d (the gap), the stationary Schrōdinger equation is:

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m} \frac{d^{2}\psi(z)}{dz^{2}} + V(z) \psi(z) = E \, \psi(z)$$

### Step 1 — Define the Barrier Geometry
In STM, the vacuum gap of width d acts as a barrier of height equal to the work function **φ** (relative to the Fermi level). For an electron of energy **E ≈ E_F** moving toward the barrier:

$$V(z) - E \approx \varphi \quad \text{inside the gap}$$

### Step 2 — Solve Inside the Barrier
Inside the gap, the wave-function decays as:

$$\psi(z) = A \, e^{-\kappa z} + B \, e^{+\kappa z}$$

For a thick barrier, the growing exponential is unphysical, leaving:

$$\psi(z) \approx A \, e^{-\kappa z}$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Decay Constant κ
Substituting into Schrōdinger's equation:

$$\kappa^{2} = \frac{2m(V - E)}{\hbar^{2}} = \frac{2m \varphi}{\hbar^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m \varphi}}{\hbar}}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Tunneling Probability
The transmission probability T through a rectangular barrier is:

$$T \propto \vert \psi(d) \vert^{2} / \vert \psi(0) \vert^{2} = e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

### Step 5 — Relate to Tunneling Current
Applying the **Bardeen transfer Hamiltonian formalism** to first order, the tunneling current between two electrodes at bias V is:

$$I_t \propto \int_{E_F}^{E_F + eV} \rho_t(E) \, \rho_s(E - eV) \, T(E, V, d) \, dE$$

For small V and slowly varying densities of states $\rho_t$ and $\rho_s$, this reduces to:

$$\boxed{I_t \approx I_0 \, e^{-2 \kappa d}}$$

### Step 6 — Sensitivity Argument
Differentiating logarithmically:

$$\frac{\Delta I_t}{I_t} = -2 \kappa \cdot \Delta d$$

For a typical metal, **φ ≈ 4.5 eV**, so:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa \approx 1.09 \times 10^{10} \ \mathrm{m^{-1}} = 1.09 \ \mathrm{\AA^{-1}}$$

Hence, for $\Delta d = 0.1 \ \mathrm{nm} = 1 \ \mathrm{\AA}$:

$$\frac{\Delta I_t}{I_t} = -2 \times 1.09 \times 1 = -2.18$$

$$I_t \text{ changes by a factor of } e^{2.18} \approx 8.8$$

This justifies the **atomic-scale vertical sensitivity** of STM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cantilever Spring Constant from Geometry

### Starting Point: Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory
For a beam of length L, fixed at one end (clamped cantilever) and loaded at the free end by a force F, the static tip deflection $\delta$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

Where **I** is the area moment of inertia of the cross-section. For a rectangular beam of width **w** and thickness **t**:

$$I = \frac{w t^{3}}{12}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute I

$$\delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E \cdot (w t^{3}/12)} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law F = kδ

$$F = k \cdot \delta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{E \, w \, t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}}$$

### Step 3 — Illustrative Numerical Example
**Given:** Si cantilever with E = 170 GPa, L = 200 µm, w = 30 µm, t = 2 µm.

$$k = \frac{(170 \times 10^{9}) (30 \times 10^{-6}) (2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}$$

$$k = \frac{(1.7 \times 10^{11})(3 \times 10^{-5})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{4 (8 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$k = \frac{4.08 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 1.27 \ \mathrm{N/m}$$

This is consistent with a **soft contact-mode cantilever** (typical range 0.01 – 1 N/m).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever (Tapping Mode)

The fundamental resonant frequency of a clamped-free beam (cantilever) is:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \beta_1^{2} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}$$

Where:
- $\beta_1 = 1.875$ (first eigenvalue for clamped-free beam)
- $\rho$ = density of cantilever material (kg/m³)
- $A = w \cdot t$ = cross-sectional area (m²)

For a rectangular cross-section $A = w t$ and $I = w t^{3}/12$:

$$f_0 = \frac{\beta_1^{2}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 0.56 \, \frac{t}{L^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}}$$

### Numerical Example
For a Si cantilever: E = 170 GPa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³, L = 200 µm, t = 2 µm.

$$f_0 \approx 0.56 \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{11}}{2330}}$$

$$f_0 \approx 0.56 \times 50 \times \sqrt{7.30 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$f_0 \approx 28 \times 8545 \approx 239 \ \mathrm{kHz}$$

This matches typical tapping-mode cantilever resonance frequencies of **~200 – 400 kHz** in commercial AFMs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Imaging Modes of AFM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Primary Imaging Modes of AFM (Frequent KTU Question):**

| Mode | Tip–Sample Gap | Dominant Force | Key Application | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact Mode** | < 0.5 nm (repulsive) | Pauli repulsion | High-resolution hard samples, friction imaging | Fast, high lateral resolution | Damages soft samples (biological) |
| **Non-Contact Mode** | 1 – 10 nm (attractive) | van der Waals | Atomic resolution on hard surfaces | No tip/sample damage | Low contrast in ambient (capillary forces) |
| **Tapping Mode** (Intermittent) | 0.5 – 2 nm (intermittent contact) | Combination | Imaging of soft biological samples | Eliminates lateral forces, good for DNA/proteins | Slower than contact; tip wear |
| **Force Modulation** | Variable | Stiffness-dependent | Mapping local mechanical properties | Stiffness mapping | Requires calibration |
| **Phase Imaging** | Tapping mode | Phase lag ↔ adhesion | Material contrast on heterogeneous surfaces | Distinguishes phases chemically | Qualitative without reference |

---

## 3.5 Force–Distance Curve Interpretation

A force–distance (F–d) curve is the **fingerprint of tip–sample interaction**, recorded by ramping the piezo z-piezo while monitoring cantilever deflection. Key regions:

1. **Region A — Approach (no contact):** Tip far away; F ≈ 0. As tip approaches, long-range attractive forces (van der Waals) begin to bend the cantilever downward.
2. **Region B — Jump-to-contact:** When dF/dz > k, the tip suddenly snaps into contact with the surface (typical in ambient humidity due to capillary condensation).
3. **Region C — Contact (repulsive):** Slope of F vs. d reflects sample stiffness. The indentation depth yields Young's modulus via the **Hertz model**.
4. **Region D — Retract (adhesion):** Adhesive and capillary forces pull the tip down further; the minimum force gives the **adhesion force**.
5. **Region E — Pull-off:** The tip suddenly releases. The force at this point is the **pull-off force** — used in single-molecule force spectroscopy to unfold proteins or rupture biotin–streptavidin bonds.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** F–d curves are the cornerstone of **AFM-based force spectroscopy** (a frequent 14-mark KTU question). Always label points A through E and explicitly mention that the **slope in region C** gives sample stiffness, and the **pull-off force in region E** gives adhesion.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — STM Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ctrlA [CONTROL UNIT]
        fbCtrlA[Feedback Controller]
        piezoDrvA[High-Voltage Piezo Driver]
        biasSrcA[Bias Voltage Source Vb]
    end
    subgraph scanA [SCAN HEAD ASSEMBLY]
        tipA[Atomically Sharp Tip W or Pt-Ir]
        piezoXYZ[XYZ Pieelectric Scanner Tube]
        coarseA[Coarse Positioner Inertial Drive]
    end
    subgraph sigA [SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
        preampA[Picoampere Preamplifier I-V Convert]
        psdA[Current-to-Voltage Converter]
        adcA[Analog-to-Digital Converter]
    end
    subgraph procA [DATA ACQUISITION]
        pcA[Computer and Topography Map Renderer]
        dispA[3D Surface Plot and Z Data]
    end
    sampA[Conductive Sample Surface]
    biasSrcA -->|applies Vb between| tipA
    biasSrcA -->|also| sampA
    piezoDrvA -->|x y z signals| piezoXYZ
    piezoXYZ -->|raster scans| tipA
    coarseA -->|coarse approach| tipA
    tipA -->|tunneling current It| preampA
    preampA --> psdA
    psdA --> adcA
    adcA --> pcA
    fbCtrlA -->|error signal It minus Iset| piezoDrvA
    pcA -->|setpoint ref| fbCtrlA
    pcA --> dispA
    sampA -.tip-sample gap of 0.3 to 1.0 nm.- tipA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **feedback loop** is the heart of constant-current STM: any deviation of measured $I_t$ from the user-defined **setpoint** $I_{set}$ generates a correction voltage to the z-piezo, which raises or lowers the tip to restore the current — hence the "topography" is the time-integrated z-piezo voltage.

---

## 4.3 Energy vs. Distance Diagram — Lennard-Jones Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LJaxis [X AXIS - tip-sample separation r]
        r0A[region of strong repulsion r less than 1 Angstrom]
        r0B[equilibrium r0 equal to 1.122 sigma]
        r0C[van der Waals tail r greater than 5 Angstrom]
    end
    subgraph LJcurve [POTENTIAL ENERGY U r]
        repCurve[Repulsive wall U goes to positive infinity as r decreases]
        wellCurve[Potential well of depth epsilon at r0]
        tailCurve[Long range attractive tail U goes to zero as r increases]
    end
    r0A --> repCurve
    r0B --> wellCurve
    r0C --> tailCurve
```

> [!TIP]
> The **minimum of the L-J well** corresponds to the equilibrium contact position. The AFM operates primarily on the **attractive (right) side** in non-contact mode, and on the **repulsive (left) side** in contact mode — the cantilever is "climbing" different walls of the same potential.

        ptA4[Point 4 - Repulsive contact - Force positive and linear]
    end
    subgraph fcB [RETRACT CURVE - moving away from surface]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) with respect to (i) the physical signal measured, (ii) sample requirement, and (iii) typical application.**

#### Model Answer
**(i) Physical signal measured:**
- **STM** measures a **quantum tunneling current** ($I_t$, in nA) flowing between a biased metallic tip and a conductive sample.
- **AFM** measures the **mechanical deflection** of a microfabricated cantilever (in nm) caused by interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, repulsive) between tip and sample.

**(ii) Sample requirement:**
- **STM** strictly requires an **electrically conductive sample** (metals, doped semiconductors, or thin conductive coatings).
- **AFM** works on **both conductive and non-conductive samples**, including biological specimens in ambient or liquid environments.

**(iii) Typical application:**
- **STM** is used for atomic-resolution imaging of crystalline surfaces (e.g., Si(111)-7×7) and atomic manipulation.
- **AFM** is used for imaging biomolecules (DNA, proteins, viruses), cells, and measuring nanomechanical properties (force spectroscopy).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the principle of operation of a Scanning Tunneling Microscope. Why is the tunneling current exponentially sensitive to tip–sample separation?**

#### Model Answer
**Principle of Operation:** STM operates on the principle of **quantum mechanical tunneling**. A bias voltage (typically 10 mV – 3 V) is applied between an atomically sharp metallic tip (tungsten or Pt-Ir) and a conductive sample. When the tip is brought within 0.3 – 1.0 nm of the surface, electrons tunnel through the vacuum gap acting as a potential barrier, producing a measurable **tunneling current** ($I_t$, typically 0.1 – 10 nA). The tip is raster-scanned across the surface using a piezoelectric scanner. In the **constant-current mode**, a feedback loop adjusts the tip's z-height to maintain $I_t$ constant, and the z-piezo voltage is recorded as a function of (x, y) to produce a topographic map.

**Why current is exponentially sensitive to distance:** From quantum mechanics, the transmission probability through a rectangular barrier of width d is $T \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$, where $\kappa = \sqrt{2m\varphi}/\hbar$. The tunneling current $I_t \approx I_0 e^{-2\kappa d}$ therefore decays **exponentially** with the tip–sample gap. For a typical metal work function φ ≈ 4.5 eV, κ ≈ 1.09 Å⁻¹, so a change in gap of just 1 Å alters the current by a factor of ~9. This extraordinary sensitivity gives STM its **sub-Å vertical resolution** and is the fundamental reason for its atomic-scale imaging capability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working principle of an **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)**. Explain in detail the three primary imaging modes: contact, non-contact, and tapping mode.

**Q. (b)** Derive the expression for the **tunneling current** in STM starting from the time-independent Schrōdinger equation. Calculate the **decay constant κ** for a metal with work function φ = 4.5 eV and estimate the **factor by which the current changes** when the tip–sample gap varies by ±0.1 nm from a reference gap of 0.5 nm.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
The key components of an AFM are:
1. **Microcantilever with sharp tip** — typically Si or Si₃N₄, tip radius 2–30 nm, spring constant 0.01–50 N/m.
2. **Piezoelectric XYZ scanner** — controls tip position with sub-Å precision.
3. **Laser source and mirror** — directs a focused laser onto the back of the cantilever.
4. **Position-Sensitive Photodetector (PSPD)** — four-quadrant detector that records the reflected beam's position.
5. **Coarse positioner** — stepper motor for initial sample–tip engagement.
6. **Feedback controller** — adjusts z-piezo to maintain setpoint (force, amplitude, or frequency).
7. **Data acquisition system** — converts PSPD signal into topography map.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The tip is brought near the surface where interatomic forces act on it. These forces deflect the cantilever, deflecting the reflected laser beam on the PSPD. The differential photodetector signal is fed to the feedback loop, which adjusts the tip height to maintain a constant force setpoint. The z-piezo voltage at each (x, y) point is recorded as topography.

**Three Imaging Modes (2 marks):**

| Mode | Operation | Dominant Force | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Tip dragged across surface, constant deflection | Repulsive (Pauli) | Hard samples, atomic resolution, friction |
| **Non-Contact** | Tip oscillates above surface (~5 nm), shifts in resonant frequency monitored | Attractive (van der Waals) | Atomic resolution on clean, hard surfaces in UHV |
| **Tapping** | Tip oscillates near resonance; intermittent contact; amplitude damping monitored | Combination; intermittent | Soft biological samples (DNA, cells, proteins) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The 1-D Schrōdinger equation for an electron of energy E in a region of constant potential $V(z)$ is:

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m} \frac{d^{2}\psi}{dz^{2}} + V(z) \psi = E \psi$$

Inside the tip–sample gap of width d, the potential barrier is $V - E \approx \varphi$. Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^{2}\psi}{dz^{2}} = \frac{2m(V - E)}{\hbar^{2}} \psi = \kappa^{2} \psi$$

with

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m \varphi}}{\hbar}$$

The general solution inside the barrier is $\psi(z) = A e^{-\kappa z} + B e^{+\kappa z}$. For an extended barrier, the growing term is unphysical, leaving:

$$\psi(z) \approx A e^{-\kappa z}$$

The transmission probability is:

$$T \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$$

Applying Bardeen's first-order perturbation approach and assuming slowly varying density of states, the tunneling current is:

$$\boxed{I_t \approx I_0 \, e^{-2\kappa d}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given φ = 4.5 eV = 4.5 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J = 7.2 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, m = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, ℏ = 1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 7.2 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{1.312 \times 10^{-48}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} = \frac{1.145 \times 10^{-24}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} = 1.086 \times 10^{10} \ \mathrm{m^{-1}}$$

For $\Delta d = \pm 0.1 \ \mathrm{nm} = \pm 1 \times 10^{-10} \ \mathrm{m}$:

$$2 \kappa \Delta d = 2 \times 1.086 \times 10^{10} \times 1 \times 10^{-10} = 2.172$$

$$\frac{I_t(d + \Delta d)}{I_t(d)} = e^{-2 \kappa \Delta d} = e^{\mp 2.172}$$

- For Δd = +0.1 nm (gap increases): $I_t$ decreases by factor $e^{2.172} \approx$ **8.77**
- For Δd = –0.1 nm (gap decreases): $I_t$ increases by factor $e^{2.172} \approx$ **8.77**

**Result:** A mere 0.1 nm (one atomic diameter) gap change alters the tunneling current by nearly an **order of magnitude** — the physical origin of STM's atomic-scale sensitivity.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### `[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **Lennard-Jones 6–12 potential** as a model for tip–sample interaction in AFM. Derive the expression for the **equilibrium separation** $r_0$ and the **maximum attractive force**.

**Q. (b)** With a schematic diagram, describe the **optical beam deflection (OBD) method** used for cantilever deflection detection in AFM. Derive the expression for the **spring constant of a rectangular cantilever** using Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. A silicon cantilever has dimensions L = 250 µm, w = 35 µm, t = 2 µm. Given E = 170 GPa, calculate the spring constant.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Lennard-Jones Potential Discussion (3 marks):**
The **6-12 Lennard-Jones potential** is the most widely used analytic model for the tip–atom interaction in AFM:

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

- The $(\sigma/r)^{12}$ term models **short-range Pauli repulsion** (electron cloud overlap).
- The $(\sigma/r)^{6}$ term models **long-range van der Waals attraction** (induced-dipole/induced-dipole).
- The potential crosses zero at $r = \sigma$ and reaches a minimum (the well depth) of $-\varepsilon$ at $r = r_0$.

**Equilibrium Separation (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, force F = -dU/dr = 0:

$$\frac{dU}{dr} = 4\varepsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right] = 0$$

$$6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} = 12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r^{6} = 2 \sigma^{6}$$

$$\boxed{r_0 = 2^{1/6} \, \sigma \approx 1.122 \, \sigma}$$

**Maximum Attractive Force (2 marks):**
The force is $F = -dU/dr$:

$$F(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \frac{12 \sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - \frac{6 \sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right] = \frac{24 \varepsilon}{r} \left[ 2 \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

To find the **maximum attractive force**, differentiate F with respect to r and set dF/dr = 0. After algebraic simplification, the extremum occurs at:

$$r = r_{\text{max}} = (26/7)^{1/6} \, \sigma \approx 1.244 \, \sigma$$

Substituting back:

$$F_{\text{max, attractive}} = -\frac{12 \varepsilon}{r_{\text{max}}} \cdot \frac{7}{26} \approx -\frac{1.926 \, \varepsilon}{\sigma}$$

This is the **maximum (most negative) attractive force** experienced by the tip — useful in force-curve interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Optical Beam Deflection Method (3 marks):**
The **Optical Beam Deflection (OBD)** system is the most widely used cantilever readout in modern AFM. It consists of:
1. A **laser diode** (typically 650 nm or 785 nm) producing a focused beam.
2. The beam is reflected off the **backside of the cantilever** (often coated with a thin gold layer for high reflectivity).
3. The reflected beam is directed onto a **Position-Sensitive Photodetector (PSPD)** divided into four quadrants (A, B, C, D).
4. The differential signals are:
   - **Normal force / topography:** $(A + B) - (C + D)$ (vertical deflection)
   - **Lateral / friction force:** $(A + C) - (B + D)$ (torsional twist)

When the cantilever bends due to tip–sample force, the reflected laser spot shifts on the PSPD, producing a measurable voltage change. A typical OBD system achieves **sub-Å displacement sensitivity**.

**Derivation of Spring Constant (2 marks):**
For a **clamped-free rectangular cantilever** of length L, width w, thickness t, and Young's modulus E, the static tip deflection under an end-loaded force F is given by Euler–Bernoulli beam theory:

$$\delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

For a rectangular cross-section, the area moment of inertia is:

$$I = \frac{w t^{3}}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$\delta = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}$$

Using Hooke's law $F = k \delta$:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
Given L = 250 µm = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ m, w = 35 µm = 3.5 × 10⁻⁵ m, t = 2 µm = 2 × 10⁻⁶ m, E = 170 GPa = 1.7 × 10¹¹ Pa:

$$k = \frac{(1.7 \times 10^{11})(3.5 \times 10^{-5})(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4 (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^{3}}$$

Numerator: $1.7 \times 10^{11} \times 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 4.76 \times 10^{-11}$
Denominator: $4 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-11} = 6.25 \times 10^{-11}$

$$k \approx \frac{4.76 \times 10^{-11}}{6.25 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 0.762 \ \mathrm{N/m}$$

This is a **soft cantilever** — well suited for **tapping-mode or non-contact imaging of biological samples** (typical range 0.01 – 1 N/m).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Wrong units in spring-constant derivation** — students frequently forget to convert µm to m, producing k in absurdly large units. Always convert to SI *first*.
> 2. **Confusing cantilever length L with thickness t** — the spring constant depends on $t^{3}/L^{3}$. A small error in t propagates as a cube.
> 3. **Sign convention for force** — remember the attractive force is **negative**; the maximum attractive force is the most negative value.
> 4. **For STM derivations** — students often forget the **factor of 2** in $e^{-2\kappa d}$; both the wave-function amplitude *and* the probability density decay.
> 5. **For Lennard-Jones derivation** — never state $r_0 = \sigma$; it is $r_0 = 2^{1/6}\sigma$. Examiners explicitly test this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bullet-List for AFM & STM:**

### STM (Scanning Tunneling Microscopy)
- Invented by **Binnig & Rohrer, 1981** (Nobel Prize 1986).
- Principle: **Quantum mechanical tunneling** of electrons through a vacuum gap.
- Sample must be **electrically conductive**.
- **Lateral resolution** ~0.1 nm; **vertical resolution** ~0.01 nm.
- **Tunneling current:** $I_t = I_0 \, e^{-2\kappa d}$, with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m\varphi}/\hbar$.
- Typical tip materials: **Tungsten (W)** or **Platinum-Iridium (Pt-Ir)**; tip prepared by **electrochemical etching**.
- Two operating modes: **Constant-Height** and **Constant-Current** (the latter is standard).
- Bias voltage: 10 mV – 3 V; tunneling current: 0.1 nA – 10 nA.
- Cannot image biological samples in their native, non-conductive state.

### AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy)
- Invented by **Binnig, Quate & Gerber, 1986**.
- Principle: Measurement of **interatomic forces** (van der Waals, repulsive, capillary, electrostatic) via cantilever deflection.
- Works on **conductors AND insulators**, including biological samples.
- Cantilever: microfabricated Si or Si₃N₄, tip radius 2–30 nm.
- Spring constant: $k = E w t^{3}/(4 L^{3})$ (cubic dependence on thickness).
- Resonant frequency: $f_0 \approx 0.56 (t/L^{2}) \sqrt{E/\rho}$ — typical values 50 – 500 kHz.
- **Cantilever spring constants** range 0.01 N/m (soft, for biology) to 50 N/m (stiff, for hard samples).
- **Optical Beam Deflection** (Meyer & Amer, 1988) is the standard readout technique using a four-quadrant PSPD.
- **Imaging modes:**
  - **Contact mode** — repulsive regime, hard samples.
  - **Non-contact mode** — attractive regime, atomic resolution, often UHV.
  - **Tapping mode** — intermittent contact, **best for soft biological samples**.
- **Force–distance curves** give access to adhesion force, sample stiffness, and Young's modulus (Hertz model).
- **Force spectroscopy** can unfold individual proteins and rupture single receptor–ligand bonds.

### Key Comparison Equations (must memorize)
- $I_t = I_0 e^{-2\kappa d}$ (STM current–distance)
- $\kappa = \sqrt{2m\varphi}/\hbar$ (decay constant)
- $U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ (\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6} \right]$ (Lennard-Jones potential)
- $r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma$ (equilibrium L-J separation)
- $F = -k \cdot z$ (Hooke's law for cantilever)
- $k = E w t^{3}/(4 L^{3})$ (rectangular cantilever spring constant)
- $f_0 = (1/2\pi) \sqrt{k/m^{*}}$ (resonant frequency)

### Frequently Asked Conceptual Points
1. **Why is STM exponentially sensitive to gap?** Because of the exponential decay of the wave-function in a classically forbidden region.
2. **Why must the STM tip be atomically sharp?** To localize the tunneling to a single atom, achieving atomic lateral resolution.
3. **Why does AFM need a feedback loop?** To maintain a constant tip–sample interaction (force, amplitude, or frequency) and prevent tip crash.
4. **Why is tapping mode preferred for biological samples?** It eliminates destructive lateral (friction/shear) forces present in contact mode, while still providing high resolution.
5. **What is a force–distance curve?** A plot of cantilever deflection (or force) vs. piezo z-displacement; used to extract adhesion, stiffness, and rupture forces.
6. **What is the principal advantage of AFM over STM?** AFM does not require conductive samples and can image biological molecules in their native hydrated state.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's-Level Coverage Map
- **Remember/Understand (CO1):** Define STM, AFM, tunneling current, cantilever, Lennard-Jones potential.
- **Apply (CO2):** Draw schematics, identify components, list applications.
- **Analyze (CO3):** Compare STM vs AFM, explain exponential sensitivity, interpret force–distance curves.
- **Evaluate (CO4):** Select appropriate mode for a given biological sample, choose cantilever spring constant.
- **Create (CO5):** Derive tunneling current expression, derive spring constant formula, solve numerical problems.
